syllabus_desc: State the International System (SI) and ICAO units used to express: Distance
concept: The SI base unit for distance (length) is the metre (m). In aviation, horizontal distances are measured in nautical miles (nm). One nautical mile equals 1,852 metres and corresponds to one minute of latitude. Short distances such as landing zone dimensions and visibility are expressed in metres. Altitude and height are expressed in feet (ft).
exam_tip: SI unit = metre. Aviation horizontal distance = nautical miles. Landing zone dimensions and visibility = metres. Altitude = feet. Don't confuse nautical miles with land miles. 1 nm = 1,852 m.
key_facts: SI base unit of distance: the metre (m)
ICAO horizontal distance: nautical miles (nm)
1 nm = 1,852 m = 1 minute of latitude
Landing zone dimensions and visibility: metres
Altitude and height: feet (ft)
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1 (Section 2: Distance and Speed), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.2.2(a)

syllabus_desc: State the International System (SI) and ICAO units used to express: Time
concept: The SI base unit for time is the second (s). All other time units (minutes, hours) are derived from the second. In aviation operations, ICAO permits the use of hours and minutes for expressing time, using Coordinated Universal Time (UTC), also known as Zulu time. UTC is the time at the Prime Meridian (0° longitude) and does not change for daylight saving. All aviation communications, flight plans, NOTAMs, and ATC operations use UTC.
exam_tip: Don't confuse SI units with ICAO operational units. The SI base unit for time is the second  -  but in day-to-day aviation, time is expressed in hours and minutes UTC. Exam questions may test whether you know the difference.
key_facts: SI base unit of time: the second (s)
ICAO operational unit: hours and minutes in UTC
UTC = Zulu time = time at the Prime Meridian
UTC does not change for daylight saving
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1 (Section 3: Time), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.2.2(b)

syllabus_desc: State the International System (SI) and ICAO units used to express: Velocity
concept: The SI unit for velocity is metres per second (m/s). However, in aviation, speed is expressed in knots (kt). One knot is one nautical mile per hour (1 nm/h), which equals 1.852 km/h. All aircraft speeds  -  airspeed indicator readings, cruise speeds, approach speeds  -  are expressed in knots. This standard ensures precision in flight planning, navigation calculations, and air traffic control coordination.
exam_tip: The SI unit is m/s, but the aviation (ICAO) unit is the knot. A question asking for the SI unit wants m/s  -  not km/h, not knots. A question asking for the ICAO or aviation unit wants knots.
key_facts: SI unit of velocity: metres per second (m/s)
ICAO/aviation unit of speed: knots (kt)
1 knot = 1 nautical mile per hour = 1.852 km/h
All aircraft speeds are expressed in knots
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1 (Section 2.2: Speed), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.2.2(c)

syllabus_desc: State the International System (SI) and ICAO units used to express: Mass
concept: The SI base unit for mass is the kilogram (kg). In aviation, aircraft weight, payload, fuel load, and baggage are all expressed in kilograms. Fuel may also be measured in litres (volume) or pounds (lb) in some contexts, but the standard unit for weight and balance calculations in NZ/AU is the kilogram.
exam_tip: Mass is measured in kilograms. Don't confuse mass (kg) with weight (a force, measured in newtons). In everyday aviation, 'weight' is often used loosely to mean mass in kg.
key_facts: SI base unit of mass: the kilogram (kg)
Aircraft weight, fuel, payload all expressed in kg
Fuel may also be expressed in litres (volume)
Weight and balance calculations use kg in NZ/AU
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1 (Section on units), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.2.2(d)

syllabus_desc: State the International System (SI) and ICAO units used to express: Volume
concept: The SI unit for volume is the cubic metre (m³). In aviation, fuel volume is measured in litres (L). Fuel flow rate is expressed in litres per hour (L/h). The litre is a derived metric unit equal to 0.001 m³. Fuel quantity may be expressed in either litres (volume) or kilograms (mass), but volume is the standard for fuel gauges and refuelling.
exam_tip: Volume = litres for fuel. Fuel flow = litres per hour. Remember that fuel volume changes with temperature (expands when hot), so mass (kg) is more accurate for flight planning.
key_facts: SI unit of volume: cubic metre (m³)
Aviation fuel volume: litres (L)
Fuel flow rate: litres per hour (L/h)
1 litre = 0.001 m³
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1 (Section on units), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.2.2(e)

syllabus_desc: State the International System (SI) and ICAO units used to express: Temperature
concept: The SI unit for temperature is the kelvin (K). In aviation, temperature is expressed in degrees Celsius (°C). The ISA standard sea-level temperature is 15°C. Temperature decreases with altitude at approximately 2°C per 1,000 ft in the troposphere. Outside air temperature (OAT) is used for performance calculations and density altitude determination.
exam_tip: SI unit = kelvin, but aviation uses degrees Celsius. ISA sea level = 15°C. The lapse rate is approximately 2°C per 1,000 ft.
key_facts: SI unit of temperature: kelvin (K)
ICAO/aviation unit: degrees Celsius (°C)
ISA sea-level temperature: 15°C
Temperature lapse rate: ~2°C per 1,000 ft
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1, Module 1.3 (ISA), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.2.2(f)

syllabus_desc: State the International System (SI) and ICAO units used to express: Altitude
concept: Altitude is expressed in feet (ft) in aviation. Pressure is expressed in hectopascals (hPa). The altimeter setting (QNH) is given in hPa. ISA standard sea-level pressure is 1013.25 hPa. Vertical speed is expressed in feet per minute (ft/min). Flight levels are expressed in hundreds of feet (e.g. FL150 = 15,000 ft on the standard pressure setting of 1013 hPa).
exam_tip: Altitude = feet. Pressure = hectopascals (hPa). QNH is in hPa. Standard pressure = 1013.25 hPa. Flight levels are hundreds of feet referenced to 1013 hPa.
key_facts: Altitude: feet (ft)
Atmospheric pressure: hectopascals (hPa)
ISA sea-level pressure: 1013.25 hPa
Vertical speed: feet per minute (ft/min)
Flight levels: hundreds of feet on 1013 hPa
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1 (Section 4: Vertical Measurement), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.2.2(g)

syllabus_desc: Define and where appropriate show the relevant relationships between: Mass, weight and gravitational force (g)
concept: Mass is the quantity of matter in an object, measured in kilograms (kg). It does not change with location. Weight is the force exerted on a mass by gravity, measured in newtons (N). Weight = mass × acceleration due to gravity (W = mg, where g ≈ 9.81 m/s²). An object's mass stays the same everywhere, but its weight varies slightly with location (gravity varies with latitude and altitude).
exam_tip: Mass = amount of matter (kg, doesn't change). Weight = force of gravity on that mass (newtons). W = mg. In everyday aviation, 'weight' is used loosely to mean mass in kg, but the exam may test the technical difference.
key_facts: Mass: quantity of matter, measured in kg
Weight: force due to gravity, measured in newtons (N)
Weight = mass × gravity (W = mg)
g ≈ 9.81 m/s²
Mass is constant; weight varies with gravity
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1 (Section 5: Energy), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.2.4(a)

syllabus_desc: Define and where appropriate show the relevant relationships between: Inertia
concept: Inertia is the tendency of an object to resist changes in its state of motion. An object at rest tends to stay at rest, and an object in motion tends to stay in motion at the same speed and in the same direction, unless acted upon by an external force. This is Newton's First Law of Motion. The greater the mass of an object, the greater its inertia.
exam_tip: Inertia = resistance to change in motion. More mass = more inertia. This is Newton's First Law. In aviation, inertia explains why a heavy aircraft needs more force (thrust) to accelerate and more distance to stop.
key_facts: Inertia: tendency to resist changes in motion
Newton's First Law of Motion
Greater mass = greater inertia
Applies to acceleration, deceleration, and direction change
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1 (Section 5: Energy), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.2.4(b)

syllabus_desc: Define and where appropriate show the relevant relationships between: Momentum
concept: Momentum is the product of an object's mass and velocity (p = m × v). It's a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. In flying terms, think about a heavy aircraft at high speed versus a light one: the heavier, faster aeroplane has far more momentum and needs much more force (and distance) to stop or change direction. That's why your landing roll is longer when you're heavy and fast. Conservation of momentum means the total momentum in a closed system stays constant — it's the same principle that explains why a propeller pushes air backwards and the aeroplane moves forwards.
exam_tip: Momentum = mass × velocity. It's a vector. Heavier and faster means more momentum. Conservation of momentum often comes up in questions about propeller thrust and jet propulsion.
key_facts: Momentum = mass × velocity (p = m × v)
Vector quantity — has both magnitude and direction
Greater mass or velocity means greater momentum
Conservation of momentum: total momentum in a closed system is constant
Heavier/faster aircraft need more force and distance to change direction or stop
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1 (Section 5), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.2.4(c)

syllabus_desc: Define and where appropriate show the relevant relationships between: Equilibrium
concept: Equilibrium is the state where all forces acting on an object are balanced — there's no net force, so no acceleration. In straight and level flight at constant speed, you're in equilibrium: lift equals weight, thrust equals drag. There are two types. Static equilibrium means the object is at rest or moving at constant velocity with all forces balanced. Dynamic equilibrium means the object is moving at constant velocity — still no net force, but there's motion. If any force changes (say you add power), the equilibrium is disturbed and the aeroplane accelerates until a new equilibrium is reached.
exam_tip: Equilibrium = balanced forces = no acceleration. Straight-and-level unaccelerated flight is the classic aviation example. If they ask 'when is an aircraft in equilibrium?' — constant speed, constant altitude, wings level.
key_facts: Equilibrium: all forces balanced, no net force, no acceleration
In level flight: lift = weight, thrust = drag
Static equilibrium: object at rest with balanced forces
Dynamic equilibrium: object moving at constant velocity with balanced forces
Disturbing equilibrium causes acceleration until new balance is reached
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1 (Section 5), Module 1.5 (force resolution), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.2.4(d)

syllabus_desc: Define and where appropriate show the relevant relationships between: Force vectors, couples and components
concept: A force vector has both magnitude (size) and direction, represented as an arrow. Two equal and opposite forces acting along different parallel lines create a couple, which produces rotation (think of the forces on a propeller blade creating torque). Component forces are what you get when you break a single force into two perpendicular parts — for example, resolving the total aerodynamic reaction into lift (perpendicular to relative airflow) and drag (parallel to it). The resultant is the single force that has the same effect as two or more forces acting together. You find it using the parallelogram of forces or by adding the components.
exam_tip: Remember the difference: a couple produces pure rotation (no translation), while a resultant is the net effect of multiple forces. Breaking lift/drag out of total reaction is the classic example of resolving into components.
key_facts: Force vector: has magnitude (size) and direction
Couple: two equal, opposite, parallel forces producing rotation
Components: a single force resolved into two perpendicular parts
Resultant: single force equivalent to two or more forces combined
Parallelogram of forces: graphical method to find the resultant
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1 (Section 5), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.2.4(e)

syllabus_desc: Define and where appropriate show the relevant relationships between: Newton's Third Law
concept: Newton's Third Law says for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction. In aviation this is everywhere. The propeller pushes air backwards (action), and the air pushes the aeroplane forwards (reaction) — that's thrust. The wing deflects air downwards (action) and the air pushes the wing upwards (reaction) — that's part of how lift works. On the ground, the aircraft's weight pushes down on the landing zone and the landing zone pushes back up with an equal force. It's also why a helicopter rotor spinning one way makes the fuselage want to spin the other way — you need a tail rotor to counteract that reaction.
exam_tip: Newton's Third Law explains thrust (propeller action/reaction) and contributes to lift theory (air deflection). Every force has an equal and opposite reaction — look for it in propulsion and aerodynamics questions.
key_facts: Newton's Third Law: every action has an equal and opposite reaction
Propeller: pushes air back, air pushes aircraft forward (thrust)
Wing: deflects air down, air pushes wing up (contributes to lift)
Ground reaction: weight pushes down, landing zone pushes up equally
Helicopter torque reaction: rotor spins one way, fuselage wants to spin the other
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1 (Section 5: Energy), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.2.4(f)

syllabus_desc: Define and where appropriate show the relevant relationships between: Distance, time, acceleration and velocity
concept: Distance is how far an object travels (metres or nautical miles). Time is measured in seconds. Velocity is the rate of change of position — distance divided by time, with a direction (m/s or knots). Speed is the same thing without the direction component. Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity — if your speed increases from 50 to 60 knots in 10 seconds, your acceleration is 1 knot per second. The key relationships: distance = velocity × time (d = v × t), and velocity = initial velocity + (acceleration × time). These are the basic equations of motion you'll use when thinking about takeoff rolls, approach speeds, and deceleration during landing.
exam_tip: Distance = speed × time. Acceleration = change in velocity ÷ time. Know the difference between speed (scalar) and velocity (vector). These relationships underpin takeoff and landing distance calculations.
key_facts: Distance = velocity × time (d = v × t)
Velocity = distance ÷ time (with direction)
Acceleration = change in velocity ÷ time (a = Δv / t)
Final velocity = initial velocity + (acceleration × time)
Speed is scalar (magnitude only); velocity is vector (magnitude + direction)
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1 (Section 5: Energy), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.2.4(g)

syllabus_desc: Define and where appropriate show the relevant relationships between: Kinetic and potential energy
concept: Kinetic energy (KE) is the energy of motion: KE = ½ × mass × velocity² (½mv²). The velocity-squared part is critical — double your speed and you've got four times the kinetic energy. That's why stopping distance increases dramatically with speed. Potential energy (PE) is stored energy due to position (height): PE = mass × gravity × height (mgh). An aircraft at altitude has potential energy that can be converted to kinetic energy in a descent — trading height for speed. In a glide, you're converting PE to KE and using it to overcome drag. Energy is conserved: the total of kinetic plus potential energy stays constant (ignoring losses like drag), which is why an aircraft that dives gains speed and one that climbs loses speed.
exam_tip: KE = ½mv² — the velocity SQUARED is the key. Double the speed, quadruple the energy and stopping distance. PE = mgh. In a glide, you trade height for speed. Energy conservation links altitude and airspeed.
key_facts: Kinetic energy: KE = ½mv² (energy of motion)
Double speed = four times the kinetic energy (velocity squared)
Potential energy: PE = mgh (energy of position/height)
Total energy conserved: KE + PE = constant (ignoring drag)
Gliding converts PE (height) to KE (speed) to overcome drag
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1 (Section 5: Energy), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.2.4(h)

syllabus_desc: Define and where appropriate show the relevant relationships between: Force, work and power
concept: Work is done when a force moves an object through a distance in the direction of that force. The formula is Work = Force × Distance (W = Fd). Work is measured in joules (J), where 1 joule = 1 newton × 1 metre. If the force and movement are not in the same direction, only the component of force in the direction of movement does work.
exam_tip: Work = Force × Distance. Measured in joules. If there's no movement, no work is done  -  no matter how much force is applied.
key_facts: Work = Force × Distance (W = Fd)
Measured in joules (J)
1 joule = 1 newton × 1 metre
Only the force component in the direction of movement counts
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1 (Section 5: Energy), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.2.4(i)

syllabus_desc: Define and where appropriate show the relevant relationships between: Forces involved in the motion of an object travelling in a circular path
concept: When an object moves in a circular path (like an aircraft in a turn), it needs a force directed towards the centre of the circle — this is centripetal force. Without it, the object would fly off in a straight line (Newton's First Law). In a banked turn, the horizontal component of lift provides the centripetal force. The tighter the turn (smaller radius) or the faster the speed, the more centripetal force you need. The formula is F = mv²/r, where m is mass, v is velocity, and r is the radius. This is why steeper bank angles are needed for tighter turns — you need more of that horizontal lift component pulling you towards the centre.
exam_tip: Centripetal force = mv²/r. In a turn, it's the horizontal component of lift. More bank = more centripetal force = tighter turn. Don't confuse centripetal (towards centre) with centrifugal (apparent outward force felt by the pilot).
key_facts: Centripetal force: directed toward the centre of the circular path
In a turn, horizontal component of lift provides centripetal force
Without centripetal force, object continues in a straight line
Centripetal force: F = mv²/r (increases with mass, speed², decreases with radius)
Steeper bank = more horizontal lift component = tighter turn possible
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.1 (Section 5: Energy), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.2.4(j)

syllabus_desc: Name the principal gases which constitute the atmosphere.
concept: The atmosphere is composed primarily of nitrogen (approximately 78%), oxygen (approximately 21%), and trace gases including argon, carbon dioxide, and water vapour (approximately 1% combined). These proportions remain essentially constant up to about 80,000 ft. Water vapour content varies with location and weather conditions and is the primary driver of weather phenomena.
exam_tip: Nitrogen 78%, oxygen 21%, other 1%. These proportions are constant with altitude. Water vapour varies and drives weather.
key_facts: Nitrogen: ~78%
Oxygen: ~21%
Argon and other trace gases: ~1%
Proportions constant up to ~80,000 ft
Water vapour content varies with conditions
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3 (atmospheric composition), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.4.2

syllabus_desc: Define air density.
concept: Air density is the mass of air per unit volume, typically expressed in kilograms per cubic metre (kg/m³). At ISA sea level, the standard air density is approximately 1.225 kg/m³. Air density is critical to aircraft performance because both engine power output and aerodynamic forces (lift and drag) depend directly on the density of the air.
exam_tip: Density = mass per unit volume. ISA sea level density ≈ 1.225 kg/m³. Both engine power and wing lift depend on air density  -  less dense air means less performance.
key_facts: Air density: mass of air per unit volume (kg/m³)
ISA sea-level density: ~1.225 kg/m³
Affects engine power output
Affects aerodynamic forces (lift and drag)
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3 (density), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.4.4

syllabus_desc: Explain how air density varies with altitude within the atmosphere.
concept: Air density decreases with increasing altitude. As altitude increases, there are fewer air molecules per unit volume because atmospheric pressure decreases. At higher altitudes, the engine receives less oxygen per cycle (reducing power output), and the wings must work at higher angles of attack or higher speeds to generate the same lift. This is why aircraft performance degrades at high altitude and in hot conditions.
exam_tip: Density decreases with altitude. Less dense air means: less engine power, less lift at the same speed, and degraded overall performance.
key_facts: Air density decreases with increasing altitude
Fewer air molecules per unit volume at height
Engine receives less oxygen → less power
Wings must work harder to produce the same lift
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3 (altitude effects on density), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.4.6

syllabus_desc: State the relationship between pressure/temperature and the density of an air mass.
concept: Pressure, temperature, and density are interrelated through the gas law. If temperature increases while pressure stays constant, density decreases (hot air is less dense). If pressure increases while temperature stays constant, density increases. In the atmosphere, as altitude increases, both pressure and temperature decrease, causing density to decrease. Hot days at high elevation produce very low air density, significantly degrading aircraft performance.
exam_tip: Hot air is less dense (pressure constant). High pressure air is more dense (temperature constant). In the atmosphere, all three decrease with altitude. Hot + high = poor performance.
key_facts: Temperature up (pressure constant) → density down
Pressure up (temperature constant) → density up
All three decrease with altitude in the troposphere
Hot days at high elevation = very low density = poor performance
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3 (gas relationships), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.4.8

syllabus_desc: Describe how pressure, temperature and density normally vary within the atmosphere.
concept: In the troposphere (the layer of atmosphere where most aviation occurs), pressure, temperature, and density all decrease with increasing altitude. Temperature decreases at approximately 2°C per 1,000 ft (the lapse rate). As temperature decreases, pressure also decreases because cooler air is less energetic. Density decreases because there are fewer air molecules per unit volume at higher altitudes. In the International Standard Atmosphere (ISA), sea-level conditions are 15°C, 1013.25 hPa.
exam_tip: All three decrease together in the troposphere. The most common wrong answer is selecting an option where one of the three increases. Remember: as you go up, everything goes down  -  pressure, temperature, and density.
key_facts: Pressure decreases with altitude
Temperature decreases at ~2°C per 1,000 ft (lapse rate)
Density decreases with altitude
ISA sea level: 15°C, 1013.25 hPa
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (atmospheric density discussion), Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3 (altitude effects), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.4.10

syllabus_desc: Explain the basis for the International Standard Atmosphere (ISA).
concept: The ISA is a theoretical model of the atmosphere used as a standard reference for aircraft performance calculations, instrument calibration, and airspace design. It defines standard values of temperature, pressure, and density at various altitudes. The ISA assumes: sea-level temperature of 15°C, sea-level pressure of 1013.25 hPa, a temperature lapse rate of 1.98°C per 1,000 ft (approximately 2°C/1,000 ft) up to the tropopause at 36,090 ft, and standard density of 1.225 kg/m³ at sea level.
exam_tip: ISA is a theoretical standard  -  the real atmosphere rarely matches it exactly. It provides a baseline for performance calculations. Know the standard values: 15°C, 1013.25 hPa, lapse rate ~2°C/1,000 ft.
key_facts: ISA: theoretical standard atmosphere model
Used for performance calculations and instrument calibration
Assumes constant lapse rate up to the tropopause
Tropopause at 36,090 ft in ISA
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3 (ISA), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.4.12

syllabus_desc: State the ISA sea level pressure and temperature conditions.
concept: The ISA standard sea-level conditions are: temperature 15°C (288.15 K) and pressure 1013.25 hPa (1013.25 inches of mercury). These values serve as the reference point for all ISA calculations. When actual conditions differ from ISA (e.g. a hot day at 25°C), the difference is expressed as ISA deviation (in this case ISA +10°C).
exam_tip: 15°C and 1013.25 hPa  -  memorise these. ISA deviation = actual temperature minus ISA temperature at that altitude.
key_facts: ISA sea-level temperature: 15°C (288.15 K)
ISA sea-level pressure: 1013.25 hPa (1013.25 hPa)
ISA deviation = actual temp minus ISA temp at altitude
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3 (ISA values), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.4.14

syllabus_desc: State the approximate temperature lapse rate up to the tropopause.
concept: In the ISA, temperature decreases at a constant rate of approximately 2°C per 1,000 ft (more precisely 1.98°C per 1,000 ft, or 6.5°C per 1,000 m) from sea level up to the tropopause at 36,090 ft. At the tropopause, the ISA temperature is -56.5°C, and temperature remains constant above this altitude in the stratosphere.
exam_tip: Lapse rate = 2°C per 1,000 ft. This continues up to the tropopause (36,090 ft), where temperature is -56.5°C and stops decreasing.
key_facts: ISA lapse rate: ~2°C per 1,000 ft (1.98°C precisely)
Lapse rate applies from sea level to tropopause
Tropopause at 36,090 ft
Temperature at tropopause: -56.5°C
Temperature constant above tropopause (stratosphere)
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3 (ISA lapse rate), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.4.16

syllabus_desc: Describe what an aerofoil is and distinguish between different aerofoil designs.
concept: An aerofoil is a cross-sectional shape designed to produce an aerodynamic force (primarily lift) when air flows over it. Common designs include: cambered (asymmetric) aerofoils with more curvature on the upper surface than the lower  -  these produce lift even at zero angle of attack; symmetrical aerofoils with equal curvature above and below the chord line  -  used for tail surfaces and aerobatic aircraft, producing lift only at positive angles of attack; and supercritical aerofoils designed for higher-speed flight with a flatter upper surface.
exam_tip: An aerofoil is any shape that produces lift in an airflow. Cambered aerofoils produce lift at zero AoA; symmetrical ones only produce lift at positive AoA.
key_facts: Aerofoil: shape designed to produce aerodynamic force
Cambered (asymmetric): lift at zero AoA
Symmetrical: lift only at positive AoA
Used for rotor blades, tail surfaces, propeller blades
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (aerofoil definitions), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.2

syllabus_desc: Define: Leading edge
concept: The leading edge is the foremost point of an aerofoil  -  the rounded front edge where the airflow first meets the rotor blade. It is typically rounded to allow smooth airflow attachment at varying angles of attack. The shape of the leading edge affects the stall characteristics of the rotor blade.
exam_tip: Leading edge = the front of the aerofoil. It's rounded to help air attach smoothly.
key_facts: Leading edge: foremost point of the aerofoil
Typically rounded for smooth airflow attachment
Shape affects stall characteristics
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (aerofoil terminology), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.4(a)

syllabus_desc: Define: Trailing edge
concept: The trailing edge is the rearmost point of an aerofoil  -  the sharp or thin rear edge where the upper and lower surface airflows rejoin. Control surfaces (cyclic controls, elevators, rudder) and flaps are hinged at or near the trailing edge. The Kutta condition requires smooth airflow departure from the trailing edge for lift to be generated.
exam_tip: Trailing edge = the back of the aerofoil. Control surfaces and flaps are attached here.
key_facts: Trailing edge: rearmost point of the aerofoil
Where upper and lower airflows rejoin
Control surfaces and flaps hinged here
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (aerofoil terminology), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.4(b)

syllabus_desc: Define: Chord
concept: The chord line is the straight line from the leading edge to the trailing edge of an aerofoil. The chord is the length of this line. The chord line is used as the reference for measuring the angle of attack (the angle between the chord line and the relative airflow). It is an imaginary reference line, not a physical feature of the rotor blade.
exam_tip: Chord line = straight line from leading edge to trailing edge. Chord = the length of that line. The angle of attack is measured between the chord line and the relative airflow.
key_facts: Chord line: straight line from leading edge to trailing edge
Chord: the length of the chord line
Used as reference for measuring angle of attack
An imaginary reference line
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (aerofoil terminology), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.4(c)

syllabus_desc: Define: Chord line
concept: Thickness is the maximum distance between the upper and lower surfaces of an aerofoil, measured perpendicular to the chord line. The thickness-to-chord ratio (t/c) expresses the thickness as a percentage of the chord length. A typical light aircraft rotor blade has a t/c ratio of about 12-15%. Thicker aerofoils generally produce more lift but also more drag; thinner aerofoils are used for higher-speed aircraft.
exam_tip: Thickness = max distance between upper and lower surfaces, measured perpendicular to the chord line. Don't confuse thickness with camber.
key_facts: Thickness: max perpendicular distance between surfaces
Thickness-to-chord ratio (t/c): expressed as percentage
Typical light aircraft rotor blade: 12-15% t/c ratio
Thicker aerofoils: more lift, more drag
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (aerofoil terminology), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.4(d)

syllabus_desc: Define: Thickness
concept: The mean camber line is the imaginary line equidistant between the upper and lower surfaces of the aerofoil at all points along the chord. For a symmetrical aerofoil, the mean camber line coincides with the chord line. For a cambered aerofoil, the mean camber line curves above the chord line. The maximum distance between the mean camber line and the chord line defines the camber of the aerofoil.
exam_tip: Mean camber line = line equidistant between upper and lower surfaces. On a symmetrical aerofoil it's the same as the chord line. Maximum distance between camber line and chord line = the camber.
key_facts: Mean camber line: equidistant between upper and lower surfaces
Symmetrical aerofoil: camber line = chord line
Cambered aerofoil: camber line curves above chord line
Maximum distance from camber line to chord line = camber
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (aerofoil terminology), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.4(e)

syllabus_desc: Define: Camber
concept: Camber is the curvature of an aerofoil, measured as the maximum distance between the chord line and the mean camber line. A positively cambered aerofoil has more curvature on the upper surface than the lower surface, and generates lift even at zero angle of attack. A symmetrical aerofoil has equal curvature above and below the chord line, and generates lift only at positive angles of attack.
exam_tip: Camber is about curvature, not thickness, not chord length, and not angle of attack. It is measured between the chord line and the mean camber line.
key_facts: Camber = curvature of the aerofoil
Measured as max distance between chord line and mean camber line
Positive camber: more curvature on upper surface
Symmetrical aerofoil: equal camber above and below
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (aerofoil definitions), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.4(f)

syllabus_desc: Define relative airflow and angle of attack.
concept: The relative airflow (or relative wind) is the direction from which the air approaches the aerofoil. It is always equal and opposite to the direction of the aircraft's flight path through the air. The angle of attack (AoA) is the angle between the chord line of the aerofoil and the relative airflow. Angle of attack is NOT the same as pitch attitude  -  an aircraft can have a nose-up pitch attitude but a low angle of attack if it is descending, and vice versa.
exam_tip: Relative airflow = opposite to flight path direction. AoA = angle between chord line and relative airflow. AoA is NOT pitch attitude  -  this is a critical distinction that exam questions test.
key_facts: Relative airflow: direction from which air approaches the aerofoil
Always opposite to the flight path through the air
Angle of attack: angle between chord line and relative airflow
AoA is NOT the same as pitch attitude
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (relative airflow, AoA), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.6

syllabus_desc: Explain Bernoulli's Theorem in simple terms.
concept: Bernoulli's Theorem states that in a steady flow of an incompressible fluid, the total pressure (static pressure + dynamic pressure) remains constant along a streamline. When air accelerates over the curved upper surface of an aerofoil, its velocity increases and dynamic pressure increases. To maintain constant total pressure, static pressure must decrease. The resulting lower static pressure on the upper surface compared to the higher static pressure on the lower surface creates the pressure difference that produces lift.
exam_tip: Total pressure = static + dynamic = constant (along a streamline). Speed up → dynamic pressure up → static pressure down. This pressure difference between upper and lower surfaces produces lift.
key_facts: Total pressure = static pressure + dynamic pressure (constant)
Air accelerates over upper surface → lower static pressure above
Higher static pressure below rotor blade
Pressure difference = lift
Applies to incompressible, steady flow
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Bernoulli, lift generation), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.8

syllabus_desc: Describe streamline airflow around an aerofoil.
concept: When streamline (smooth, laminar) airflow passes over an aerofoil at a moderate angle of attack, the air accelerates over the curved upper surface and decelerates over the lower surface. The accelerated flow over the upper surface creates reduced static pressure above the rotor blade, while higher static pressure is maintained below. This pressure difference is the primary mechanism generating lift. At the critical angle of attack (around 15-20° for typical aerofoils), the smooth airflow over the upper surface breaks down, separation occurs, and the rotor blade stalls.
exam_tip: Streamline airflow means the air flows smoothly over the surface without separating. The key point is that airflow accelerates over the upper surface, resulting in lower static pressure above than below.
key_facts: Airflow accelerates over the upper surface
Lower static pressure on upper surface, higher on lower surface
Pressure difference produces lift
Smooth flow breaks down at the critical angle of attack (stall)
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (streamline flow, lift generation), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.10

syllabus_desc: Explain the changes which occur to dynamic and static pressure wherever the speed of the airflow is: Increased
concept: From Bernoulli's principle, in incompressible flow the total pressure (static + dynamic) remains constant. When the speed of the airflow increases, dynamic pressure increases and static pressure decreases. This is the fundamental principle behind how a rotor blade generates lift  -  the faster flow over the upper surface has higher dynamic pressure and lower static pressure compared to the slower flow beneath.
exam_tip: Speed up = dynamic pressure up, static pressure down. Total pressure stays constant. This is Bernoulli's principle and it underpins everything about how rotor blades produce lift.
key_facts: Total pressure = static pressure + dynamic pressure (constant in incompressible flow)
Increased airflow speed: dynamic pressure increases
Increased airflow speed: static pressure decreases
This is Bernoulli's principle
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (lift generation, pressure relationships), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.12(a)

syllabus_desc: Explain the changes which occur to dynamic and static pressure wherever the speed of the airflow is: Decreased
concept: An aerofoil is designed so that airflow over the curved upper surface accelerates relative to the lower surface. By Bernoulli's principle, when airflow speed increases, static pressure decreases. Therefore, the upper surface has lower pressure than the lower surface. This pressure differential creates a net upward force (lift). The magnitude of this pressure difference depends on the angle of attack and aerofoil shape. At higher angles of attack, the upper surface pressure becomes more negative relative to the lower surface, increasing lift until the stall point is reached.
exam_tip: Bernoulli: faster airflow = lower pressure. Upper surface curves more, airflow accelerates, pressure drops. This pressure difference creates lift.
key_facts: Airflow accelerates over curved upper surface
Faster airflow → lower static pressure (Bernoulli)
Upper surface pressure < lower surface pressure
Pressure differential creates net upward force (lift)
Magnitude depends on AoA and aerofoil shape
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Pressure distribution and lift), Kermode Mechanics of Flight, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.12

syllabus_desc: With the aid of diagrams, explain: Venturi effect
concept: A venturi is a tube that narrows in the middle. As air flows through the narrow section (the throat), it must accelerate to maintain the same mass flow rate. According to Bernoulli's principle, this increased velocity produces a decrease in static pressure at the throat. A carburettor uses this effect  -  air florotor blade through the carburettor venturi creates a pressure drop that draws fuel from the float chamber. The venturi effect also explains how air accelerates over the curved upper surface of a rotor blade.
exam_tip: Air speeds up through a constriction → static pressure drops. This is how a carburettor draws fuel. It also relates to how air accelerates over the top of a rotor blade.
key_facts: Venturi: tube that narrows in the middle
Air accelerates through the narrow section (throat)
Static pressure decreases at the throat
Carburettor uses venturi effect to draw fuel
Relates to airflow acceleration over a rotor blade
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (venturi, Bernoulli), Module 1.2 (carburettor venturi), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.14(a)

syllabus_desc: With the aid of diagrams, explain: The pressure distribution around an aerofoil which is producing lift
concept: The pressure distribution around a lifting aerofoil shows reduced static pressure on the upper surface, with the greatest pressure reduction near the area of maximum curvature (maximum camber). The lower surface has relatively higher static pressure. The net effect of this pressure distribution across the entire aerofoil surface produces the total aerodynamic force, which can be resolved into lift (perpendicular to the relative airflow) and drag (parallel to the relative airflow).
exam_tip: The pressure reduction is not uniform across the upper surface  -  it is greatest near the point of maximum camber. The lower surface has higher pressure than the upper surface but not necessarily higher than freestream.
key_facts: Reduced static pressure on upper surface
Greatest pressure reduction near point of maximum camber
Higher pressure on lower surface relative to upper
Net pressure difference produces the total aerodynamic force
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (pressure distribution, lift), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.14(b)

syllabus_desc: Define the terms: Total reaction (TR)
concept: The total reaction (TR) is the single resultant aerodynamic force produced by the pressure distribution around an aerofoil. It represents the combined effect of all pressure forces (both above and below the rotor blade) acting on the aerofoil surface. The total reaction can be resolved into two components: lift (perpendicular to the relative airflow) and drag (parallel to the relative airflow, opposing motion).
exam_tip: Total reaction = the single resultant of all aerodynamic pressure forces on the aerofoil. It is resolved into lift (perpendicular to airflow) and drag (parallel, opposing motion).
key_facts: TR: single resultant of all aerodynamic forces on the aerofoil
Result of pressure distribution around the rotor blade
Resolved into lift (perpendicular to relative airflow) and drag (parallel)
Acts through the centre of pressure
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (total reaction), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.16(a)

syllabus_desc: Define the terms: Centre of pressure (CP)
concept: The centre of pressure (CP) is the single point on the chord line through which the total aerodynamic force (the resultant of all the pressure forces acting on the aerofoil) is considered to act. It moves with changes in angle of attack.
exam_tip: CP is about where the total reaction acts on the chord line  -  not where drag acts, not the geometric centre, and not the boundary layer transition point.
key_facts: CP is the point on the chord line where the total reaction is considered to act
It is the resultant of all pressure forces on the aerofoil
CP position moves with changes in angle of attack
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Section 2.4: Centre of Pressure and Centre of Gravity), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.16(b)

syllabus_desc: Describe how TR and CP change with increasing angle of attack for a lifting aerofoil.
concept: As the angle of attack increases (below the stall), the total reaction increases in magnitude (more lift and drag are produced) and the centre of pressure moves forward along the chord line. The forward CP movement with increasing AoA has implications for longitudinal stability  -  it tends to pitch the nose further up, which is destabilising. At angles beyond the stall, the total reaction decreases sharply and the CP moves rearward.
exam_tip: Increasing AoA → bigger total reaction and CP moves forward. After the stall → TR drops and CP moves back. The forward CP movement is destabilising (pitches nose up further).
key_facts: Increasing AoA: total reaction increases in magnitude
Increasing AoA: CP moves forward along the chord
Forward CP movement is destabilising (nose-up tendency)
Beyond stall: TR decreases, CP moves rearward
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (CP movement, AoA effects), Kermode  -  Mechanics of Flight, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.18

syllabus_desc: Show how movement of the CP varies between symmetrical and non-symmetrical aerofoils.
concept: On a cambered (non-symmetrical) aerofoil, as the angle of attack increases from a small positive value toward the stall, the CP moves forward. After the stall, the CP moves rearward. On a symmetrical aerofoil, the CP remains approximately at the quarter-chord point (25% of the chord from the leading edge) regardless of angle of attack.
exam_tip: For cambered aerofoils: increasing AoA = CP moves forward (up to the stall). For symmetrical aerofoils: CP stays roughly at the quarter-chord point. This is a common exam question.
key_facts: Cambered aerofoil: CP moves forward as AoA increases (below stall)
Cambered aerofoil: CP moves rearward after the stall
Symmetrical aerofoil: CP remains at approximately the quarter-chord point
CP movement affects longitudinal stability
source: Kermode  -  Mechanics of Flight (Ch. on aerofoil characteristics), Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.20

syllabus_desc: Define the Lift and Drag components of Total Reaction.
concept: The total reaction (TR) on an aerofoil is resolved into two perpendicular components: lift acts perpendicular to the relative airflow (upward in level flight, supporting the aircraft's weight), and drag acts parallel to the relative airflow (rearward, opposing the aircraft's motion). Both increase with angle of attack up to the stall. At the stall, lift decreases sharply while drag continues to increase.
exam_tip: Lift = perpendicular to relative airflow. Drag = parallel to relative airflow (opposing motion). Both are components of the total reaction. At the stall, lift drops but drag keeps increasing.
key_facts: Total reaction resolves into lift and drag
Lift: perpendicular to relative airflow
Drag: parallel to relative airflow, opposing motion
Both increase with AoA (below stall)
At stall: lift drops, drag increases
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (lift and drag components), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.22

syllabus_desc: With respect to lift: State the lift formula
concept: The lift formula is: L = CL × ½ρV²S, where L = lift force (in newtons), CL = coefficient of lift (dimensionless), ρ (rho) = air density (kg/m³), V = true airspeed (m/s), and S = rotor blade planform area (m²). This formula shows that lift is proportional to air density, the square of airspeed, and rotor blade area. It is proportional to the coefficient of lift, which is controlled by angle of attack and aerofoil shape.
exam_tip: Memorise L = CL × ½ρV²S. Note that lift varies with V² (the square of airspeed), so doubling speed quadruples lift.
key_facts: L = CL × ½ρV²S
L = lift force (newtons)
CL = coefficient of lift (dimensionless)
ρ = air density (kg/m³)
V = true airspeed (m/s); note V²
S = rotor blade planform area (m²)
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Lift formula), Kermode Mechanics of Flight Chapter 2, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.24

syllabus_desc: With respect to lift: Summarise the factors affecting lift. (i.e. angle of attack, aerofoil shape
concept: From the lift formula L = CL × ½ρV²S, the factors affecting lift are: (1) Coefficient of lift (CL), determined by angle of attack and aerofoil shape. (2) Air density (ρ), which decreases with altitude and increases in cold air. (3) Indicated airspeed (IAS), with lift varying as the square of velocity. (4) Rotor blade area (S), which is fixed for a given aircraft. In practical flying, the pilot controls lift primarily through angle of attack (which changes CL) and airspeed. Altitude and temperature affect density, reducing lift in high-altitude or warm conditions.
exam_tip: Exam may ask about factors affecting lift. Know: AoA and aerofoil shape affect CL, density decreases with altitude, lift varies with V². The pilot controls AoA and IAS to control lift.
key_facts: Coefficient of lift (CL): affected by AoA and aerofoil shape
Air density (ρ): decreases with altitude; higher in cold air
Indicated airspeed (IAS): lift ∝ V² (square of velocity)
Rotor blade area (S): fixed for given aircraft
Pilot controls AoA and IAS to manage lift
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5, 1.6 (Lift factors), Kermode Mechanics of Flight, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.24

syllabus_desc: Identify the primary factors determining the coefficient of lift (CL) for an aerofoil.
concept: The coefficient of lift (CL) for an aerofoil is primarily determined by the angle of attack and the aerofoil's shape (camber and thickness distribution). CL increases linearly with angle of attack up to the critical angle (stall). Rotor blade area and airspeed determine the actual lift force, but CL itself is a function of the aerofoil's geometry and angle of attack.
exam_tip: CL depends on angle of attack and aerofoil shape. It does not depend on airspeed or rotor blade area  -  those affect the lift force, not the coefficient.
key_facts: CL depends primarily on angle of attack
CL depends on aerofoil shape (camber and thickness distribution)
CL increases linearly with AoA up to the stall
Airspeed and rotor blade area affect lift force, not CL itself
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (lift factors, AoA relationship), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.26

syllabus_desc: Describe a typical CL versus angle of attack curve (graph).
concept: The CL vs AoA curve shows that CL increases approximately linearly with angle of attack from a small negative angle (where CL = 0 for a cambered aerofoil) through the normal operating range. The curve reaches a maximum value (CLmax) at the critical angle of attack (typically 15-18° for training aircraft). Beyond this critical angle, CL drops sharply as airflow separation occurs and the rotor blade stalls. For a symmetrical aerofoil, CL = 0 at zero AoA; for a cambered aerofoil, CL = 0 at a small negative AoA.
exam_tip: CL increases linearly with AoA until the critical angle, then drops sharply (stall). CLmax occurs at the critical angle. Cambered aerofoil has positive CL at zero AoA; symmetrical has zero CL at zero AoA.
key_facts: CL increases linearly with AoA in normal range
CLmax reached at the critical angle of attack (~15-18°)
Beyond critical angle: CL drops sharply (stall)
Cambered aerofoil: positive CL at zero AoA
Symmetrical aerofoil: zero CL at zero AoA
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (CL curve), Module 1.6 (stall angle), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.28

syllabus_desc: On a typical CL versus angle of attack curve, identify the critical stalling angle.
concept: The critical angle of attack is the angle at which CL reaches its maximum value (CLmax) on the CL vs AoA curve. Beyond this angle, airflow separates from the upper surface of the rotor blade and the rotor blade stalls. For most light training aircraft, the critical angle is approximately 15-18°. The critical angle of attack is essentially constant for a given aerofoil shape  -  it does not change significantly with airspeed, weight, altitude, or bank angle. However, the airspeed at which the critical angle is reached does change with these factors.
exam_tip: The critical angle is where CLmax occurs  -  typically 15-18° for training aircraft. The critical angle stays the same regardless of speed, weight, or altitude. What changes is the speed at which you reach it.
key_facts: Critical angle: where CLmax occurs on the CL/AoA curve
Approximately 15-18° for typical training aircraft
Critical angle is constant for a given aerofoil shape
Stall speed changes with weight, load factor, configuration  -  but critical angle does not
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (stall), Module 1.6 (critical angle), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.30

syllabus_desc: State the precaution against flying with ice, frost, snow or other contamination of the aerofoil surfaces.
concept: Aerofoil contamination (frost, ice, snow, dirt, insects, or other deposits on the rotor blade surface) degrades aerodynamic performance by disrupting the smooth airflow over the rotor blade. Even a thin layer of frost can reduce CLmax significantly (increasing stall speed) and increase drag. Precautions include: removing all frost, ice, and snow before flight; keeping rotor blade surfaces clean; inspecting leading edges for insect accumulation on pre-flight; and never attempting to take off with contaminated rotor blades. Contamination increases the stall speed and reduces climb performance.
exam_tip: Even a thin layer of frost or insects on the rotor blade can increase stall speed significantly. NEVER take off with contaminated rotor blade surfaces. Always clean the rotor blades before flight.
key_facts: Contamination disrupts smooth airflow over the rotor blade
Reduces CLmax and increases stall speed
Increases drag and reduces climb performance
Remove all frost, ice, snow, dirt before flight
Even a thin layer of frost is dangerous
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6 (contamination effects), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.32

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between induced drag, parasite and profile drag.
concept: Induced drag is caused by the rotor blade's production of lift  -  rotor bladetip vortices create a downwash that tilts the lift vector rearward, adding a drag component. It decreases with increasing airspeed. Parasitic drag is the drag caused by the shape and surface friction of all non-lifting parts of the aircraft; it increases with the square of airspeed. Profile drag is the drag associated with the aerofoil section itself and consists of two parts: form drag (due to the aerofoil's shape) and skin friction drag (due to air rubbing along the surface).
exam_tip: Induced drag: from lift production, high at low speed. Parasitic drag: from shape/friction of non-lifting parts, high at high speed. Profile drag: form drag + skin friction drag of the aerofoil itself.
key_facts: Induced drag: caused by lift production (rotor bladetip vortices)
Parasitic drag: caused by shape and friction of non-lifting parts
Profile drag: form drag + skin friction drag of the aerofoil
Induced drag decreases with speed; parasitic drag increases with speed²
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (drag types), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.34

syllabus_desc: List the elements of profile drag.
concept: Profile drag consists of two elements: form drag, which is caused by the shape of the aerofoil  -  blunter shapes create more turbulent wake and higher form drag (reduced by streamlining); and skin friction drag, which is caused by the viscosity of air rubbing along the surface of the aerofoil  -  rougher surfaces produce more skin friction drag (reduced by smooth, polished surfaces). Profile drag increases with airspeed.
exam_tip: Profile drag = form drag + skin friction. Form drag is about shape (streamline to reduce). Skin friction is about surface roughness (smooth surfaces reduce it).
key_facts: Profile drag = form drag + skin friction drag
Form drag: caused by aerofoil shape; reduced by streamlining
Skin friction drag: caused by air rubbing on surface; reduced by smooth surfaces
Profile drag increases with airspeed
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (profile drag), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.36

syllabus_desc: State the factors affecting parasite drag, and profile (form and skin friction) drag.
concept: Parasite drag is the drag caused by the shape and surface of all non-lifting parts of the aircraft. It increases with the square of airspeed. Parasite drag has three components: skin friction drag (caused by air rubbing against the aircraft surface  -  reduced by smooth surfaces), form drag (caused by the shape of components  -  reduced by streamlining), and interference drag (caused by airflow disruption where different parts of the aircraft meet, such as rotor blade-fuselage junctions).
exam_tip: Parasite drag is about shape, frontal area, and surface roughness. It increases with airspeed squared. Don't confuse it with induced drag, which is related to lift production.
key_facts: Parasite drag increases with airspeed² (V²)
Skin friction drag: air rubbing on surfaces
Form drag: caused by bluff shapes
Interference drag: airflow disruption at component junctions
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Section 4.1: Parasite Drag), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.38

syllabus_desc: Explain how induced drag varies depending on: Angle of attack of the aerofoil
concept: Induced drag is caused by the rotor blade's production of lift. When a rotor blade generates lift, it creates rotor bladetip vortices  -  swirling air trailing behind the rotor blade. Greater lift production means stronger vortices and more induced drag. As the angle of attack increases (below the stall), the rotor blade produces more lift, the rotor bladetip vortices strengthen, and induced drag increases. At slow speeds near the stall, the rotor blade must operate at a high angle of attack to generate sufficient lift, so induced drag is very high. At high speeds, the rotor blade operates at a low angle of attack and induced drag is small.
exam_tip: Induced drag increases with angle of attack. Think: higher AoA = more lift = stronger vortices = more induced drag. The opposite behaviour to parasite drag.
key_facts: Induced drag is caused by lift production (rotor bladetip vortices)
Increases with angle of attack
High at slow speeds (high AoA), low at high speeds (low AoA)
Greater lift production = stronger vortices = more induced drag
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Section 4.2: Induced Drag), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.40(a)

syllabus_desc: Explain how induced drag varies depending on: Aspect ratio
concept: Induced drag decreases as aspect ratio increases. Aspect ratio is the ratio of rotor bladespan to average chord (or rotor bladespan² to rotor blade area). A high aspect ratio rotor blade (long and narrow, like a glider) produces less intense rotor bladetip vortices because the tip effects influence a smaller proportion of the total span. A low aspect ratio rotor blade (short and wide) has proportionally larger tip vortices and higher induced drag. Gliders use very high aspect ratio rotor blades to minimise induced drag and maximise glide performance.
exam_tip: Higher aspect ratio = less induced drag. Gliders have long, narrow rotor blades (high aspect ratio) for this reason. Short, stubby rotor blades (low aspect ratio) have more induced drag.
key_facts: Induced drag decreases with increasing aspect ratio
Aspect ratio = rotor bladespan² ÷ rotor blade area (or span ÷ average chord)
High aspect ratio (long/narrow): less induced drag
Low aspect ratio (short/wide): more induced drag
Gliders use high aspect ratio to minimise induced drag
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (induced drag, aspect ratio), Kermode  -  Mechanics of Flight, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.40(b)

syllabus_desc: Identify curves of parasite, profile, induced and total drag versus aerofoil airspeed.
concept: Total drag is the sum of parasite drag and induced drag. As airspeed increases from zero: induced drag decreases (the rotor blade works at a lower AoA), while parasite drag increases (proportional to V²). There is a specific speed at which total drag is minimum  -  this is where the induced drag and parasite drag curves cross. Below this speed, induced drag dominates; above it, parasite drag dominates. The total drag curve is U-shaped, with minimum total drag at the crossover point.
exam_tip: Total drag = induced + parasite. The total drag curve is U-shaped. Minimum drag speed is where induced drag = parasite drag. Below that speed, induced drag dominates; above, parasite drag dominates.
key_facts: Total drag = induced drag + parasite drag
Induced drag decreases with speed; parasite drag increases with V²
Total drag curve is U-shaped
Minimum total drag: where induced drag = parasite drag
Below min drag speed: induced dominates. Above: parasite dominates
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (drag curves), Module 1.6 (total drag), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.42

syllabus_desc: Describe a typical curve of lift/drag (L/D) ratio versus angle of attack for a symmetrical aerofoil.
concept: The lift-to-drag ratio (L/D) curve shows how the ratio of lift to drag varies with airspeed (or angle of attack). L/D reaches a maximum value at a specific speed  -  this is the speed for best aerodynamic efficiency. At speeds below or above this optimum, the L/D ratio decreases. The maximum L/D speed corresponds to the best glide speed (maximum distance in a glide) and is related to the speed for best range in powered flight.
exam_tip: Maximum L/D = best aerodynamic efficiency. This speed gives: best glide range (engine off), and is close to the best range speed (engine on). Flying faster or slower reduces the L/D ratio.
key_facts: L/D ratio = lift divided by drag
Maximum L/D occurs at one specific speed/AoA
Max L/D speed = best glide speed (max distance, no engine)
Related to best range speed in powered flight
L/D decreases at speeds above or below the optimum
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (L/D ratio), Module 1.6 (range, glide), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.44

syllabus_desc: Identify the approximate angle for best L/D ratio.
concept: The best L/D ratio for most light aircraft aerofoils occurs at an angle of attack of approximately 3-5°. This corresponds to the speed at which total drag is minimum. At angles of attack below this, the rotor blade is not producing enough lift efficiently (too much of the total reaction is wasted as drag). At higher angles, induced drag increases rapidly, degrading the L/D ratio.
exam_tip: Best L/D is at about 3-5° AoA for typical training aircraft rotor blades. This is a relatively small angle of attack  -  roughly the attitude you'd see in cruise or best-glide flight.
key_facts: Best L/D ratio at approximately 3-5° AoA
Corresponds to minimum total drag speed
Below this AoA: inefficient lift production
Above this AoA: induced drag increases rapidly
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (best L/D), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.6.46

syllabus_desc: Identify typical cylinder configurations used for aircraft piston engines.
concept: Light aircraft piston engines most commonly use a horizontally opposed (flat or boxer) cylinder configuration, where cylinders are arranged horizontally on either side of the crankshaft. Common examples include Lycoming O-235, O-320, and Continental O-200 series. This configuration provides a compact, low-profile design with good balance and low vibration. Other configurations include inline (cylinders in a single row) and radial (cylinders arranged in a circle around the crankshaft), though these are less common in modern light aircraft.
exam_tip: Horizontally opposed is the standard for light aircraft  -  know this. Inline and radial are historical or specialist configurations.
key_facts: Horizontally opposed (flat/boxer): most common in light aircraft
Cylinders arranged horizontally on either side of crankshaft
Low vibration, compact design
Common engines: Lycoming O-235/O-320, Continental O-200
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (engine configurations), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.10.2

syllabus_desc: Explain the function of the main components of a four-stroke cycle piston engine including: Cylinders
concept: The cylinders are precisely machined housings within which the pistons move up and down. Each cylinder forms part of the combustion chamber where the fuel/air mixture is compressed and ignited. Cylinders must withstand high temperatures and pressures during the power stroke. In air-cooled engines, cylinders have external cooling fins to dissipate heat. The cylinder walls must be smooth and precisely manufactured to allow the piston rings to form an effective seal.
exam_tip: Cylinders house the pistons and form the combustion chamber. They have cooling fins (air-cooled engines) and must withstand high temperatures and pressures.
key_facts: Cylinders: housings for piston movement
Form part of the combustion chamber
Withstand high temperatures and pressures
Cooling fins for heat dissipation (air-cooled engines)
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (four-stroke components), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.10.4(a)

syllabus_desc: Explain the function of the main components of a four-stroke cycle piston engine including: Cylinder heads
concept: The cylinder head seals the top of the cylinder, houses the inlet and exhaust valves and the spark plugs, and forms part of the combustion chamber. In air-cooled engines (standard in light aircraft), the cylinder head also has cooling fins to dissipate heat. The spark plugs are threaded into the cylinder head and create the electrical spark that ignites the fuel/air mixture at the moment of maximum compression.
exam_tip: The cylinder head does multiple jobs: seals the cylinder, houses valves and spark plugs, provides part of the combustion chamber, and provides cooling surfaces.
key_facts: Seals the top of the cylinder
Houses inlet and exhaust valves
Houses spark plugs
Forms part of the combustion chamber
Has cooling fins (air-cooled engines)
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (four-stroke cycle, spark plugs in cylinder head), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.10.4(b)

syllabus_desc: Explain the function of the main components of a four-stroke cycle piston engine including: Pistons
concept: Pistons are cylindrical plugs that move up and down inside the cylinders. During the compression stroke, the piston compresses the fuel/air mixture. During the power stroke, expanding combustion gases force the piston downward, which drives the crankshaft via the connecting rod. Piston rings (typically 2-3 per piston) form a gas-tight seal between the piston and cylinder wall, preventing combustion gases from leaking past the piston (blow-by) and preventing oil from entering the combustion chamber.
exam_tip: Pistons compress the mixture and are driven down by combustion. Piston rings seal the gap between piston and cylinder wall.
key_facts: Pistons: cylindrical plugs moving up and down in cylinders
Compress fuel/air mixture on compression stroke
Driven down by combustion gases on power stroke
Piston rings (2-3) seal between piston and cylinder wall
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (piston function), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.10.4(c)

syllabus_desc: Explain the function of the main components of a four-stroke cycle piston engine including: Connecting rods
concept: Connecting rods are rigid links between each piston and the crankshaft. They convert the linear (up-and-down) motion of the piston into rotational motion of the crankshaft. Each cylinder has one connecting rod, attached to the piston at the small end (gudgeon pin) and to the crankshaft at the big end (crankpin). Connecting rods must be strong enough to withstand the enormous forces generated during the power stroke.
exam_tip: Connecting rods link pistons to the crankshaft, converting linear motion to rotational motion.
key_facts: Connect each piston to the crankshaft
Convert linear piston motion to rotational crankshaft motion
Small end attaches to piston; big end attaches to crankshaft
Must withstand high forces during power stroke
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (connecting rods), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.10.4(d)

syllabus_desc: Explain the function of the main components of a four-stroke cycle piston engine including: Crankshaft
concept: The crankshaft is the main rotating shaft of the engine. It converts the linear reciprocating motion of all pistons into continuous rotational motion, which ultimately drives the propeller. The crankshaft absorbs power from all cylinders and delivers it as a smooth rotational output. It is supported in the crankcase by main bearings and typically has counterweights for dynamic balancing to reduce vibration.
exam_tip: The crankshaft converts piston motion into rotation and drives the propeller. It absorbs power from all cylinders.
key_facts: Main rotating shaft of the engine
Converts linear piston motion into rotational motion
Drives the propeller
Supported by main bearings in the crankcase
Counterweights for vibration reduction
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (crankshaft), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.10.4(e)

syllabus_desc: Explain the function of the main components of a four-stroke cycle piston engine including: Valves
concept: Each cylinder has an inlet valve and an exhaust valve. The inlet valve opens during the induction stroke to allow the fuel/air mixture to enter the cylinder, then closes to seal the cylinder for compression and power strokes. The exhaust valve opens after the power stroke to allow burned gases to be pushed out of the cylinder during the exhaust stroke. Valves are opened by the camshaft mechanism and closed by valve springs. Precise timing of valve opening and closing is critical for engine efficiency.
exam_tip: Inlet valve lets mixture in during induction. Exhaust valve lets burned gases out during exhaust. Both are closed during compression and power strokes.
key_facts: Inlet valve: admits fuel/air mixture during induction stroke
Exhaust valve: releases burned gases during exhaust stroke
Both closed during compression and power strokes
Opened by camshaft; closed by valve springs
Precise timing is critical for efficiency
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (valve function), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.10.4(f)

syllabus_desc: Explain the function of the main components of a four-stroke cycle piston engine including: Valve operating mechanism
concept: The camshaft is a shaft with lobes (cams) that control the opening and closing of the inlet and exhaust valves. It is driven by the crankshaft through gears, typically rotating at half the crankshaft speed (because in a four-stroke engine, each valve only opens once every two crankshaft revolutions). The shape of each cam lobe determines how far the valve opens and for how long. The camshaft ensures valves open and close in the correct sequence and timing relative to piston position.
exam_tip: The camshaft controls valve timing. It rotates at half crankshaft speed because each valve opens once per two revolutions in a four-stroke engine.
key_facts: Camshaft: controls opening and closing of valves
Driven by crankshaft through gears
Rotates at half crankshaft speed
Cam lobes determine valve lift and duration
Ensures correct valve timing relative to piston position
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (camshaft), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.10.4(g)

syllabus_desc: Explain the function of the main components of a four-stroke cycle piston engine including: Camshaft
concept: The camshaft is a shaft with a series of lobes (cams) that operate the inlet and exhaust valves. As the camshaft rotates, the lobes push on valve lifters or rocker arms, opening the valves at precise times during the engine cycle. The camshaft rotates at half the speed of the crankshaft (one rotation per two crankshaft revolutions) because valves open once per two revolutions in a four-stroke engine. In horizontally opposed engines, there is typically one camshaft per cylinder bank, operating both inlet and exhaust valves on that side.
exam_tip: Camshaft rotates at half crankshaft speed. Lobes push on lifters/rocker arms to open valves at the right time in the cycle.
key_facts: Shaft with lobes (cams) that operate inlet and exhaust valves
Lobes push on valve lifters or rocker arms
Rotates at half crankshaft speed
One rotation per two crankshaft revolutions
Necessary for precise valve timing in four-stroke cycle
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (Engine components), ATC Aircraft General Knowledge, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.10.4

syllabus_desc: Explain the function of the main components of a four-stroke cycle piston engine including: Spark plugs
concept: Spark plugs ignite the fuel-air mixture in the cylinder. Each cylinder typically has two spark plugs (dual ignition) for redundancy and more complete combustion. The spark plugs are fired by the magneto ignition system at the precise moment when the piston is near top dead centre on the compression stroke. The spark jumps across a small air gap between two electrodes, creating a high-temperature flame front that propagates through the fuel-air mixture. Dual ignition ensures that even if one magneto fails, the other continues to fire the opposite spark plug, maintaining engine operation.
exam_tip: Two spark plugs per cylinder for redundancy and complete combustion. Fired by magneto at precise timing to ignite the fuel-air mixture.
key_facts: Ignite fuel-air mixture in cylinder
Two per cylinder (dual ignition)
Provide redundancy; engine continues if one fails
Fired by magneto ignition system
Fire at precise moment near top dead centre
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (Ignition systems), ATC Aircraft General Knowledge, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.10.4

syllabus_desc: Explain the function of the main components of a four-stroke cycle piston engine including: Injectors
concept: Fuel injectors deliver a metered amount of fuel directly into the cylinder or intake port. In fuel-injected engines, injectors are electrically controlled and fire in synchronisation with the engine cycle. Each cylinder has one or more injectors. Fuel injection provides more precise fuel metering than a carburettor, allows better control of fuel distribution among cylinders, permits engine operation over a wider power range, and improves cold-start characteristics. Injectors spray atomised fuel that mixes with intake air to form a combustible mixture.
exam_tip: Injectors deliver metered fuel directly to cylinder/intake. Electrical control allows precise metering and better fuel distribution than carburettors.
key_facts: Deliver metered fuel to cylinder or intake port
Electrically controlled and synchronised with engine cycle
Provide precise fuel metering
Better fuel distribution among cylinders
Improve cold-start and high-altitude performance
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3 (Fuel injection systems), ATC Aircraft General Knowledge, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.10.4

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic principle of operation of a four stroke internal combustion engine.
concept: The four-stroke cycle consists of: (1) Induction  -  inlet valve opens, piston moves down, drawing fuel/air mixture into the cylinder; (2) Compression  -  both valves closed, piston moves up, compressing the mixture to increase pressure and temperature; (3) Power  -  at maximum compression (TDC), spark plug ignites the mixture, high-pressure combustion gases force the piston down  -  this is the only stroke that produces power; (4) Exhaust  -  exhaust valve opens, piston moves up, pushing burned gases out. The cycle completes in two full crankshaft revolutions (720°).
exam_tip: Four strokes: Suck, Squeeze, Bang, Blow. Only the power stroke produces power. The cycle takes two full crankshaft revolutions (720°).
key_facts: Induction: mixture drawn in (inlet valve open, piston down)
Compression: mixture compressed (both valves closed, piston up)
Power: spark ignites mixture, piston forced down (only power-producing stroke)
Exhaust: burned gases expelled (exhaust valve open, piston up)
Complete cycle = 2 crankshaft revolutions (720°)
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (four-stroke cycle), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.10.6

syllabus_desc: Describe the correlation between engine rpm and power output.
concept: In a normally aspirated piston engine, power output increases with increasing RPM. The throttle controls the flow of fuel/air mixture into the engine  -  opening it allows more mixture into the cylinders, increasing the fuel burned per cycle, which increases both power and RPM. More revolutions per minute means more power strokes per minute, producing more power. This is a direct, positive relationship.
exam_tip: More RPM = more power. It is a direct, positive relationship. The most common wrong answer suggests power only increases to 50% RPM then decreases, or that power is inversely proportional to RPM  -  both are incorrect.
key_facts: Power increases with increasing RPM (direct positive relationship)
Throttle controls mixture flow into cylinders
More RPM = more power strokes per minute = more power
Applies to normally aspirated piston engines
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (engine power and throttle control), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.10.8

syllabus_desc: Explain the need for valve timing (i.e. valve lead, lag and overlap).
concept: Valve timing refers to the precise moments when inlet and exhaust valves open and close relative to piston position. Valve lead means opening a valve slightly before the piston reaches the ideal position  -  the inlet valve opens slightly before TDC to take advantage of the piston's momentum. Valve lag means closing a valve after the piston has passed the ideal position  -  the exhaust valve closes slightly after TDC to allow complete scavenging of exhaust gases. Valve overlap is the brief period when both valves are open simultaneously (near TDC between exhaust and induction strokes), which improves cylinder scavenging and helps draw fresh mixture into the cylinder.
exam_tip: Valve lead = valve opens early. Valve lag = valve closes late. Valve overlap = both open simultaneously. All improve engine efficiency and scavenging.
key_facts: Valve lead: valve opens slightly before ideal piston position
Valve lag: valve closes slightly after ideal piston position
Valve overlap: both valves open simultaneously near TDC
Overlap improves cylinder scavenging (exhaust gas removal)
All improve engine breathing efficiency
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (valve timing), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.10.10

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic differences between compression ignition (diesel) engines and conventional ignition engines.
concept: Conventional spark ignition (used in most light aircraft) uses spark plugs to ignite a fuel/air mixture at a precisely timed moment. The mixture is ignited externally by the spark. Compression ignition (diesel cycle) compresses air alone to very high pressure and temperature, then injects fuel which ignites spontaneously from the heat of compression  -  no spark plug is needed. Compression ignition engines are heavier but more fuel-efficient and can burn cheaper fuels (such as jet fuel/AVTUR). They are rare in light aviation but are becoming more common in some modern aircraft.
exam_tip: Spark ignition = spark plug ignites the mixture (standard in light aircraft). Compression ignition = fuel ignites from compression heat alone (diesel cycle). No spark plug needed for compression ignition.
key_facts: Spark ignition: spark plug ignites fuel/air mixture at timed moment
Compression ignition: fuel self-ignites from heat of compression
No spark plug in compression ignition (diesel) engines
Compression ignition: heavier but more fuel-efficient
Compression ignition can use jet fuel (AVTUR)
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (ignition types), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.10.12

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of carburation.
concept: Carburation is the process of mixing fuel with air in the correct proportions before it enters the engine cylinders. The carburettor meters fuel into the incoming airstream using the pressure drop created by a venturi (a narrowing in the air passage). As air flows through the venturi, it accelerates and static pressure drops, drawing fuel from a float chamber through a discharge nozzle into the airstream. The pilot controls the fuel-to-air ratio using the mixture control.
exam_tip: Carburation = mixing fuel and air in the right proportions. The venturi creates a pressure drop that draws fuel into the airstream. The pilot fine-tunes with the mixture control.
key_facts: Carburation: mixing fuel with air in correct proportions
Venturi creates pressure drop to draw fuel from float chamber
Fuel enters via discharge nozzle into airstream
Pilot adjusts fuel/air ratio with mixture control
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (carburation principle), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.12.2

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic principle of operation of a simple float-type carburettor.
concept: A float-type carburettor maintains a constant fuel level in a float chamber using a float connected to a needle valve. As fuel is consumed, the float drops, opening the needle valve to admit more fuel. As the fuel level rises, the float rises and closes the valve. Air enters through the air intake, passes through the venturi where it accelerates and pressure drops, drawing fuel from the float chamber through the main jet and discharge nozzle.
