By switching radios off before starting, the battery power is reserved for the starter motor and engine starting systems. Once the engine is running and the alternator has stabilised its output (at cruise RPM), radios can be turned back on. This practice also reduces overall electrical load during the startup transient period.
exam_tip: Start with radios off: starter motor draws huge current, causing voltage dips. This prevents radio interference and damage to avionics during startup.
key_facts: Starter motor draws very large current (several hundred amperes)
Large current causes significant voltage drop in system
Radio operation during voltage dip causes interference
Can damage sensitive avionics during startup transient
Turn radios on after engine stable at cruise RPM
Reserves battery power for starter and essential starting systems
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Starting procedures), POH, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.26.6

syllabus_desc: Explain the precautions to take during normal operation of the electrical system, including: Avoiding prolonged operation of the starter motor
concept: The starter motor is designed for short bursts (10-15 seconds per attempt) of high current draw. Prolonged starter operation causes the motor to overheat, potentially burning out the windings and rendering the starter inoperable. Additionally, prolonged cranking may flood the engine if the engine does not fire (fuel continues to be drawn into cylinders without combustion). If the engine does not start within 15 seconds, the pilot should stop cranking, pause for 30 seconds to allow the starter to cool, and attempt again. Excessive starter use can also flatten the battery quickly, leaving insufficient electrical power for subsequent restart attempts.
exam_tip: Limit starter motor to 10-15 seconds per attempt. Prolonged use causes overheating, flooding, and battery depletion. Stop, cool, and retry if needed.
key_facts: Starter motor rated for short bursts only (~10-15 seconds)
Prolonged operation causes overheating and burnout
Extended cranking floods engine (unburned fuel accumulates)
Excessive use depletes battery charge
If no start: stop, wait 30 seconds to cool, retry
Never attempt continuous cranking for >30 seconds
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Starting procedures), POH, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.26.6

syllabus_desc: Explain the precautions to take during normal operation of the electrical system, including: Releasing the starter once the engine is running
concept: Once the engine starts and begins firing, the starter motor must be released (ignition switch returned to normal RUN position or starter switch released) immediately. If the starter remains engaged while the engine is running, the engine can backfire and drive the starter motor backward (causing the pinion gear to grind against the starter ring gear on the flywheel). This rapidly damages both the starter motor and the engine's flywheel ring gear. Backdriving the starter can also create dangerous vibration and mechanical damage. Modern starters have a one-way overrunning clutch that prevents the starter motor from being driven backward by the engine, but the clutch can be damaged by extended contact after engine start. Good practice is to release the starter immediately when engine ignition is confirmed (engine catches and begins self-sustaining combustion).
exam_tip: Release starter immediately when engine fires. Prolonged engagement after start causes backdriving, grinding, and damage to starter and ring gear.
key_facts: Release starter immediately when engine catches
If starter remains engaged, engine can backfire
Backfire drives starter backward (backdriving)
Causes grinding of pinion gear on ring gear
Rapidly damages starter motor and flywheel ring gear
Can create dangerous vibration and mechanical damage
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Starting procedures), POH, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.26.6

syllabus_desc: Explain the precautions to take during normal operation of the electrical system, including: Checking satisfactory operation of the system after start, and monitoring during flight
concept: After engine start, the pilot must verify that the electrical system is functioning correctly by checking: (1) Ammeter reading (should show positive charging current), (2) Voltmeter reading (should be in green arc, typically 24-28V for 24V system or 12-15V for 12V system), (3) No illumination of alternator warning light or OV light (if installed), and (4) All essential instruments have stable power. During flight, the ammeter and voltmeter must be monitored continuously. A negative ammeter reading during cruise (battery discharging) indicates alternator failure; in this case, the pilot should reduce electrical load and divert to nearest aerodrome. A voltmeter reading outside the green arc or an illuminated OV light also indicates a problem. Continuous monitoring of electrical instruments ensures early detection of system failure before critical power is lost.
exam_tip: After start: check ammeter (positive charge), voltmeter (green arc), warning lights off, instruments stable. Monitor continuously in flight for early failure detection.
key_facts: Check ammeter after start: should show positive charging
Check voltmeter: should be in green arc (24-28V or 12-15V)
Verify no alternator warning light or OV light illuminated
Confirm all essential instruments have stable power
Monitor ammeter and voltmeter continuously in flight
Negative ammeter in cruise = alternator failure; reduce load, divert
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (In-flight monitoring), POH, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.26.6

syllabus_desc: Explain the precautions to take during normal operation of the electrical system, including: Switching off ancillary equipment before shut-down
concept: Before engine shut-down, non-essential ancillary systems (radio, avionics, lights) should be switched off to prevent the battery from supplying unnecessary current to these systems while the alternator is no longer generating power. As engine RPM decays during shut-down, the alternator output decreases and eventually stops when RPM falls below the minimum generation threshold (~500-1000 RPM). If ancillary systems remain on during this transition, the battery must supply all power, causing a rapid discharge. Switching these systems off before reducing engine RPM to idle ensures the battery is not unnecessarily drained, preserving reserve charge for the final stages of shut-down (eg magneto and master switch operations). This practice extends battery life and ensures sufficient charge remains for future engine starts.
exam_tip: Before shutdown: switch off radios, lights, and ancillary systems. Prevents battery drain as alternator output decreases during engine deceleration.
key_facts: Switch off radio before reducing RPM
Switch off lights and non-essential systems
Alternator output decreases as engine RPM decreases
Generator stops when RPM falls below ~500-1000
Battery must supply all power after generator stops
Prevents unnecessary battery discharge during shutdown
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Engine shutdown), POH, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.26.6

syllabus_desc: Explain the precautions to take during normal operation of the electrical system, including: Switching the battery master switch off before leaving the aircraft
concept: The battery master switch should be turned off before leaving the aircraft to prevent the battery from powering any electrical systems while the aircraft is unattended. Even with the engine off and ignition switch off, some electrical circuits may have residual power or parasitic drain (clock, avionics with backup batteries, beacon lights). Leaving the master switch on allows the battery to continue discharging, slowly draining it over hours or days. A fully discharged battery may prevent engine start on the next flight and can suffer permanent damage (lead-acid batteries left in a discharged state develop lead sulphate crystal buildup that reduces future capacity). Turning off the master switch ensures the aircraft electrical system is completely de-energised and the battery is protected from parasitic drain. This is standard procedure in all aircraft.
exam_tip: Before leaving aircraft: master switch OFF. Prevents parasitic drain and battery depletion. Protects battery from damage due to discharged state.
key_facts: Battery master switch OFF before exiting aircraft
Prevents parasitic drain (clocks, beacon lights, avionics)
Ensures complete de-energisation of electrical system
Battery discharge over time damages lead-acid batteries
Discharged battery may not start engine next flight
Protects battery from lead sulphate crystal buildup
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Post-flight procedures), POH, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.26.6

syllabus_desc: Identify the cockpit indications of the following electrical system malfunctions: Excessive alternator/generator charge rate
concept: Excessive charge rate occurs when the voltage regulator fails and allows the alternator to output voltage higher than the normal regulated value (typically >30V in a 24V system or >15V in a 12V system). Symptoms include: (1) Voltmeter reading in the red arc (above normal), (2) Ammeter showing abnormally high positive (charging) current, (3) Overvoltage warning light (OV light) illuminated if installed, (4) Possible smell of burning or overheating coming from the electrical system or battery, (5) Battery may appear to be boiling or steaming (in lead-acid batteries with removable caps). Excessive voltage damages electrical components, boils battery electrolyte, and can cause component failure or even fire. If excessive charge is detected in flight, the pilot should reduce electrical load immediately (switch off non-essential systems) and land as soon as practicable to diagnose the voltage regulator.
exam_tip: Excessive charge: voltmeter in red arc, high charge current, OV light on, possible burning smell. Voltage regulator failure. Reduce load, land immediately.
key_facts: Voltage regulator failure causes excessive output
Voltmeter reading above normal (>30V for 24V system)
Ammeter shows abnormally high positive current
OV warning light illuminated
Possible burning smell from electrical system
Battery may appear to boil or steam
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (Electrical system faults), POH troubleshooting, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.26.8

syllabus_desc: Identify the cockpit indications of the following electrical system malfunctions: Lack of alternator/generator charge
concept: Lack of charge (or low charge rate) occurs when the alternator is not generating sufficient electrical power or the voltage regulator is set too low. Symptoms include: (1) Ammeter showing zero or negative (discharge) current during cruise at normal RPM, (2) Voltmeter reading below normal (below 24V in a 24V system or below 12V in a 12V system), (3) Alternator warning light illuminated (if installed), (4) Avionics or instruments operating dimly or unstably due to low voltage, (5) Battery becoming gradually discharged (ammeter persistently negative). Causes include: alternator failure (bearing failure, internal winding damage), broken alternator belt, loose electrical connection, or voltage regulator failure (output set too low). In flight, if lack of charge is detected, the pilot should reduce electrical load (turn off non-essential avionics, lights) and divert to nearest suitable aerodrome. Continued flight with low charge will eventually deplete the battery, leaving insufficient power for essential systems.
exam_tip: Lack of charge: ammeter zero or negative, low voltmeter reading, alternator light on. Battery gradually discharges. Reduce load, divert to nearest aerodrome.
key_facts: Ammeter shows zero or negative current at cruise RPM
Voltmeter reading below normal (e.g., <24V in 24V system)
Alternator warning light illuminated
Avionics and instruments dim or unstable
Battery gradually discharges
Causes: alternator failure, broken belt, loose connection, or regulator failure
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (Electrical system faults), POH troubleshooting, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.26.8

syllabus_desc: Identify the cockpit indications of the following electrical system malfunctions: Blown fuse or popped circuit breaker
concept: A blown fuse or popped circuit breaker indicates overcurrent in the protected circuit, caused by a short circuit, component failure, or excessive load. Symptoms include: (1) One or more specific electrical systems inoperative (e.g., landing gear, flaps, fuel pump, lights, avionics), (2) Cockpit circuit breaker popped out (visible reset button or switch in OFF position), (3) If fuse, it will be visibly dark or broken filament inside fuse barrel. Blown fuses typically occur due to: short circuits in wiring, failed component drawing excessive current, or water/corrosion in electrical connectors. If a circuit breaker pops during flight, the pilot may attempt to reset it once; if it pops again immediately, the circuit has a fault and must remain off. If a fuse blows, it must be replaced with one of the same amperage rating (never substitute a higher-rated fuse as this defeats the protection and risks fire). If multiple fuses/breakers blow, or if resetting does not restore function, a significant electrical fault exists and the aircraft should not be flown until repaired.
exam_tip: Blown fuse or popped breaker: specific system inoperative. Reset breaker once if possible. Replace fuse with correct rating. Repeated failures = electrical fault.
key_facts: One or more systems suddenly inoperative
Circuit breaker visible in popped-out position
Fuse has visible dark or broken filament
Caused by short circuit, component failure, or overload
May attempt to reset breaker once
Replace blown fuse with correct amperage rating only
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (Electrical circuit protection), POH troubleshooting, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.26.8

syllabus_desc: State the actions available to the pilot to deal with: Excessive alternator/generator charge rate
concept: Excessive charge rate (overvoltage) indicates the voltage regulator is not maintaining constant alternator output, resulting in voltage higher than normal (>30V in 24V system). This is caused by voltage regulator failure. In flight, if overvoltage is detected (OV light illuminated or voltmeter in red arc): (1) Immediately switch off all non-essential electrical loads (radio, autopilot, lighting) to reduce demand on the electrical system. (2) Reduce alternator output load indirectly by minimising avionics use. (3) Monitor voltmeter and ammeter continuously. (4) Divert to the nearest suitable aerodrome to land and investigate. (5) Do not delay landing if overvoltage persists. Excessive voltage damages battery electrolyte, corrodes wiring insulation, and can cause component failure or electrical fire. The voltage regulator must be replaced before the aircraft flies again.
exam_tip: Overvoltage in flight: switch off non-essential loads, reduce avionics use, monitor gauges, divert to nearest aerodrome immediately.
key_facts: Voltage regulator failure causes overvoltage (>30V in 24V system)
OV light illuminated or voltmeter in red arc
Switch off non-essential loads (radio, autopilot, lights)
Reduce avionics use to lower system demand
Monitor voltmeter and ammeter continuously
Divert to nearest aerodrome without delay
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (In-flight electrical faults), POH emergency procedures, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.26.10

syllabus_desc: State the actions available to the pilot to deal with: Lack of alternator/generator charge
concept: Lack of charge (or low charge rate) during flight indicates the alternator is not generating sufficient power or the battery is discharging. Symptoms: ammeter showing negative (discharge) current at normal cruise RPM, or voltmeter reading below normal. Causes: alternator failure (bearing damage, internal short, loose belt), voltage regulator failure (output too low), or severe electrical system fault. In flight, if lack of charge is detected: (1) Immediately switch off all non-essential electrical systems (autopilot, non-essential avionics, landing lights, anti-collision light) to reduce battery load. (2) Retain only essential systems: essential flight instruments, one radio for communication, engine instruments. (3) Monitor ammeter; if it returns to positive (neutral or slight charge), the load reduction was successful. (4) If ammeter remains negative (battery still discharging), the alternator has likely failed completely. (5) Divert to nearest suitable aerodrome and land. (6) Do not attempt to reach a distant aerodrome on battery power alone; battery capacity is limited (typically 30-45 minutes of essential systems only). Proper descent and landing planning is critical.
exam_tip: No charge in flight: switch off non-essential loads, retain essentials only, monitor ammeter, divert to nearest aerodrome. Battery alone will not support extended flight.
key_facts: Ammeter shows negative (discharge) at normal cruise RPM
Voltmeter below normal (e.g., <24V in 24V system)
Switch off non-essential loads (autopilot, lights, non-essential avionics)
Retain essential systems only (instruments, radio, engine gauges)
Monitor ammeter for improvement
If negative persists, alternator has failed; divert immediately
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (In-flight electrical faults), POH emergency procedures, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.26.10

syllabus_desc: State the actions available to the pilot to deal with: Blown fuse or popped circuit breaker
concept: A blown fuse or popped circuit breaker in flight indicates overcurrent in that specific circuit, typically due to an electrical fault (short circuit) or component failure. One or more systems protected by that fuse/breaker will become inoperative. Management depends on which circuit is affected: (1) If the affected system is non-essential (landing lights, anti-collision light, trim system), the flight may continue to the nearest suitable aerodrome with caution. (2) If the affected system is essential (fuel pump, engine instrument power, essential avionics), the flight must be diverted to nearest suitable aerodrome immediately. (3) If a circuit breaker pops, the pilot may attempt ONE reset attempt only: if the breaker pops again immediately, it indicates a fault that requires maintenance. Do not repeatedly reset the same breaker. (4) If a fuse is blown and a spare is available, the pilot may replace it with one of identical amperage rating (carrying replacement fuses is good practice); if the replacement immediately blows, do not replace it again. (5) Upon landing, investigate the cause and repair before the next flight. Multiple blown fuses/breakers indicate a serious electrical fault requiring maintenance.
exam_tip: Fuse/breaker blow in flight: assess if system is essential. Reset breaker max once. Replace fuse with identical rating only. Land for investigation.
key_facts: One specific system becomes inoperative
Non-essential systems: flight continues to nearest aerodrome
Essential systems: immediate diversion required
Breaker: attempt ONE reset only
If breaker pops again: do not reset further
Fuse: replace with identical amperage rating only
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (In-flight electrical faults), POH emergency procedures, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.26.10

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of the following components of a simple fuel system: Fuel selector valve, supply line, strainer and strainer drain
concept: Fuel selector valve: allows the pilot to select which fuel tank supplies the engine (left, right, or both in multi-tank aircraft). Fuel supply line: carries fuel from the tank through the selector valve to the engine fuel system. Fuel strainer (also called fuel filter or fuel sump): located in the fuel system between the tank and the carburetor/fuel injection system, removes debris, water, and particulates from the fuel before it reaches the engine. The strainer has a drain plug (strainer drain) at the lowest point that allows the pilot to drain sediment and water accumulated in the strainer during preflight inspection. Draining the fuel strainer before flight is a critical preflight check that may reveal water or contamination in the fuel tanks. All three components are essential to prevent contaminated fuel from reaching the engine and causing blockages or damage.
exam_tip: Fuel selector: selects tank. Supply line: carries fuel. Strainer: filters debris and water. Strainer drain: allows pilot to check for contamination preflight.
key_facts: Fuel selector valve: allows pilot to select fuel tank source
Fuel supply line: carries fuel from tank to engine
Fuel strainer: removes debris, water, particulates
Located between tank and carburetor/injection system
Strainer drain: lowest point, allows sediment/water drainage
Drain check is critical preflight action
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3 (Fuel system components), POH preflight procedures, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.28.2

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of the following components of a simple fuel system: Fuel primer, engine-driven pump, auxiliary (boost) pumps
concept: Fuel primer: a hand-operated pump (with a plunger or T-handle) that allows the pilot to inject fuel directly into engine cylinders before cranking, ensuring fuel is present for initial combustion during cold starts. Engine-driven pump: a mechanical pump (usually gear-driven from the engine crankshaft) that automatically circulates fuel from the tank through the strainer and on to the carburetor or fuel injectors. The engine-driven pump operates only when the engine is running and provides adequate fuel flow for all flight regimes. Auxiliary pump (or boost pump): an electric motor-driven fuel pump installed in or near the fuel tank that supplements the engine-driven pump during high-demand conditions (high-altitude flight, lean-mixture conditions) or provides fuel circulation when the engine is not running (e.g., before start to prime the system). Many aircraft have a boost pump that is switched on for takeoff, climb, and landing to ensure adequate fuel pressure, then switched off during cruise. In fuel-injected engines, the boost pump is often mandatory for proper engine-driven pump operation.
exam_tip: Primer: hand-operated, injects fuel for cold start. Engine pump: mechanical, circulates fuel when running. Boost pump: electric, supplements pressure for high-demand/non-running conditions.
key_facts: Fuel primer: hand-operated, injects fuel to cylinders before start
Engine-driven pump: mechanical, automatic when engine running
Provides fuel flow for all flight regimes
Auxiliary (boost) pump: electric motor-driven
Supplements pressure during high-demand conditions
Often mandatory for fuel-injected engines; required for takeoff/climb
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3 (Fuel pumps), POH normal procedures, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.28.2

syllabus_desc: Describe the correct management of the fuel system, including fuel selection and handling of priming and auxiliary pumps.
concept: Correct fuel management includes: verifying fuel quantity and quality during preflight (drain and check samples from each tank sump and the fuel strainer); selecting the correct fuel tank before takeoff; switching between tanks periodically during flight to maintain lateral balance (prevent one wing becoming heavier); monitoring fuel quantity gauges and comparing with planned fuel burn; using the auxiliary/boost pump during takeoff and landing on aircraft so equipped; never selecting an empty tank in flight; and verifying the fuel selector is on the correct tank before landing.
exam_tip: Switch tanks periodically for lateral balance. Never select an empty tank. Use boost pump during critical phases. Always verify fuel before flight.
key_facts: Drain and check fuel samples during preflight
Select correct tank before takeoff
Switch tanks periodically for lateral balance
Monitor fuel gauges vs planned fuel burn
Use boost pump during takeoff and landing
Never select an empty tank in flight
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (fuel management), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.28.4

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of the following components of a simple fuel system: Fuel tank, sump, drain point, supply line standpipe, vents, overflow drain
concept: Fuel tank: the main storage vessel for aircraft fuel, constructed of aluminium alloy or composite materials designed to withstand pressure and vibration. Sump: a low point in the fuel tank or fuel system where water and sediment collect due to gravity. Drain point (or sump drain): a valve at the lowest point allowing the pilot to drain water and sediment from the tank during preflight. Standpipe: a tube inside the fuel tank extending from the bottom toward the top, with its intake at a point above the lowest region, ensuring that some fuel always remains accessible (preventing the fuel pump from drawing sediment and water from the sump when the tank is nearly empty). Vents: allow air to enter the tank as fuel is consumed, preventing vacuum that would collapse the tank or stop fuel flow. Overflow drain: allows excess fuel to exit the tank safely during fueling or in flight if fuel expands due to temperature increase, preventing tank overpressurisation. All components work together to ensure clean, continuous fuel supply and prevent tank damage.
exam_tip: Tank stores fuel. Sump collects sediment. Drain allows preflight check for water. Standpipe ensures clean fuel access. Vents prevent vacuum. Overflow prevents overpressure.
key_facts: Fuel tank: main storage, aluminium/composite construction
Sump: low point where water and sediment collect
Drain point: valve for preflight sediment/water check
Standpipe: tube extending into tank, draws fuel above sump
Vents: allow air entry as fuel consumed (prevent vacuum)
Overflow drain: safely releases excess fuel or pressure
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3 (Fuel tank design), POH preflight procedures, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.30.2

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of the following components of a simple fuel system: Fuel quantity indicators
concept: Fuel quantity indicators are cockpit instruments that display the amount of fuel remaining in the fuel tanks. Two common types: (1) Electrical capacitive gauges: measure fuel quantity by sensing the change in electrical capacitance as fuel level changes in the tank; output is displayed on a cockpit gauge (usually with a needle that moves across a scale marked in gallons or litres). (2) Float-type gauges: a float arm in the tank is connected mechanically to a gauge needle via a wire or cable; as fuel level changes, the float moves and the needle indicates quantity. Fuel quantity gauges are prone to fluctuation or inaccuracy, especially in turbulent flight when fuel sloshes and changes the apparent level. For this reason, fuel quantity gauges are supplemental; the primary method for confirming adequate fuel is visual inspection of the fuel tanks (looking into the filler access) during preflight. Pilot technique requires cross-checking the gauge with flight time and fuel flow to ensure accurate understanding of fuel remaining. Gauges should be checked before takeoff and monitored throughout flight to ensure fuel is available for all planned operations and reserves.
exam_tip: Quantity indicators: electrical (capacitive) or float-type gauges in cockpit. Gauges may fluctuate; visual preflight check and fuel flow monitoring are primary methods.
key_facts: Electrical capacitive gauges: sense capacitance change with fuel level
Float-type gauges: float arm mechanically linked to needle
Displayed in cockpit on gauge face (gallons or litres)
Prone to fluctuation in turbulent flight (fuel sloshes)
Not primary fuel check; visual inspection is primary
Cross-check with flight time and fuel flow throughout flight
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3 (Fuel quantity measurement), POH normal procedures, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.30.2

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of the following components of a simple fuel system: Fuel tank construction and associated limitations
concept: Fuel tanks in light aircraft are typically constructed from welded aluminium alloy (5000 or 6000 series) or composite materials (fiberglass or epoxy resin). Aluminium tanks are light, durable, and easy to repair. Composite tanks are corrosion-resistant and lighter. Tank construction must meet structural safety standards to withstand vibration, aerodynamic loads, and pressurisation (from boost pump or venting). Limitations: (1) Fuel tanks cannot withstand prolonged exposure to certain fuels (e.g., some unleaded 100LL variants can attack older seals or composite resins over time). (2) Water absorption: composite tanks may absorb small amounts of water if not properly sealed; regular draining is essential. (3) Temperature sensitivity: fuel expands and contracts with temperature changes, hence overflow drain is necessary. (4) Pressure limits: tanks are designed for specific maximum pressures; overloading with excess fuel or using incorrect fuel pump settings can exceed these limits and crack the tank. (5) Corrosion: aluminium tanks can corrode internally if water is not drained regularly; corrosion products (rust) can contaminate fuel and block filters. (6) Quantity gauge errors: tank shape affects gauge accuracy; tanks with irregular shapes show non-linear fuel quantity indication. Pilots must respect tank limitations by using correct fuel types, maintaining seals, draining regularly, and avoiding overfilling.
exam_tip: Aluminium or composite construction. Limitations: water absorption, temperature expansion, pressure limits, corrosion. Proper maintenance and fuel type essential.
key_facts: Aluminium alloy: light, durable, easy to repair but corrodes
Composite: corrosion-resistant but absorbs water if not sealed
Water drainage essential to prevent corrosion and contamination
Fuel expands/contracts with temperature; overflow prevents overpressure
Tank pressure limits must be respected
Correct fuel type required; some fuels attack seals or resins
Tank shape affects fuel quantity gauge linearity
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.3 (Fuel tank design and limitations), POH fuel specifications, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.30.2

syllabus_desc: Describe the procedure to be used for a fuel drain check.
concept: Before every flight, draw a fuel sample from the lowest point of each fuel tank (sump drain) and from the fuel strainer using a clear sampling cup. Check that the fuel is the correct colour (blue for 100LL), is clear or only slightly hazy, and is free from visible water droplets, sediment, or other contamination. Water is denser than fuel and collects at the bottom  -  it appears as clear droplets or a separate layer. If contamination is found, continue draining until the sample is clean. If contamination persists, the aircraft is unserviceable.
exam_tip: Check colour, clarity, and contamination. Water sits at the bottom  -  blue fuel with clear droplets underneath = water. Keep draining until clean.
key_facts: Draw samples from each tank sump and fuel strainer
Use clear sampling cup
Check correct colour (blue = AVGAS 100LL)
Look for water droplets (clear, separate layer at bottom)
Check for sediment or discolouration
Continue draining until clean; persistent = unserviceable
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (fuel drain), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.30.4

syllabus_desc: State the general rules for fuelling of aircraft, including the special precautions for the use of drum stock, and plastic containers.
concept: Use only the fuel type specified in the aircraft flight manual. Verify fuel type and colour before and during refuelling. Ensure the aircraft is properly earthed (grounded) during refuelling to prevent static discharge. Do not refuel with passengers on board. When using fuel from drums, use proper filtering equipment and allow fuel to settle before use. Avoid refuelling from plastic containers  -  static buildup in plastic cannot be discharged safely. Fill tanks after the last flight of the day to minimise condensation overnight.
exam_tip: Use correct fuel type. Earth the aircraft. No passengers during refuelling. No plastic containers. Fill tanks after flying to prevent condensation.
key_facts: Use only the specified fuel type
Verify fuel type and colour during refuelling
Earth/ground the aircraft during refuelling
No passengers on board during refuelling
Use proper filtering from drum stock
Avoid plastic containers (static risk)
Fill tanks after last flight to prevent condensation
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (fuelling rules), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.30.6

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of aircraft earthing during refuelling.
concept: During refuelling, fuel flowing through hoses and nozzles generates static electricity. If this static charge is not safely dissipated, a spark could ignite fuel vapour, causing a fire or explosion. The aircraft must be electrically bonded (connected) to the refuelling equipment and to an earth point before fuel begins to flow. This ensures any static charge is safely conducted to ground. The bonding wire should be connected before the fuel cap is removed and should remain connected until the cap is replaced and refuelling is complete.
exam_tip: Static electricity from fuel flow can cause a spark → fire. The bonding wire must be connected BEFORE the fuel cap is opened and stay connected until refuelling is complete.
key_facts: Fuel flow generates static electricity
Static spark can ignite fuel vapour → fire/explosion
Aircraft must be bonded to refuelling equipment and earth
Connect bonding wire BEFORE opening fuel cap
Keep connected until cap replaced and refuelling complete
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (earthing), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.30.8

syllabus_desc: State the functions of an engine lubrication system.
concept: Engine oil serves five primary functions: (1) Lubrication  -  reduces friction between moving parts (bearings, pistons, camshaft); (2) Cooling  -  carries heat away from hot engine components to the oil cooler; (3) Sealing  -  helps piston rings seal against cylinder walls; (4) Corrosion protection  -  coats metal surfaces to prevent rust and chemical attack; (5) Cleaning/debris removal  -  carries wear particles and combustion by-products to the oil filter.
exam_tip: Five functions: lubricate, cool, seal, protect from corrosion, and clean. The cooling function is often underestimated  -  oil removes a significant amount of engine heat.
key_facts: Lubrication: reduces friction between moving parts
Cooling: carries heat to oil cooler
Sealing: helps piston rings seal in cylinders
Corrosion protection: coats metal surfaces
Cleaning: carries wear debris to oil filter
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (lubrication functions), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.32.2

syllabus_desc: Explain the term viscosity.
concept: Viscosity is a measure of an oil's resistance to flow  -  its 'thickness'. High viscosity oil is thick and flows slowly; low viscosity oil is thin and flows easily. Oil viscosity changes with temperature: cold oil is thick (high viscosity), hot oil is thin (low viscosity). Multi-grade oils (e.g. 15W-50) are designed to maintain acceptable viscosity across a wide temperature range  -  the 'W' number indicates cold viscosity and the second number indicates hot viscosity.
exam_tip: Viscosity = how thick or thin the oil is. Cold oil is thick; hot oil is thin. Multi-grade oils work across a wider temperature range.
key_facts: Viscosity: measure of oil's resistance to flow (thickness)
High viscosity = thick, flows slowly
Low viscosity = thin, flows easily
Viscosity decreases as temperature increases
Multi-grade oils maintain viscosity across wider temp range
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (viscosity), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.32.4

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of temperature on the lubricating qualities of oil.
concept: As temperature increases, oil becomes thinner (lower viscosity) and may not maintain an adequate lubricating film between moving parts, increasing wear and the risk of metal-to-metal contact. Conversely, when oil is very cold, it becomes too thick to flow freely, which can prevent adequate lubrication during cold starts and may cause high oil pressure readings. The oil cooler maintains oil within the correct temperature range. Correct oil grade selection for the operating temperature range is essential.
exam_tip: Hot oil gets thin (may not lubricate properly). Cold oil gets thick (may not flow to where it's needed). The oil cooler keeps oil in the correct temperature range.
key_facts: Hot oil: lower viscosity, may not maintain adequate film
Cold oil: higher viscosity, may not flow freely
Oil cooler maintains correct temperature range
Correct oil grade for operating temperature is essential
Cold starts: oil may be too thick for adequate lubrication
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (temperature effects on oil), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.32.6

syllabus_desc: Briefly describe the function of the following components of an oil system: Wet sump
concept: Wet sump system: the engine oil reservoir is located at the base of the engine (integrated into the engine block). Oil drains by gravity from all engine components back to the sump. Most common in light aircraft piston engines due to simplicity, light weight, and reliability. Oil is drawn from the wet sump by the engine-driven pump. Advantages: simple design, low weight, easy maintenance. Disadvantages: limited sump capacity means some aerobatic maneuvers (negative-G flight) can cause oil starvation if sump pickup draws air. Dry sump system: the oil reservoir is a separate external tank (not part of the engine). A scavenge pump actively returns oil from the engine back to the external tank. The engine pump then draws oil from the external tank. Dry sump is more complex but allows: larger oil capacity, consistent oil supply during negative-G maneuvers, and better engine cooling (larger external tank improves heat dissipation). Dry sump is typically found in high-performance or aerobatic aircraft. Light aircraft trainers and touring aircraft almost always use wet sump.
exam_tip: Wet sump: oil stored in engine base, simpler, lighter. Dry sump: separate external tank with scavenge pump, more capacity, better for aerobatic flight.
key_facts: Wet sump: oil reservoir in engine base (most common)
Oil drains by gravity back to sump
Simple design, light weight, easy maintenance
Limited capacity; negative-G can cause starvation
Dry sump: separate external oil tank
Scavenge pump returns oil from engine to tank
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (Oil system design), ATC Aircraft General Knowledge, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.32.8

syllabus_desc: Briefly describe the function of the following components of an oil system: Dry sump, scavenge pump, tank
concept: In a dry sump system, the scavenge pump is a secondary mechanical pump (usually gear-driven from the crankshaft) that continuously removes oil from the engine galleries, oil passages, and crankcase and returns it to the external oil tank (reservoir). The scavenge pump ensures that oil does not accumulate in the engine during operation (which would increase drag and heat). The external oil tank is a separate vessel (often mounted on the aircraft fuselage) that stores the oil between scavenge returns and engine-pump draws. The tank is larger than a wet sump, providing greater heat dissipation surface area, which helps cool the oil during high-power flight. The tank has internal baffles to separate returned (hot) oil from supply oil, improving cooling efficiency. Dry sump systems are preferred in high-performance and aerobatic aircraft because they provide consistent oil supply during negative-G maneuvers and allow larger oil quantities for better cooling.
exam_tip: Scavenge pump: removes oil from engine, returns to external tank. External tank: larger capacity, better cooling via larger surface area and internal baffles.
key_facts: Scavenge pump: gear-driven, removes oil from engine
Returns oil to external tank (reservoir)
Prevents oil accumulation in engine (reduces drag and heat)
External tank: separate vessel, larger capacity than wet sump
Improved heat dissipation via larger surface area
Internal baffles separate hot and supply oil for cooling
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (Dry sump design), ATC Aircraft General Knowledge, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.32.8

syllabus_desc: Briefly describe the function of the following components of an oil system: Engine-driven pump, pressure relief valve
concept: Engine-driven pump: a mechanical pump (typically gear-driven from the engine crankshaft) that circulates oil under pressure to all engine bearings, moving parts, and galleries. The pump draws oil from the sump (wet sump system) or external tank (dry sump) and forces it through oil passages at high pressure (typically 40-100 psi depending on engine and oil viscosity). Higher RPM increases pump output pressure; at idle the pressure is lower, at high power the pressure is higher. Pressure relief valve: a spring-loaded valve in the oil system that opens if pressure exceeds a safe limit (typically 100 psi for typical light aircraft engines), allowing excess oil to bypass the system or return to the sump. This prevents dangerously high pressure that could rupture oil lines or damage seals. The relief valve is set to open at a specific pressure and maintains system pressure within the safe operating range. Both components work together: the pump provides adequate pressure for lubrication, and the relief valve protects the system from overpressure.
exam_tip: Engine pump: gear-driven, circulates oil under pressure (40-100 psi). Relief valve: opens if pressure exceeds limit, protects system from overpressure.
key_facts: Engine-driven pump: mechanical, gear-driven from crankshaft
Circulates oil under pressure to all bearings and moving parts
Pressure range: 40-100 psi (engine and oil-dependent)
Higher RPM = higher pressure
Pressure relief valve: spring-loaded, prevents overpressure
Opens at specific pressure (typically ~100 psi)
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (Oil circulation), ATC Aircraft General Knowledge, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.32.8

syllabus_desc: Briefly describe the function of the following components of an oil system: Oil lines, passages and galleries
concept: Oil lines: external tubes or hoses that carry pressurised oil from the pump to the oil cooler, filter, and other components. Lines are constructed from steel, aluminium, or flexible hose with crimped fittings. Oil lines must be pressure-rated to handle pump output pressure without rupturing. Passages: internal routes within the engine block through which oil flows under pressure. These are drilled passages inside the engine casting that connect bearing journals, main galleries, and component drain points. Galleries: large internal channels in the engine block that distribute oil to multiple bearings and moving parts. The main gallery runs the length of the engine along the crankshaft, supplying oil to all main bearings and connecting rod bearings via cross-drilled passages. Branch galleries supply oil to valve train components (cam bearings, rocker arms), magnetos, fuel injection nozzles, and other accessories. All three elements (external lines, internal passages, galleries) must be clean and unobstructed to ensure adequate oil distribution. Blockages (from contamination, sludge, or debris) reduce pressure and can cause localized oil starvation, leading to bearing damage.
exam_tip: Oil lines: external tubes carrying pressurised oil. Passages: internal routes in engine. Galleries: large channels distributing oil to bearings. All must be clean; blockages cause starvation.
key_facts: Oil lines: external tubes, steel/aluminium/hose construction
Carry pressurised oil to cooler, filter, components
Must be pressure-rated to pump output
Passages: internal drilled routes in engine block
Galleries: large channels in engine distributing oil
Main gallery runs along crankshaft to main bearings
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (Oil distribution system), ATC Aircraft General Knowledge, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.32.8

syllabus_desc: Briefly describe the function of the following components of an oil system: Oil cooler, bypass valves and filters
concept: Oil cooler: an air-to-oil heat exchanger that cools engine oil by passing air over the cooler's external fins (typically mounted in the engine cowling where airflow is available). As the aircraft flies, ram air flows over the cooler, removing heat from the oil circulating through it. The cooler maintains oil temperature within the green arc (typically 50-100°C). If oil temperature rises above normal, the cooler has insufficient capacity or airflow. Bypass valve: a relief valve in the oil cooler or filter circuit that opens if the cooler or filter becomes restricted (blockage, cold viscous oil in winter), allowing oil to flow around the obstruction and reach engine bearings. The bypass ensures that the engine receives oil even if the cooler is blocked or filter is clogged. Filters: remove contaminants (wear debris, sludge) from the oil to maintain oil cleanliness and protect bearings. Spin-on or cartridge filters with a bypass valve (internal to the filter element) trap particles while allowing oil flow. During cold starts, cold oil is viscous and flows slowly; the filter bypass valve opens to allow cold oil to bypass the filter media, ensuring the engine receives lubrication. As the oil warms, viscosity decreases and the bypass closes, filtering all oil.
exam_tip: Oil cooler: removes heat to maintain normal oil temperature. Bypass valve: opens if cooler/filter blocked, ensures oil reaches bearings. Filter: removes contaminants; bypass allows cold oil flow.
key_facts: Oil cooler: air-to-oil heat exchanger, cools via ram air
Maintains oil temperature in green arc (50-100°C)
Bypass valve: allows flow around obstruction
Opens if cooler or filter restricted (blockage, cold viscous oil)
Filter: removes wear debris and sludge
Filter bypass allows cold oil flow on cold starts
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (Oil cooling and filtration), POH, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.32.8

syllabus_desc: Briefly describe the function of the following components of an oil system: Oil pressure and temperature gauges
concept: Oil pressure gauge: a cockpit instrument that displays the pressure of oil in the main engine oil gallery (typically in psi). The gauge is supplied by a pressure line from the engine oil system. Normal operating pressure (green arc) is typically 40-100 psi depending on engine type and oil viscosity. At startup, oil pressure should rise within 15-30 seconds; zero or low pressure indicates inadequate lubrication and the engine must be stopped immediately. During flight, oil pressure increases with engine RPM and decreases with increasing oil temperature (warmer oil is less viscous). Oil temperature gauge: displays the temperature of engine oil in degrees Celsius or Fahrenheit. Normal operating temperature (green arc) is typically 50-100°C (120-210°F). High oil temperature indicates insufficient cooling (cooler blockage or excessive power), and the pilot should reduce power and/or open cowl flaps to increase airflow. Low oil temperature (during initial start and cold-weather operation) is normal; oil warms as the engine runs. Both gauges are essential for monitoring engine health; abnormal readings indicate problems requiring investigation and potential power reduction or landing.
exam_tip: Oil pressure gauge: displays pressure (40-100 psi normal), must rise within 15-30 sec at start. Oil temperature gauge: displays temperature (50-100°C normal). Both essential for engine health monitoring.
key_facts: Oil pressure gauge: displays main gallery pressure (psi)
Normal (green arc): 40-100 psi (engine-dependent)
Must rise within 15-30 seconds of start
Zero pressure at start: STOP engine immediately
Oil temperature gauge: displays oil temperature (°C or °F)
Normal: 50-100°C; high temp indicates cooling problem
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Engine monitoring instruments), POH, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.32.8

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of: Using the correct type and grade of oil for a particular aircraft
concept: Aircraft engines use mineral oil (ashless dispersant, AD oil) or synthetic oil specifically formulated for aircraft use. The most common is AD oil, approved under specifications such as SAE 50, 40, or 30/50 depending on aircraft and operating conditions. SAE grades indicate viscosity: SAE 50 is heavier (thicker) and used in warm climates or high-power engines; SAE 40 is medium; SAE 30/50 is multigrade (flows easily when cold, maintains viscosity when hot). Typical aircraft use Aeroshell 15W-50, Aeroshell 50, Mobil 80, or equivalent aircraft-grade mineral oil. Synthetic oils (such as Exxon Elite or equivalent) are approved for some modern engines but must be specifically approved for the aircraft engine (some engines have mineral-only restrictions). Never use automotive motor oil in aircraft engines - automotive oil has detergents and additives that form sludge in aircraft engines and cause damage. Using the correct oil grade is critical for proper engine lubrication, cooling, and longevity. The POH specifies the required oil type and grade; this must be strictly observed.
exam_tip: Use aircraft-grade oil (mineral or synthetic per POH). Never automotive oil. Typical grades: SAE 50, 40, 30/50. Correct type essential for lubrication and engine life.
key_facts: Aircraft-grade mineral (ashless dispersant) or synthetic oil
Common types: Aeroshell 15W-50, Aeroshell 50, Mobil 80
SAE grades: 50 (heavy), 40 (medium), 30/50 (multigrade)
SAE grade choice depends on climate and engine
NEVER use automotive oil (forms sludge, damages engine)
POH specifies required type and grade; strictly observe
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.2 (Oil specifications), POH fuel and oil sections, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.32.10

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of: Checking the correct oil quantity before flight
concept: Oil quantity is checked during the preflight inspection using the dipstick or oil sight glass (depending on aircraft design). Procedure: (1) Ensure the aircraft is on level ground (uneven ground gives inaccurate readings). (2) Allow time after engine shutdown for oil to drain from the engine galleries back to the sump (typically 1-2 minutes); checking oil immediately after shutdown gives falsely high readings. (3) Locate the dipstick (usually on the engine) or sight glass (on the sump); refer to POH for exact location. (4) For dipstick: pull it out, wipe clean on a rag, reinsert fully, then withdraw again; read the level between the MIN and MAX marks. (5) For sight glass: observe the oil level in the sight glass; it should be between the minimum and maximum marks. (6) Oil level should be at or very close to the MAX mark (never above, as overfilling can cause seals to leak or oil to be expelled). (7) If oil is below MIN, add oil of the correct type and grade until level reaches MAX. (8) Secure the dipstick or cap after checking. Adequate oil quantity is critical; insufficient oil causes starvation and bearing damage; excessive oil can cause leaks or seal damage.
exam_tip: Preflight oil check: level ground, wait 1-2 min after shutdown, read dipstick or sight glass, must be between MIN and MAX. Top up if needed with correct oil grade.
key_facts: Ensure aircraft on level ground
Wait 1-2 minutes after shutdown for oil to drain
Locate dipstick or sight glass per POH
Dipstick: wipe, reinsert fully, remove, read level
Level must be between MIN and MAX marks
Never overfill; overfilling causes seal leaks
Add correct oil type/grade if below MIN
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Preflight checks), POH preflight section, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.32.10

syllabus_desc: Identify the possible oil system malfunctions indicated by: Low/zero oil pressure
concept: Low or zero oil pressure at startup indicates inadequate lubrication to engine bearings. Causes: (1) Insufficient oil quantity in sump (low oil level causes pump to draw air). (2) Blocked oil filter or strainer (contamination prevents oil flow). (3) Faulty oil pressure gauge (gives false reading while oil is actually flowing). (4) Oil pump failure or wear. (5) Blocked main oil gallery or galleries (sludge or debris). At startup, if oil pressure fails to rise to green arc within 15-30 seconds, stop the engine immediately. Do not advance power; do not taxi. Continued operation without adequate oil pressure causes rapid bearing wear, bearing seizure, and complete engine failure. On the ground: check oil quantity with dipstick; if oil is low, add oil and restart. If pressure still zero, investigate further. In flight: if oil pressure suddenly drops to zero, reduce power immediately, declare an emergency, and divert to nearest suitable aerodrome to land. Do not attempt to reach a distant airport. Once the aircraft is on the ground and the engine has cooled, investigate the cause (check dipstick level, check for oil leaks, have maintenance inspect the system).
exam_tip: Low/zero pressure at start: STOP engine immediately. Check oil quantity, add if low, restart. In flight: reduce power, divert to nearest aerodrome. Continued operation risks bearing failure.
key_facts: Causes: low oil level, clogged filter, pump failure, blocked galleries
At startup: pressure must rise within 15-30 seconds
Zero pressure: do not advance power, do not taxi, STOP
In flight: zero pressure = emergency
Reduce power, declare emergency, divert to nearest aerodrome
Risk of rapid bearing wear and complete engine failure
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Emergency procedures), POH emergency section, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.32.12

syllabus_desc: Identify the possible oil system malfunctions indicated by: High oil pressure
concept: High oil pressure (above green arc, typically >100 psi) indicates excessive pressure in the oil system. Causes: (1) Wrong oil viscosity (oil too thick for current conditions, typically cold-weather high-viscosity oil used in warm conditions). (2) Oil cooler blockage (cooler restricted, pressure builds up as pump tries to push oil through). (3) Oil filter blocked (partial or complete blockage of filter element). (4) Faulty oil pressure gauge (gives false reading while actual pressure is normal). (5) Pressure relief valve sticking or failed (should open and regulate pressure but stays closed). On the ground: high pressure during startup in cold weather is normal (cold oil is thick); as oil warms, pressure should decrease. If pressure remains high after 5-10 minutes of operation, investigate. In flight: if pressure rises above red line, reduce power, check for cooler blockage (visual check if possible, though difficult in flight), and prepare to land. High pressure stresses oil lines, seals, and can cause leaks or ruptures. Management: reduce power to lower RPM (reduces pump pressure), monitor temperature to ensure cooling is adequate, and land for investigation. Check oil type and grade against POH; if incorrect, change to correct grade.
exam_tip: High pressure (>green arc, typically >100 psi) in cold conditions normal initially. If persists: wrong oil grade, blocked cooler/filter, or stuck relief valve. Reduce power, monitor, land for inspection.
key_facts: Causes: wrong oil viscosity, cooler blockage, filter blockage, stuck relief valve
High pressure normal at cold start; decreases as oil warms
If pressure remains high: investigate cooler, filter
Stress on oil lines, seals, risk of rupture or leak
Reduce power to lower pressure
Check oil grade against POH; change if incorrect
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Engine monitoring), POH troubleshooting, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.32.12

syllabus_desc: Identify the possible oil system malfunctions indicated by: Fluctuating oil pressure
concept: Fluctuating oil pressure (needle oscillating up and down on the pressure gauge) indicates an unstable oil supply or measurement issue. Causes: (1) Low oil level (pump partially draws air instead of oil, causing transient pressure changes). (2) Partially blocked oil filter or strainer (intermittent blockage as pump cycles). (3) Air in the oil system (air bubbles reduce effective pressure and cause fluctuation). (4) Faulty oil pressure gauge (intermittent electrical contact or mechanical stickiness causes needle to flutter). (5) Oil pump wear (worn pump produces inconsistent pressure). (6) Relief valve chattering (relief valve opening and closing repeatedly due to pressure boundary oscillation). At startup, check oil level immediately if pressure fluctuates. If oil is low, add oil and restart. In flight, if pressure begins to fluctuate, reduce power (to lower pressure cycling rate), monitor the gauge trend, and prepare to divert. Fluctuating pressure while flying is concerning because it indicates potential air or oil starvation. Land at the nearest suitable aerodrome and investigate the cause. Do not continue flight to a distant location with fluctuating oil pressure.
exam_tip: Fluctuating pressure: check oil level first (most common cause). If low, add oil. In flight: reduce power, monitor trend, divert to nearest aerodrome. Indicates potential air in system or pump wear.
key_facts: Causes: low oil level, partial filter blockage, air in system, faulty gauge, pump wear
At startup: check oil level immediately
Add oil if low and restart
In flight: reduce power, monitor trend
Indicates potential starvation or air in system
Divert to nearest aerodrome; do not continue to distant airfield
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Engine monitoring), POH troubleshooting, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.32.12

syllabus_desc: Identify the possible oil system malfunctions indicated by: Low oil temperature
concept: Low oil temperature is a normal condition during cold-weather operation and immediately after engine start. During a cold start (winter, early morning), engine oil is very cold and viscous. As the engine runs, friction generates heat and oil temperature rises. Oil typically warms from below 40°C at startup to the green arc (50-100°C) within 5-10 minutes of idle operation. Low oil temperature at startup is expected. Management: (1) Do not advance power (increase throttle) until oil temperature has risen to at least 50°C (lower end of green arc). High power with cold, viscous oil reduces circulation and can cause bearing starvation. (2) Operate the engine at low to moderate power until oil temperature stabilises in the green arc. (3) Extended operation at low RPM or with cowl flaps fully open can keep oil temperature low; if necessary, close cowl flaps to allow warming. (4) In flight, if oil temperature remains low (below 50°C) at cruise power, it may indicate cooler bypass valve stuck open or excessive cooling. This is less common but warrants monitoring. Continuing flight with persistently low oil temperature is acceptable provided temperature is stable and not dropping further; however, monitor for any unusual engine behavior.
exam_tip: Low oil temperature normal at cold start. Do not advance power until temperature rises to green arc. Allow 5-10 min idle warmup. In flight: monitor if persistently cool.
key_facts: Low oil temperature normal at cold start
Oil warms from below 40°C to green arc (50-100°C) within 5-10 min
Do not advance power until oil reaches lower green arc (50°C)
Operate at low-moderate power during warmup
Close cowl flaps if needed to promote warming
Monitor in flight; persistently low temperature indicates cooler issue
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Warmup procedures), POH normal procedures, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.32.12

syllabus_desc: Identify the possible oil system malfunctions indicated by: High oil temperature
concept: High oil temperature (above green arc, typically >100°C or >210°F) indicates insufficient cooling of the engine oil. Causes: (1) Oil cooler blockage or restriction (debris, ice, or bugs blocking air intake). (2) Inadequate airflow over cooler (flying too slowly, cooler positioned poorly, insufficient ram air). (3) Oil filter partially blocked (reduces circulation, increases heat generation). (4) Excessive power (high-power climb or continuous high-power operation generates more heat than cooler can dissipate). (5) Wrong oil grade (too thin oil causes lower viscosity and increased friction/heat, or oil with poor cooling properties). (6) Low oil quantity (less oil means less thermal capacity to absorb heat). Management in flight: (1) Reduce engine power to lower power output and heat generation. (2) Open cowl flaps fully to increase airflow over the cooler (if aircraft has cowl flaps). (3) If possible, reduce load (lean mixture to reduce power slightly, reduce altitude if applicable). (4) Monitor oil temperature trend; if temperature continues rising, descend and prepare to land. (5) If oil temperature reaches the red arc or exceeds 105-110°C, declare an emergency, reduce power further, and divert to the nearest suitable aerodrome immediately. Continued high-temperature operation damages oil, reduces lubrication effectiveness, and can cause engine damage. Once on the ground, check oil level, inspect cooler for blockage, and have maintenance investigate.
exam_tip: High oil temperature: reduce power, open cowl flaps, lean mixture, reduce altitude. If temp rises further or enters red arc: emergency descent to nearest aerodrome.
key_facts: Causes: cooler blockage, low airflow, filter blockage, excessive power, wrong oil, low oil level
Management: reduce power, open cowl flaps, lean mixture
Monitor temperature trend continuously
If red arc or >105°C: emergency descent
Divert to nearest suitable aerodrome
High-temperature operation risks oil breakdown and engine damage
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Emergency procedures), POH emergency section, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.32.12

syllabus_desc: State the actions (if any) that the pilot can take to rectify: Low/zero oil pressure
concept: Low or zero oil pressure in flight is an emergency indicating inadequate lubrication to engine bearings. Causes: (1) Oil level depleted (leak in oil system, pump failure, or gauge malfunction). (2) Oil pump failure. (3) Main oil gallery blockage. (4) Complete alternator or electrical failure affecting oil quantity gauge. Pilot response: (1) Immediately reduce engine power to minimum (reduce power manually if necessary, do not cruise at high power). (2) Check oil temperature gauge; if temperature is rising rapidly, this indicates the engine is generating excessive heat due to friction (bearing damage occurring). (3) Monitor the ammeter and voltmeter to confirm electrical system is working; electrical failure could indicate a gauge malfunction. (4) Do not turn back toward an airfield; if the engine fails, glide distance and flight characteristics may be compromised. Proceed straight ahead to the nearest suitable landing site. (5) Declare an emergency on ATC frequency (or broadcast if no ATC coverage): Mayday call with zero oil pressure, diverting to nearest aerodrome. (6) Plan a descent and landing at the nearest aerodrome; do not climb or accelerate. (7) Once on the ground, allow the engine to cool before inspection. Do not restart the engine; have it inspected for cause of pressure loss. Continued flight with zero oil pressure will result in bearing failure and complete engine seizure.
exam_tip: Low/zero oil pressure in flight: MAYDAY, reduce power immediately, proceed straight to nearest aerodrome, declare emergency, land as soon as possible. Do not restart.
key_facts: Emergency condition; indicates bearing lubrication loss
Reduce power immediately to minimum
Check oil temperature; rapid rise = bearing damage occurring
Proceed straight to nearest suitable aerodrome
Declare Mayday on ATC or broadcast frequency
Land without delay; allow engine to cool before inspection
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Emergency procedures), POH emergency section, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.32.14

syllabus_desc: State the actions (if any) that the pilot can take to rectify: High oil pressure
concept: High oil pressure in flight (typically >100 psi, above green arc) indicates excessive pressure in the oil system. Causes: (1) Oil cooler blockage (ice ingestion, debris, or restriction). (2) Oil filter clogged (contamination blocking filter element). (3) Wrong oil viscosity (oil too thick, typically high-grade oil used in wrong season or temperature condition). (4) Relief valve sticking or failure. (5) Faulty oil pressure gauge (false reading while actual pressure is normal). Pilot response: (1) Reduce engine power to lower RPM (reduces pump output pressure). (2) Check cowl flap position (open cowl flaps fully to increase airflow through cooler). (3) Monitor oil temperature; if temperature is normal, the high-pressure reading may be a gauge fault. (4) Continue to monitor pressure trend; if pressure decreases with power reduction, the fault is likely normal operation at high power or incorrect oil grade. (5) If high pressure persists at reduced power, or if oil temperature is rising, plan a descent and landing at the nearest suitable aerodrome for investigation. (6) Do not continue long-distance flight with persistent high pressure; this stresses oil lines and seals and risks ruptures. (7) Once on the ground, check oil level and grade against POH; change to correct grade if necessary, and have the cooler and relief valve inspected.
exam_tip: High oil pressure in flight: reduce power, open cowl flaps, monitor temperature. If persists or temperature rises: divert to nearest aerodrome. Check oil grade and cooler after landing.
key_facts: Causes: cooler blockage, filter blockage, wrong oil grade, stuck relief valve, gauge fault
Reduce power to lower pump pressure
Open cowl flaps to increase cooler airflow
Monitor temperature; high pressure + rising temp = cooler issue
If pressure persists at reduced power: divert to nearest aerodrome
Risk: high pressure stresses lines and seals, risks ruptures
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Engine monitoring), POH troubleshooting, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.32.14

syllabus_desc: State the actions (if any) that the pilot can take to rectify: Fluctuating oil pressure
concept: Fluctuating oil pressure in flight (pressure gauge needle oscillating up and down) indicates an unstable oil supply, transient blockage, air in the system, or pump wear. Causes: (1) Low oil level (pump partially draws air). (2) Partial blockage in oil filter or main gallery (intermittent blockage causes oscillation). (3) Air in the oil system (entrapped air bubbles reduce pressure). (4) Worn oil pump (produces inconsistent output). (5) Relief valve chatter (relief valve hunting between open and closed positions). (6) Faulty oil pressure gauge. Pilot response: (1) Reduce engine power immediately to lower RPM (reduces pressure cycling). (2) Monitor oil temperature; if temperature is rising, this indicates real oil starvation is occurring and bearing damage may be happening. (3) Check ammeter and voltmeter to confirm electrical system is functioning; electrical failure could indicate a gauge malfunction. (4) Observe the fluctuation trend: if pressure is oscillating around the green arc and temperature is stable, the problem may be a faulty gauge or minor relief valve chattering. (5) If oil temperature rises significantly while pressure fluctuates, or if pressure trend is downward, this indicates real oil system trouble. (6) Declare an emergency and plan descent and landing at the nearest suitable aerodrome. (7) Once on the ground, check oil level; add oil if low and have the system inspected for air leaks or pump wear.
exam_tip: Fluctuating pressure in flight: reduce power, monitor oil temperature trend. Rising temperature + falling pressure = oil starvation. Declare emergency, descend to nearest aerodrome.
key_facts: Causes: low oil level, partial blockage, air in system, pump wear, relief valve chatter
Reduce power immediately
Monitor oil temperature for rising trend
Rising temperature + fluctuating pressure = real oil starvation
Declare emergency, plan descent to nearest aerodrome
Once landed: check oil level, inspect for air leaks
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Emergency procedures), POH emergency section, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.32.14

syllabus_desc: State the actions (if any) that the pilot can take to rectify: Low oil temperature
concept: Low oil temperature in flight (below green arc, typically <50°C) is generally not an emergency condition but indicates that the engine is not warming properly. Causes: (1) Cold ambient temperature (high-altitude flight, winter operation). (2) Cooler bypass valve stuck open (allows all oil to bypass cooler, preventing warming). (3) Excessive airflow over cooler (cowl flaps fully open in cold conditions). (4) Engine running at idle or very low power for extended period. Pilot response: (1) Low oil temperature by itself does not require emergency action. (2) Monitor oil temperature trend; if temperature is slowly rising toward the green arc, normal warming is occurring. (3) If temperature remains flat (not rising), the cooler bypass valve may be stuck; open or close cowl flaps as appropriate to change cooling and attempt to warm oil. (4) Continue flight; low oil temperature does not prevent flight operations. (5) In climb or cruise, apply moderate power to generate engine heat; this warms the oil over time. (6) Once at cruising altitude, monitor oil temperature periodically. (7) If oil temperature never enters the green arc after extended flight, record this and have the cooler bypass valve and cooling system inspected after landing. Low oil temperature alone is not an emergency, but persistently low temperature warrants investigation post-flight.
exam_tip: Low oil temperature in flight not emergency. Monitor trend; oil should gradually warm. If temperature plateaus: cooler bypass may be stuck. Apply moderate power to warm engine. Investigate post-flight if temperature doesn't enter green arc.
key_facts: Causes: cold ambient, cooler bypass stuck, excessive cooling, low power operation
Low temperature alone not emergency
Monitor trend; temperature should slowly rise
Apply moderate power to generate engine heat
Adjust cowl flaps as appropriate
Persistent low temperature: cooler bypass investigation post-flight
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Engine monitoring), POH troubleshooting, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.32.14

syllabus_desc: State the actions (if any) that the pilot can take to rectify: High oil temperature
concept: High oil temperature in flight (above green arc, typically >100°C or >210°F) is a caution condition requiring immediate pilot action to prevent engine damage. Causes: (1) Cooler blockage (ice, debris, or restricted airflow). (2) Inadequate airflow (flying too slowly, cooler poorly positioned, low-level flight in hot air). (3) Filter blockage (partial or complete). (4) Excessive power (continuous high-power climb or high-load operation). (5) Low oil level (less oil capacity to absorb heat). (6) Wrong oil grade (poor cooling properties or too-thin viscosity causing excessive friction). Pilot response: (1) Immediately reduce engine power to lower power output and heat generation. (2) If aircraft has cowl flaps, open them fully to maximise airflow through the cooler. (3) If applicable, lean the fuel mixture slightly to reduce power without increasing friction heating. (4) Monitor oil temperature trend; if temperature is decreasing, the actions taken are working. (5) If temperature continues rising or enters the red arc (typically >105-110°C), declare an emergency immediately. (6) Plan a descent and landing at the nearest suitable aerodrome without delay. (7) Avoid high-altitude flight; descend to lower altitude where air temperature is cooler and airflow over cooler is greater. (8) Once on the ground, allow the engine to cool completely, then check oil level (add oil if low) and inspect the cooler for blockage or damage. Have the cooling system thoroughly inspected before the next flight. Extended high-temperature operation damages oil quality, reduces lubrication effectiveness, and can cause permanent engine damage.
exam_tip: High oil temperature: reduce power, open cowl flaps, lean mixture, monitor temperature. If temp enters red arc or continues rising: Mayday, descend to nearest aerodrome immediately.
key_facts: Causes: cooler blockage, inadequate airflow, filter blockage, excessive power, low oil, wrong grade
Reduce power immediately
Open cowl flaps fully
Lean fuel mixture if needed
Monitor temperature trend
Red arc or rising temp: Mayday, descend to nearest aerodrome immediately
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Emergency procedures), POH emergency section, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.32.14

syllabus_desc: Describe the correct oil replenishment procedure for a typical aircraft.
concept: Check oil level with the dipstick during every preflight inspection. The oil filler is typically on the top of the engine, accessed through the cowling. Add only the approved oil type and grade. Add oil in small increments to avoid overfilling. Do not mix different oil types (mineral and synthetic) unless specifically approved. After adding oil, replace the filler cap securely and recheck the dipstick. Record the oil addition in the aircraft technical log. If oil consumption seems unusually high, report to maintenance.
exam_tip: Check with dipstick every preflight. Add only approved oil. Don't overfill. Don't mix oil types. Secure the cap. Log the addition.
key_facts: Check oil level with dipstick every preflight
Add only approved oil type and grade
Add in small increments to avoid overfilling
Do not mix different oil types unless approved
Replace filler cap securely
Record addition in technical log
Report unusually high consumption to maintenance
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (oil replenishment), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.32.16

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and principle of operation of the following instruments: Tachometers (rpm) gauges (centrifugal and drag cup)
concept: A tachometer is a cockpit instrument that displays engine revolutions per minute (RPM). The tachometer is essential for monitoring engine speed and ensuring the engine operates within its approved range. Light aircraft engines typically have a maximum RPM limit (red line, usually 2500-2700 RPM for typical Lycoming or Continental engines). The gauge face displays a green arc (normal operating range, typically 1000-2500 RPM), a yellow arc (caution range, 2500-2700 RPM), and a red line (maximum RPM, do not exceed). Operating at red line or beyond causes excessive vibration, stresses engine components, and risks catastrophic engine failure. The tachometer is mechanically or electrically driven (depending on aircraft age); mechanical tachometers have a cable drive from the engine, electrical tachometers receive a signal from the magneto or electronic engine monitor. The pilot uses the tachometer to manage engine power: setting manifold pressure and RPM for cruise, climb, or approach configurations. The tachometer is one of the primary instruments for engine health monitoring.
exam_tip: Tachometer displays engine RPM. Green arc: normal operating range. Yellow arc: caution. Red line: maximum RPM. Do not exceed red line. Essential for power management.
key_facts: Displays engine revolutions per minute (RPM)
Green arc: normal operating range (typically 1000-2500 RPM)
Yellow arc: caution range (2500-2700 RPM)
Red line: maximum RPM (do not exceed)
Exceeding red line causes vibration, engine stress, failure risk
Used for power management and engine health monitoring
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Engine instruments), POH normal procedures, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.34.2

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and principle of operation of the following instruments: Manifold pressure and boost gauges
concept: Manifold pressure gauge: displays the air pressure in the engine intake manifold (between the carburetor and the intake valves), measured in inches of mercury (in. Hg). In naturally aspirated engines, manifold pressure varies with throttle position and altitude; at sea level with throttle fully open, manifold pressure may reach 28-30 in. Hg. As altitude increases, the ambient pressure decreases, and manifold pressure decreases. At 10,000 ft, manifold pressure may only reach 15-17 in. Hg with throttle fully open. Manifold pressure is used to set engine power in cruise: the pilot reduces manifold pressure to a lower value (e.g., 18-20 in. Hg) to reduce power and fuel consumption. Boost gauge: found only on turbocharged or supercharged engines. The boost gauge measures pressure above atmospheric (boost), typically showing 0 psi at sea level (no boost) and increasing with altitude or supercharger activation to maintain higher manifold pressures at altitude. Boost gauges allow pilots of high-performance aircraft to maintain manifold pressure and power at higher altitudes. Both gauges are essential for proper power management and preventing engine overboosting.
exam_tip: Manifold gauge: displays intake manifold pressure (in. Hg). Used to set engine power. Decreases with altitude. Boost gauge: measures supercharger pressure above atmospheric.
key_facts: Manifold pressure: air pressure in intake manifold (in. Hg)
Naturally aspirated: manifold pressure = ambient pressure × throttle setting
At sea level full throttle: ~28-30 in. Hg
At 10,000 ft full throttle: ~15-17 in. Hg
Used to set cruise power (reduce for lean cruise)
Boost gauge: supercharged engines, measures boost pressure above atmospheric
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Engine instruments), POH normal procedures, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.34.2

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and principle of operation of the following instruments: Direct reading oil pressure gauges
concept: A direct-reading oil pressure gauge (or oil pressure gauge) is a cockpit instrument that measures and displays the pressure of engine oil in the main oil gallery. The gauge measures pressure in pounds per square inch (psi). The gauge is supplied by a small-bore tube (pressure line) from a pressure port on the engine oil system; this line contains oil at system pressure, which pushes a Bourdon tube (curved pressure-sensing element) in the gauge, deflecting a needle on the gauge face. The gauge face is marked with: green arc (normal operating range, typically 40-100 psi depending on engine and oil grade), yellow arc (caution range, if applicable), and red zones (high and low limits). At engine startup, oil pressure should rise to green arc within 15-30 seconds. At idle, oil pressure may be at the lower end of green arc; at high power, pressure rises toward the upper end. The oil pressure gauge is essential for monitoring engine health; abnormal pressure readings indicate oil system faults (see sections on oil pressure abnormalities).
exam_tip: Direct-reading oil pressure gauge: measures oil pressure (psi). Green arc: 40-100 psi (typical). Must rise to green within 15-30 sec at start. Essential for engine health monitoring.
key_facts: Measures oil pressure in main oil gallery (psi)
Supplied by pressure line from engine
Bourdon tube deflects needle on gauge face
Green arc: normal operating range (40-100 psi typical)
Pressure must rise within 15-30 seconds of start
Pressure varies with RPM and oil viscosity
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Engine instruments), POH normal procedures, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.34.2

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and principle of operation of the following instruments: Vacuum gauges
