concept: A vacuum gauge (or suction gauge) measures the air pressure (or negative pressure, vacuum) in the engine induction system or the vacuum line supplying vacuum-powered instruments. The gauge is marked in inches of mercury (in. Hg) and displays negative pressure (vacuum). Vacuum gauges have two main uses: (1) Engine vacuum measurement: displays vacuum generated by the engine intake system (typically 3-5 in. Hg in a naturally aspirated engine at cruise). Engine vacuum decreases if the induction system is blocked (intake filter clogged) or if there is an air leak in the system. (2) Instrument vacuum measurement: displays the vacuum supplied to vacuum-operated flight instruments (artificial horizon, heading indicator, or turn coordinator). These vacuum-powered instruments require a steady vacuum source (typically 4-5 in. Hg) to operate correctly. A vacuum gauge in the instrument vacuum line shows if the vacuum system is providing adequate suction. If instrument vacuum falls below the required range, the artificial horizon and heading indicator will become unreliable. Modern aircraft increasingly use electric gyroscope instruments instead of vacuum-powered ones, reducing the need for vacuum gauges. On older aircraft with vacuum-powered flight instruments, a vacuum gauge is essential for monitoring instrument reliability.
exam_tip: Vacuum gauge measures negative pressure (vacuum) in in. Hg. Engine vacuum: 3-5 in. Hg typical. Instrument vacuum: requires 4-5 in. Hg for reliable flight instruments.
key_facts: Measures vacuum (negative pressure) in in. Hg
Engine vacuum: 3-5 in. Hg typical at cruise
Vacuum decreases if intake filter clogged or system has air leak
Instrument vacuum: supplies vacuum-powered flight instruments
Instrument vacuum: requires 4-5 in. Hg for reliable operation
Modern aircraft increasingly electric instruments; vacuum becoming less critical
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Engine instruments), POH normal procedures, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.34.2

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and principle of operation of the following instruments: Outside air temperature gauges
concept: An outside air temperature (OAT) gauge displays the ambient air temperature (temperature of the air outside the aircraft) in degrees Celsius or Fahrenheit. The probe is mounted on the aircraft exterior (typically on a wing strut or fuselage) where it measures static air temperature (SAT). The OAT gauge is essential for flight planning and performance calculations: (1) Density altitude calculation: OAT combined with altitude determines air density, which affects aircraft performance, engine power, and takeoff/landing distance. High temperature and high altitude result in high density altitude, reducing aircraft performance. (2) Icing forecasting: if OAT is between −5°C and +15°C (the icing layer), there is significant risk of aircraft icing. Pilots monitor OAT to assess icing risk and determine appropriate flight altitudes and de-icing actions. (3) Engine performance: OAT affects air density into the engine and is used to calculate true airspeed (TAS) from indicated airspeed (IAS). The OAT gauge should be calibrated and accurate; some aircraft use a heated probe to reduce temperature measurement errors caused by air pressure (ram rise) at high speed. Without an OAT gauge, pilots lose an important tool for performance prediction and icing risk assessment.
exam_tip: OAT gauge displays outside air temperature (°C or °F). Used for density altitude, icing risk, and TAS calculation. Icing risk: −5°C to +15°C. Essential for flight planning.
key_facts: Displays outside air temperature (static air temperature)
Probe mounted on aircraft exterior
Used for density altitude calculation (affects aircraft performance)
Used for icing risk assessment (−5°C to +15°C is icing layer)
Used to calculate true airspeed (TAS)
Heated probe reduces temperature measurement errors at high speed
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Engine and environmental instruments), POH, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.34.2

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and principle of operation of the following instruments: Fuel quantity gauges
concept: A fuel quantity gauge displays the amount of fuel remaining in the aircraft fuel tanks in litres or gallons. The gauge is located in the cockpit and is linked to a fuel level sensor in the fuel tank. Two common types of fuel gauges: (1) Electrical capacitive gauge: a fuel level sender in the tank measures the capacitance (which changes with fuel level), and the sender transmits an electrical signal to the cockpit gauge. The gauge needle moves proportionally to the fuel quantity. (2) Float-type gauge: a float arm moves vertically with the fuel level; as the float rises and falls, it mechanically drives a needle on the cockpit gauge face. Fuel quantity gauges are notorious for inaccuracy, especially in turbulent flight when fuel sloshes and the float or sensor gives a false reading. For this reason, fuel quantity gauges are considered unreliable for precise fuel planning; visual inspection of the fuel tanks (looking into the filler access) during preflight is the primary method for confirming fuel quantity. During flight, the pilot should cross-check the gauge indication with actual flight time and fuel flow (in litres per hour) to track fuel consumption and estimate remaining flight time. Many modern aircraft also include a fuel flow meter (which measures fuel burn rate) to provide better fuel tracking.
exam_tip: Fuel quantity gauge displays fuel remaining (litres or gallons). Prone to fluctuation; visual preflight check and fuel flow monitoring are primary methods.
key_facts: Electrical capacitive or float-type gauge
Displays fuel in cockpit (litres or gallons)
Prone to fluctuation in turbulent flight
Not reliable for precise fuel planning
Visual preflight inspection is primary fuel check
Cross-check with flight time and fuel flow during flight
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Engine instruments), POH normal procedures, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.34.2

syllabus_desc: Identify the three basic instruments which rely on air pressure for their operation.
concept: The three air pressure instruments are: the airspeed indicator (ASI), which measures the difference between pitot (total) pressure and static pressure; the altimeter, which measures static pressure to determine altitude; and the vertical speed indicator (VSI), which measures the rate of change of static pressure to show climb or descent rate. All three depend on accurate pitot and/or static pressure inputs.
exam_tip: ASI, altimeter, and VSI  -  all rely on pitot-static pressure. If the pitot or static source is blocked, one or more of these instruments will be affected.
key_facts: Airspeed indicator (ASI): uses pitot and static pressure
Altimeter: uses static pressure only
Vertical speed indicator (VSI): uses static pressure only
All depend on pitot-static system integrity
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (pressure instruments), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.36.2

syllabus_desc: Describe static pressure and dynamic pressure, and the main factors which affect them.
concept: Static pressure is the atmospheric pressure at the aircraft's altitude  -  it decreases with altitude and is measured through static ports on the fuselage. Dynamic pressure (also called ram pressure or impact pressure) is the pressure caused by the aircraft's movement through the air  -  it increases with airspeed (proportional to V²). Total pressure = static + dynamic. The pitot tube measures total pressure; static ports measure static pressure. The difference (total minus static) equals dynamic pressure, which the ASI converts to an airspeed reading.
exam_tip: Static pressure = atmospheric pressure at altitude. Dynamic pressure = pressure from movement (proportional to V²). Total = static + dynamic. ASI reads the difference.
key_facts: Static pressure: atmospheric pressure at altitude (decreases with height)
Dynamic pressure: caused by movement through air (proportional to V²)
Total pressure = static + dynamic
Pitot tube measures total pressure
Static ports measure static pressure
ASI reads the difference (dynamic pressure)
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (pitot-static pressures), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.36.4

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation of a pitot-static system, including: Static vent(s)
concept: Static vent: an opening or tube on the aircraft exterior that measures static air pressure (atmospheric pressure unaffected by aircraft motion). Static vents are connected via tubing to pitot-static instruments (altimeter, airspeed indicator, vertical speed indicator) to provide the reference pressure needed for those instruments to function. Static vents must be positioned on the aircraft in areas where the pressure is truly static (undisturbed by the aircraft's motion through the air). Typical locations: fuselage side, near the wing root, or sometimes on both sides of the fuselage for cross-checking. Modern aircraft often have multiple static vents to reduce errors caused by aircraft attitude changes. If the static vent becomes blocked (ice, dirt, water), the pitot-static instruments lose their pressure reference and give false readings (altimeter freezes, airspeed indicator becomes unreliable). Static vent blockage is a potential safety issue; pilots must ensure static vents are clear during preflight inspection. Some aircraft have alternative static sources (alternate static pressure port or cabin pressure connection) that can be selected if the main static vent is blocked.
exam_tip: Static vent measures atmospheric pressure. Located on fuselage, free from airflow disturbance. If blocked: altimeter freezes, airspeed becomes unreliable. Check clear during preflight.
key_facts: Measures static (atmospheric) pressure
Located on fuselage side or wing root
Positioned to avoid airflow disturbance
Connected to altimeter, airspeed, VSI
Blockage: instruments freeze or become unreliable
Multiple vents reduce pressure errors with aircraft attitude
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Pitot-static systems), POH preflight procedures, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.36.6

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation of a pitot-static system, including: Pitot tube
concept: Pitot tube: an external tube on the aircraft that measures dynamic air pressure (the pressure created by the aircraft moving through the air). The pitot tube has an opening facing forward into the airflow (ram air inlet). Air enters the pitot tube, creating a pressure at the inlet proportional to the aircraft's airspeed. This pressure is transmitted via tubing to the airspeed indicator, which displays the dynamic pressure as indicated airspeed (IAS). A pitot tube typically has: (1) A forward-facing ram air inlet (opening into the relative wind). (2) A static pressure port on the side of the tube (connected to the static vents to provide reference pressure). (3) Drain holes at the rear to prevent water accumulation. The pitot tube must remain clean and unobstructed; even small blockages (ice, dirt, moisture) significantly affect airspeed indication accuracy. Pitot tube blockage is a serious flight safety issue; airspeed becomes unreliable, which impairs stall protection, performance calculations, and speed limits. During preflight, pilots check that the pitot tube opening is clear. A pitot cover (removable protective cover) is often placed on the pitot tube when the aircraft is parked to protect it from dirt and insects; the pilot must remove this cover before flight.
exam_tip: Pitot tube measures dynamic air pressure from airflow. Ram air inlet faces forward. Measures airspeed. Blockage: airspeed becomes unreliable (serious safety issue). Remove pitot cover before flight.
key_facts: Measures dynamic air pressure (pressure from aircraft motion)
Ram air inlet faces forward into relative wind
Transmits pressure to airspeed indicator
Static port on tube provides reference pressure
Drain holes prevent water accumulation
Blockage is safety critical: airspeed becomes unreliable
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Pitot-static systems), POH preflight procedures, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.36.6

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation of a pitot-static system, including: Combined pitot-static head
concept: Combined pitot-static head (or integrated pitot head): a single probe that combines both the pitot (dynamic) and static pressure functions into one unit on the aircraft exterior. Instead of separate pitot and static vents, a combined head has: (1) A forward-facing ram air inlet (measures dynamic pressure, like a pitot tube). (2) Static ports on the side of the probe (measures static pressure, like static vents). Both pressures are internally separated and transmitted to the pitot-static instruments via different tubing lines. The combined head reduces the number of external openings, simplifying installation and reducing weight. However, the combined head is more susceptible to errors if the probe becomes misaligned with the airflow (e.g., during a slip or unusual attitude) because the static ports may not be in an undisturbed pressure region. Combined pitot-static heads are common on many light aircraft. The combined head must remain clean and unobstructed; blockage of either the pitot inlet or static ports renders the pitot-static instruments unreliable.
exam_tip: Combined pitot-static head: single probe combining pitot (dynamic) and static (atmospheric) pressure. Simplifies installation. More prone to errors in unusual attitudes.
key_facts: Single probe combines pitot and static functions
Forward inlet measures dynamic pressure
Side ports measure static pressure
Reduces external openings and weight
More susceptible to errors in slips or unusual attitudes
Blockage of either function renders instruments unreliable
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Pitot-static systems), POH, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.36.6

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation of a pitot-static system, including: Drain holes, heating, and pitot cover
concept: Drain holes: small openings (typically at the bottom of the pitot tube or static vent tubing) that allow water, moisture, or condensation to drain out of the system. Water in pitot or static lines causes erratic instrument readings; drain holes prevent water accumulation. Pilots can manually drain pitot and static lines during preflight by opening drain plugs or using a manual drain valve (if installed). Heating: some aircraft have electrically heated pitot tubes and static probes to prevent ice formation on these probes during flight in icing conditions. A heated pitot/static system is important for aircraft that intentionally fly in instrument meteorological conditions (IMC) or known icing. Heating is controlled by a switch in the cockpit; the pilot switches on pitot heat before entering icing conditions. Pitot cover: a removable protective cover (usually foam or plastic) that is placed over the pitot tube when the aircraft is parked. The cover protects the pitot inlet from dirt, insects, and water. The pilot must remove the pitot cover before flight; flying with the pitot cover in place renders the airspeed indicator completely inoperative (a critical safety failure).
exam_tip: Drain holes: allow water to exit pitot/static lines. Heating: electrically heated probe prevents ice (required for icing conditions). Pitot cover: removable protective cover - must be removed before flight.
key_facts: Drain holes: allow water/condensation to exit system
Manual drains can be opened during preflight
Heating: electric heater prevents ice formation
Pitot heat required for flight in icing conditions
Pitot cover: protective foam cover for parked aircraft
Cover MUST be removed before flight (critical)
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Pitot-static systems), POH preflight and equipment, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.36.6

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation of a pitot-static system, including: Alternate pressure source
concept: Alternate pressure source (or alternate static): a backup source of static pressure that can be selected if the primary static vent becomes blocked. Alternate static pressure is typically obtained from: (1) An alternative static port on the aircraft fuselage (different location from the primary static vent). (2) The cabin pressure (in pressurised aircraft, cabin air is maintained at a reference pressure that can serve as static reference). (3) A manual valve that connects cabin air directly to the pitot-static instruments. If the primary static vent becomes blocked (ice, obstruction), the pilot can select the alternate static source using a cockpit selector valve or switch. This restores static pressure reference to the pitot-static instruments, allowing the altimeter and airspeed indicator to function. The alternate static source is a safety feature that ensures the pilot retains airspeed and altitude information even if the primary static vent fails. In some aircraft, flying with alternate static selected may introduce small errors in altitude and airspeed readings due to the alternate port being in a different pressure environment, but the information is better than the frozen instruments that result from a blocked primary vent.
exam_tip: Alternate static: backup static pressure source. Selected if primary vent blocked (ice). Restores instrument function. May introduce small errors but better than frozen instruments.
key_facts: Backup source of static pressure
Alternative port on fuselage or cabin pressure source
Selected via cockpit valve if primary vent blocked
Restores altimeter and airspeed function
May introduce small altitude/airspeed errors
Better than frozen instruments from blocked primary vent
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Pitot-static system redundancy), POH, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.36.6

syllabus_desc: With respect to the airspeed indicator, describe the: Basic principle of operation and serviceability checks
concept: The airspeed indicator displays the aircraft's speed through the air (indicated airspeed, IAS) by measuring the difference between dynamic pressure (from the pitot tube) and static pressure (from the static vents). A Pitot-pressure diaphragm inside the instrument responds to this pressure difference, deflecting a needle on the gauge face to indicate IAS in knots. Basic principle: dynamic pressure increases with airspeed, so higher airspeed produces higher pressure difference and larger needle deflection. Serviceability checks: (1) Preflight: ensure pitot cover is removed and pitot tube opening is clear. (2) Check static vents are unobstructed. (3) During taxi and takeoff, verify the airspeed needle moves smoothly from zero as the aircraft accelerates. (4) At altitude, cross-check airspeed against other instruments and performance expectations. (5) Stall warning: if equipped, the airspeed indicator may have a stall warning flag or colour coding that appears near the stall speed. If the airspeed indicator becomes unreliable (needle erratic, frozen, or reading zero while flying), the pitot or static system has a blockage. The pilot must rely on pitch attitude, power setting, and other instruments to estimate airspeed until landing.
exam_tip: Airspeed indicator measures pressure difference (pitot minus static). Displays IAS in knots. Check pitot/static clear during preflight. In flight: smooth needle movement and reasonable values confirm serviceability.
key_facts: Measures dynamic pressure minus static pressure
Pitot-pressure diaphragm deflects needle
Displays IAS in knots
Preflight: remove pitot cover, check clear
Check static vents unobstructed
In flight: verify needle moves smoothly and values are reasonable
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Air data instruments), POH preflight procedures, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.36.8

syllabus_desc: With respect to the airspeed indicator, describe the: Colour coding, and the meaning of VSO, VS1, VFE, VNO and VNE
concept: Airspeed colour coding on the indicator face represents different speed regimes and limits: VSO (stall speed in landing configuration): white arc lower limit. Green arc (normal operating range): VS1 to VNO. VFE (flap extended speed): white arc upper limit. VNO (maximum structural cruise speed): green arc upper limit. VNE (never exceed speed): red line. Specific definitions: VSO = stall speed in landing configuration (flaps down, gear down). VS1 = stall speed in clean configuration (flaps up, gear up). VFE = maximum speed with flaps extended (if flaps are extended beyond this speed, aerodynamic forces may damage the flap structure). VNO = maximum structural cruise speed (beyond this speed, turbulence may exceed aircraft structural limits). VNE = never exceed speed (beyond this speed, structural failure is imminent; always avoid exceeding VNE). The colour arcs help pilots quickly identify safe and unsafe speed ranges. White arc (flap operating range) is between VSO and VFE. Green arc (normal range) is between VS1 and VNO. Yellow arc (caution range) is between VNO and VNE. Red line is VNE. Knowing these speed limits is critical for safe aircraft operations.
exam_tip: VSO/VS1: stall speeds. VFE: max flap speed. VNO: max cruise speed. VNE: never exceed (red line). White arc: flap range. Yellow arc: caution. Always avoid VNE.
key_facts: VSO (white arc bottom): stall speed landing config
VS1 (green arc bottom): stall speed clean config
VFE (white arc top): max flap extended speed
VNO (green arc top): max structural cruise speed
VNE (red line): never exceed speed
Yellow arc: between VNO and VNE (caution range)
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Airspeed limits), AC 61-3 §12.1, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.36.8

syllabus_desc: With respect to the airspeed indicator, describe the: IAS/TAS/groundspeed relationship
concept: Indicated airspeed (IAS): the airspeed displayed on the airspeed indicator, measured from pitot-static pressure. IAS is affected by aircraft altitude and temperature (at high altitude, air density is lower, so a given IAS indicates lower true airspeed). True airspeed (TAS): the aircraft's actual speed through the air mass, in knots. TAS = IAS adjusted for altitude and temperature using the outside air temperature (OAT) and altitude. At sea level, TAS is approximately equal to IAS. At 10,000 ft, TAS may be 20-30% higher than IAS. Groundspeed (GS): the aircraft's actual speed over the ground, affected by wind. GS = TAS ± wind component. Headwind reduces GS; tailwind increases GS. Relationship: IAS is the primary flight instrument for controlling aircraft (stall speeds, speed limits, airspeed control are all based on IAS). TAS is used for flight planning, fuel consumption estimates, and time calculations (a longer flight time is needed if TAS is lower due to altitude). Groundspeed is used to calculate actual track over the ground and time to reach waypoints or destination. Modern aircraft with flight computers automatically convert IAS to TAS using OAT and altitude data.
exam_tip: IAS: displayed airspeed. TAS: actual speed through air (IAS corrected for altitude/temperature). Groundspeed: TAS ± wind. Use IAS for flight control; TAS/GS for navigation.
key_facts: IAS: airspeed indicator reading (affected by density)
TAS: actual airspeed through air (IAS corrected for altitude and OAT)
TAS ≈ IAS at sea level; TAS > IAS at altitude
Groundspeed: TAS ± wind component
Use IAS for flight control (stall speeds, limits)
Use TAS/GS for flight planning and navigation
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.6 (Airspeed relationships), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.36.8

syllabus_desc: With respect to the airspeed indicator, describe the: Errors affecting the ASI, and how position error correction is applied
concept: Airspeed errors: (1) Position error: the static vent may not be in a perfectly static pressure region depending on aircraft attitude and speed. In a climb or descent, or with the aircraft in a slip, the static pressure sensed may differ from true static pressure, causing the airspeed to read high or low. (2) Instrument error: internal friction or wear in the airspeed indicator mechanism can cause the needle to lag or read inaccurately. (3) Blocked pitot: if the pitot tube becomes blocked with ice or debris, airspeed indicator freezes at whatever value it showed when the blockage occurred. (4) Blocked static: if the static vent becomes blocked, airspeed becomes unreliable (typically reads zero if static pressure is completely blocked, or oscillates if partially blocked). Position error correction: manufacturers test aircraft in a wind tunnel to measure airspeed errors at various attitudes and speeds, creating a correction curve or table. Corrections are typically small (a few knots) and may be published in the POH. Modern aircraft often use multiple static sources or alternate static systems to reduce position errors. Pilots should be aware that airspeed may not be perfectly accurate and should cross-check with performance expectations and other instruments.
exam_tip: Airspeed errors: position error (attitude, slip), instrument error (friction, wear), blockages (pitot, static). Position error correction published in POH. Cross-check with performance and other instruments.
key_facts: Position error: static vent not in perfect static region
Error varies with aircraft attitude (climb, descent, slip)
Instrument error: friction, wear cause lag or inaccuracy
Pitot blockage: airspeed freezes
Static blockage: airspeed reads zero or oscillates
Position error correction: table or curve in POH
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Airspeed errors), POH, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.36.8

syllabus_desc: With respect to the altimeter, describe the: Basic principle of operation and serviceability checks
concept: The altimeter displays the aircraft's altitude above a reference pressure level by measuring static air pressure. As altitude increases, atmospheric pressure decreases. The altimeter contains an aneroid capsule (thin-walled metal container with low internal pressure) that expands or contracts with changing external (static) pressure. This mechanical movement drives a needle on a dial that indicates altitude in feet. Serviceability checks: (1) Preflight: check that static vents are clear and unobstructed. (2) On the ground before flight, set the altimeter subscale (Kollsman window) to the airfield elevation or local barometric pressure (QNH). (3) The altimeter should read approximately the field elevation ±100 ft. (4) In flight, cross-check altimeter reading with other altitude references (visual terrain recognition, radio altitude if equipped). (5) If the altimeter becomes erratic (needle jumps, reads zero, or is stuck), the static system may be blocked. The altimeter is essential for safe flight; loss of reliable altitude information is a critical safety issue. If the main static vent is blocked, the alternate static source (if available) can be selected to restore altitude information.
exam_tip: Altimeter measures static pressure to display altitude. Check static vents clear. Set subscale to field elevation before flight. Altimeter should read ±100 ft of field elevation.
key_facts: Aneroid capsule expands/contracts with pressure change
Mechanical linkage drives needle on dial
Displays altitude in feet
Requires accurate static pressure reference
Preflight: set subscale (Kollsman window) to QNH
Field elevation check: altimeter should read ±100 ft
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Altitude measurement), POH preflight procedures, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.36.10

syllabus_desc: With respect to the altimeter, describe the: Subscale settings and the meaning of QNH and QFE
concept: Subscale: the setting window (Kollsman window) on the altimeter that allows the pilot to adjust the altitude reference pressure. Two pressure settings are commonly used: QNH (altimeter setting, barometric pressure): the barometric pressure at sea level at the aircraft's current location. Setting the altimeter subscale to QNH makes the altimeter read height above mean sea level (MSL). This is the standard setting used for flight planning, approach plates, and navigation. QFE (airfield pressure): the atmospheric pressure at the specific airfield. Setting the subscale to QFE makes the altimeter read height above the airfield (approximately zero on the ground at that airfield). QFE is less commonly used but may be preferred by military or operations in areas where aerodrome height above MSL is critical. In New Zealand civil aviation, QNH is the standard setting. Before flight, the pilot obtains the current QNH from ATIS or ATC and sets the altimeter subscale accordingly. This ensures the altimeter reads MSL altitude, which is essential for compliance with altitude restrictions, terrain clearance, and safe flight. If the altimeter subscale is set incorrectly, the entire altitude reading is offset, which can cause terrain collision or altitude restriction violations.
exam_tip: QNH: barometric pressure at sea level (standard setting). QFE: pressure at the airfield. Set subscale to QNH before flight for MSL altitude. Incorrect setting causes altitude offset errors.
key_facts: Subscale (Kollsman window) adjusts altitude reference
QNH: sea-level barometric pressure (standard)
Setting to QNH: altimeter reads MSL altitude
QFE: airfield pressure (less common)
Setting to QFE: altimeter reads height above aerodrome
Obtain current QNH from ATIS or ATC before flight
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Altitude reference), CAA NZ flight operations procedures, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.36.10

syllabus_desc: With respect to the altimeter, describe the: Errors affecting the altimeter, including subscale setting error
concept: Altimeter errors: (1) Subscale setting error: if the pilot sets the subscale incorrectly (wrong QNH, or forgets to update QNH after changing location), the altimeter reads high or low by the same amount as the pressure error. For example, if QNH is 1020 mb but the pilot set 1010 mb, the altimeter reads 10 mb too low (approximately 300 ft too low at sea level). This is a critical error that can lead to controlled flight into terrain (CFIT). (2) Position error: the static pressure source location may not be perfectly static, especially in unusual attitudes, climbs, or descents. Static pressure errors cause altitude reading errors of a few hundred feet. (3) Instrument error: mechanical wear or friction in the altimeter mechanism can cause the needle to lag or read inaccurately by small amounts. (4) Hysteresis error: the aneroid capsule does not expand and contract uniformly; climbing to an altitude and then descending to the same altitude may give different readings. (5) Temperature error: in non-standard temperature conditions, the pressure-to-altitude relationship changes. Cold air is denser, so a given pressure indicates higher altitude than standard. Warm air is less dense, so a given pressure indicates lower altitude. (6) Blocked static vent: complete blockage of the static system causes the altimeter to freeze at whatever altitude it displayed when the blockage occurred. Awareness of these errors helps pilots interpret altimeter readings critically and maintain terrain clearance through cross-checking.
exam_tip: Subscale error: incorrect QNH causes altitude offset (critical for CFIT). Position error: attitude changes cause error. Temperature, hysteresis, instrument error: small. Blocked static: freezes altimeter.
key_facts: Subscale setting error: incorrect QNH causes altitude offset
Example: wrong QNH setting by 10 mb = 300 ft altitude error
Position error: unusual attitudes cause small errors
Temperature error: cold = altitude error high, warm = error low
Hysteresis error: climb vs descent to same altitude reads differently
Instrument error: mechanical wear causes lag or inaccuracy
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Altitude errors), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.36.10

syllabus_desc: With respect to the vertical speed indicator, describe the: Basic principle of operation
concept: The vertical speed indicator (VSI, also called the rate of climb indicator) displays the aircraft's vertical speed (rate of altitude change) in feet per minute (ft/min). The instrument measures the rate of change of static pressure. Two aneroid capsules and a restricted orifice (time-delay mechanism) in the VSI respond to pressure changes: the internal capsule responds quickly to pressure changes, while the restricted orifice creates a time lag. This comparison produces a needle displacement proportional to the rate of pressure change, which corresponds to vertical speed. A positive reading (needle above zero) indicates climbing (altitude increasing); negative reading indicates descending (altitude decreasing). Errors: (1) Lag error: the VSI has a built-in time delay (1-5 seconds) due to the restrictive orifice. The needle lags behind actual vertical speed changes; the indicator does not instantly reflect changes in climb or descent rate. (2) Initial trend error: at the start of a climb or descent, the VSI may give a momentary false reading before settling to the true rate. (3) Blocked static: if the static vent is blocked, the VSI becomes unreliable (needle oscillates or freezes). (4) Instrument error: mechanical wear can reduce accuracy. Pilots must be aware of the lag and avoid overcontrolling the vertical speed; small adjustments are made and then time is allowed for the VSI to stabilize before judging the result.
exam_tip: VSI measures rate of static pressure change (vertical speed in ft/min). Lag error: needle lags actual changes by 1-5 sec. Do not overcontrol; allow time to stabilize.
key_facts: Displays vertical speed (rate of altitude change) in ft/min
Positive: climbing; negative: descending
Uses aneroid capsule and restricted orifice (time-delay mechanism)
Lag error: needle lags actual changes 1-5 seconds
Initial trend error: momentary false reading at start of climb/descent
Requires static pressure reference
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Vertical speed indication), POH, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.36.12

syllabus_desc: With respect to the vertical speed indicator, describe the: Errors affecting the VSI
concept: VSI errors: (1) Lag error: the internal time-delay mechanism causes the needle to lag behind actual vertical speed changes by 1-5 seconds. When the pilot begins a climb, the VSI does not immediately show a positive rate; the needle slowly rises. When the climb is leveled off, the needle slowly descends back to zero. This lag can cause pilot overcontrol if not understood. (2) Trend error (initial transient): at the moment a climb or descent begins, the VSI may show a brief exaggerated reading before settling to the true rate. For example, a sudden pitch-up for a climb might cause the VSI to momentarily show a high climb rate, then settle to the actual rate. (3) Blockage: if the static vent is blocked or restricted, the VSI becomes inaccurate or erratic. If the blockage is complete, the VSI needle may oscillate wildly or freeze. (4) Instrument error: mechanical friction, worn pivots, or failed springs reduce accuracy and cause lag. (5) Altitude-related errors: at very high altitudes where air pressure is extremely low, the aneroid mechanism may become less responsive. Pilots should be aware of these limitations and avoid relying solely on the VSI for precise vertical speed control. The VSI is best used as a trend instrument (is the aircraft climbing or descending?) rather than a precise rate instrument. Cross-checking with altimeter needle movement and pitch attitude provides confirmation.
exam_tip: Lag error (1-5 sec): needle doesn't instantly reflect rate changes. Trend error: momentary false reading. Blockage: oscillates or freezes. Use VSI as trend indicator, cross-check with altimeter.
key_facts: Lag error: needle lags actual vertical speed 1-5 sec
Trend error: initial exaggerated reading before settling
Blockage: oscillates or freezes if static vent blocked
Instrument error: friction, wear reduce accuracy
High altitude: aneroid less responsive
Use VSI as trend indicator; cross-check with altimeter
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Vertical speed errors), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.36.12

syllabus_desc: Indicate the normal checks for serviceability of the pitot-static system, both pre- flight and during operation.
concept: Pre-flight: visually inspect pitot tube for blockages (insects, dirt, covers left on); check static ports are clear; check pitot heat functions (if fitted); verify ASI, altimeter, and VSI readings make sense on the ground. In-flight: cross-check ASI against expected performance; cross-check altimeter against known altitude references; VSI should read zero in straight and level flight; if any instrument gives suspect readings, consider pitot or static blockage and select alternate static source if available.
exam_tip: Preflight: check pitot and static ports are clear, check pitot heat works. In flight: cross-check instruments against expected values and each other.
key_facts: Preflight: check pitot tube for blockages
Preflight: check static ports are clear
Preflight: verify pitot heat operates (if fitted)
Preflight: check instrument readings make sense on ground
In-flight: cross-check ASI against performance expectations
In-flight: VSI should read zero in level flight
Select alternate static if readings are suspect
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (pitot-static checks), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.36.14

syllabus_desc: Identify the cockpit indications of the following pitot-static system malfunctions, and state the actions available to the pilot to deal with the problem: Blockage of the pitot tube
concept: Pitot tube blockage: the pitot tube opening becomes blocked with ice, water, dirt, or insects, preventing dynamic air pressure from entering the instrument system. Symptoms: (1) Airspeed indicator freezes at whatever value it displayed when blockage occurred (if blockage occurred at cruise, airspeed appears normal initially but does not respond to speed changes). (2) As the aircraft slows (descent, slowing for approach), the frozen airspeed does not decrease; airspeed indicator becomes inconsistent with pitch attitude and engine power. (3) Stall warning systems relying on airspeed become unreliable. Prevention and management: (1) Preflight: visually inspect pitot tube opening and ensure it is clear. (2) Remove pitot cover before flight (critical). (3) In flight, if airspeed becomes erratic or frozen, suspect pitot blockage. (4) Cross-check airspeed against pitch attitude (for a given pitch and power, airspeed should have a normal value) and other instruments. (5) If pitot blockage is suspected, the pilot can use pitch attitude, power setting, and descent rate (from VSI or altimeter) to estimate airspeed and maintain control. (6) Land as soon as practicable and clear the pitot tube. Never assume the airspeed indicator is correct if there is any doubt; the consequences of a stall due to unreliable airspeed are severe.
exam_tip: Pitot blockage: airspeed freezes at blockage moment. In flight: airspeed doesn't change despite pitch/power changes. Preflight: ensure pitot clear, remove cover. If suspected in flight: cross-check with attitude, land ASAP.
key_facts: Blockage: ice, water, dirt, or insects block pitot inlet
Airspeed freezes at moment of blockage
Airspeed unresponsive to pitch and power changes
Stall warning becomes unreliable
Preflight: visually check pitot, remove cover
In flight: cross-check with attitude; land for investigation
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Pitot-static failures), POH, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.36.16

syllabus_desc: Identify the cockpit indications of the following pitot-static system malfunctions, and state the actions available to the pilot to deal with the problem: Blockage of the static source
concept: Static source blockage: the static vent opening(s) or tubing become blocked with ice, water, or debris, preventing static pressure from reaching the pitot-static instruments. Symptoms: (1) Altimeter freezes at whatever altitude it displayed when blockage occurred. (2) Airspeed indicator becomes unreliable (if pitot is clear but static is blocked, the dynamic pressure builds up in the pitot line unrelieved, causing the airspeed to increase and become unusually high, or freeze). (3) Vertical speed indicator becomes erratic or freezes near zero. (4) All three pitot-static instruments become unreliable or inoperative simultaneously. (5) The pilot loses accurate altitude reference, airspeed reference, and vertical speed information. Management: (1) If blockage is suspected (all three instruments suddenly become unreliable, or multiple instruments fail simultaneously), declare an emergency. (2) If the aircraft has an alternate static source, select it immediately (if available). (3) Divert to the nearest suitable aerodrome to land. (4) Use pitch attitude, power setting, and visual references to control the aircraft until landing. (5) Once on the ground, have the static system inspected for blockage, water, or damage. Prevention: check that static vent(s) are clear of obstruction during preflight; in winter, be aware that icing of the static vent is possible.
exam_tip: Static blockage: altimeter and VSI freeze, airspeed unreliable (may read high). All three instruments fail together. Select alternate static if available. Declare emergency, divert to nearest aerodrome.
key_facts: Blockage: ice, water, debris block static vent/tubing
Altimeter freezes at blockage moment
Airspeed becomes unreliable (may read very high)
VSI freezes near zero
All three instruments fail simultaneously
Select alternate static if available; declare emergency; divert to nearest aerodrome
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Pitot-static failures), POH emergency procedures, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.36.16

syllabus_desc: Given a sample deviation card, show how to apply corrections.
concept: The deviation card is mounted near the magnetic compass and shows the correction needed to convert from compass heading to magnetic heading (or vice versa) for various headings. Aircraft magnetic fields cause the compass to deviate from the actual magnetic heading  -  this deviation varies depending on which direction the aircraft is pointing. To steer a desired magnetic heading, the pilot looks up the deviation correction for that heading and applies it to find the compass heading to steer. For example, if deviation on heading 090° is +2°, steer 092° on the compass to fly 090° magnetic.
exam_tip: The deviation card tells you how much the compass is 'off' on each heading. Apply the correction to convert between magnetic heading and compass heading.
key_facts: Deviation card mounted near compass
Shows compass-to-magnetic correction for each heading
Deviation varies by heading
Apply correction to find compass heading to steer
Regularly updated during compass swing maintenance
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (deviation), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.38.2

syllabus_desc: Briefly: Describe the construction of a present-day direct-reading compass
concept: A direct-reading magnetic compass (magnetic heading indicator) is a simple compass consisting of: (1) Compass card: a circular disk marked with magnetic compass headings (N, E, S, W and numbered 0-360°). (2) Magnetic needles: two or more permanent magnets attached to the underside of the compass card that align with the Earth's magnetic field. (3) Pivot: a needle point or jeweled bearing that allows the compass card and magnet to rotate freely. (4) Compass bowl: a glass or plastic container filled with magnetic liquid (typically a mixture of alcohol and water) that dampens oscillations and reduces card spin. (5) Lubber line: a fixed line or mark on the compass housing (pointing forward toward the aircraft nose) that aligns with the compass card to show the aircraft's heading. The liquid in the compass bowl also provides buoyancy, reducing friction on the pivot and allowing smooth rotation. The compass responds directly to changes in the Earth's magnetic field and the aircraft's orientation relative to that field. It requires no electrical power and is highly reliable, making it an essential backup to the gyroscopic heading indicator.
exam_tip: Compass card with magnetic needles rotates freely in liquid-filled bowl. Lubber line shows heading. Simple, no power required. Oscillates; requires damping.
key_facts: Compass card: marked with magnetic headings (0-360°)
Magnetic needles attached to underside
Pivot point allows free rotation
Compass bowl: liquid-filled (alcohol/water mix)
Liquid provides damping and buoyancy
Lubber line: fixed line pointing forward (shows heading)
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Navigation instruments), POH, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.38.4

syllabus_desc: Briefly: Define lubber line
concept: The lubber line is a fixed reference mark or line on the compass housing (typically painted or engraved) that always points toward the aircraft's nose (forward direction). As the aircraft rotates, the lubber line stays fixed relative to the aircraft. The compass card inside rotates to align with the Earth's magnetic field. The heading is read by observing which number on the compass card aligns with the lubber line. For example: if the compass card shows 180° aligned with the lubber line, the aircraft is heading south (180°); if 270° aligns with the lubber line, the aircraft is heading west (270°). The lubber line is essential because it provides a fixed reference point from which to read the compass heading. Without it, the pilot would be trying to read the compass card (which rotates) without knowing which direction the aircraft is pointed. The lubber line effectively translates the compass card rotation into a heading readout in the aircraft's reference frame.
exam_tip: Lubber line: fixed mark on compass housing, always points forward (aircraft nose). Aligns with compass card to show heading. Without lubber line: heading cannot be read.
key_facts: Fixed reference line or mark on compass housing
Always points toward aircraft nose (forward)
Aligns with rotating compass card
Heading read where compass card number meets lubber line
Essential for reading compass in aircraft reference frame
Remains fixed as aircraft and compass card rotate
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Magnetic compass design), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.38.4

syllabus_desc: Briefly: The functions of the fluid in the compass bowl
concept: The liquid (fluid) in the compass bowl serves several critical functions: (1) Damping: the liquid provides viscous resistance to the compass card's motion, reducing oscillations and preventing the card from spinning wildly with every aircraft movement. Without damping, the compass card would oscillate continuously (especially during banking turns), making the heading impossible to read. (2) Buoyancy: the liquid provides buoyancy, supporting part of the weight of the compass card and magnetic needles. This reduces friction on the pivot point and allows the compass to rotate freely and respond quickly to heading changes. (3) Thermal expansion: as temperature changes, the liquid expands and contracts. The compass bowl must be designed to accommodate this expansion (often a rubber diaphragm in the bowl allows expansion/contraction) to prevent the bowl from cracking or leaking. (4) Prevents corrosion: the liquid (typically alcohol-water mix) prevents water and air corrosion of the magnetic needles and internal components. The liquid must be kept sealed inside the bowl; if the bowl cracks or develops a leak, water and air can enter, causing the magnetic needles to corrode and the compass to become unreliable. Regular inspection of the compass bowl for cracks or leaks is part of aircraft maintenance.
exam_tip: Compass liquid: dampens oscillations (prevents wild spinning), provides buoyancy (reduces friction), accommodates thermal expansion, prevents corrosion. Cracked bowl = compass unreliable.
key_facts: Damping: viscous resistance stops oscillations
Buoyancy: supports card weight, reduces pivot friction
Allows free rotation and quick response to heading changes
Thermal expansion: liquid expands/contracts with temperature
Rubber diaphragm accommodates expansion
Prevents corrosion of magnets and components
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Magnetic compass design), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.38.4

syllabus_desc: With respect to magnetic dip: Describe what it is
concept: Magnetic dip (or magnetic inclination): the Earth's magnetic field is not horizontal; it tilts downward in the northern hemisphere and upward in the southern hemisphere (or vice versa, depending on hemisphere). The angle of this tilt varies with geographic latitude. In New Zealand (southern hemisphere), the magnetic field dips downward toward the south at an angle of about 60-65° from horizontal. This dip causes the magnetic compass needles to experience a downward (vertical) force component in addition to the horizontal orienting force. Symptoms: when the aircraft is in a level turn, especially a turn toward the north (in the northern hemisphere) or south (in the southern hemisphere), the magnetic dip causes the compass to give a false reading (compass oscillates or shows incorrect heading). Compensation: aircraft magnetic compasses have compensating magnets (small permanent magnets) mounted inside the compass bowl to counteract the vertical component of the Earth's magnetic field. These compensators are adjusted so that the effective magnetic field is horizontal, allowing the compass to read accurately in level flight. The compensation is latitude-dependent: a compass compensated for one geographic latitude may be inaccurate at a different latitude. Periodically, the compass should be recalibrated or compensated by a qualified technician to ensure accuracy across all headings and geographic regions of operation.
exam_tip: Magnetic dip: Earth's field tilts (down in N-hemisphere, up in S). Causes false compass readings especially in north/south turns. Compensating magnets counteract dip for accurate heading.
key_facts: Earth's magnetic field not horizontal; tilts with latitude
In N-hemisphere: dip downward (south); in S: dip upward (north)
Dip angle: ~60-65° in New Zealand
Causes compass oscillation in north/south turns (N-hem) or south turns (S-hem)
Compensating magnets mounted in compass bowl
Counteract dip; maintain horizontal effective field
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Magnetic compass compensation), POH, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.38.6

syllabus_desc: With respect to magnetic dip: State how it is compensated for
concept: Compensation for magnetic dip is achieved by installing compensating magnets (also called corrector magnets or dip correctors) inside the compass bowl, typically positioned to apply a force that exactly opposes the vertical component of the Earth's magnetic field. Design approach: (1) In the northern hemisphere, a compensating magnet oriented north-south is installed to apply a downward force that cancels the downward dip of the Earth's field. (2) In the southern hemisphere, the compensating magnet (or its polarity) is adjusted to apply an upward force. (3) Adjustment: the strength and position of the compensating magnets are tweaked by a compass technician during installation or maintenance to ensure the effective magnetic field is horizontal. The technician may rotate the compensator or install shims to fine-tune the correction. (4) Latitude-specific: the degree of dip changes with latitude, so a compass compensated for one latitude may not be perfectly compensated at a very different latitude. Aircraft operating across a wide latitude range may experience small compass errors at latitudes far from the original compensation point. For precision, the compass can be recompensated if the aircraft operates primarily in a new geographic region. Modern aircraft have compass cards that are periodically swung (rotated through all headings while the aircraft is parked on a known heading reference) to verify accuracy and adjust if needed.
exam_tip: Compensating magnets mounted in compass bowl oppose vertical dip component. Adjusted so effective field is horizontal. Compensation latitude-specific; may require recalibration in new region.
key_facts: Compensating magnets: small permanent magnets in compass bowl
Position and polarity adjusted to oppose vertical dip
Result: effective magnetic field becomes horizontal
Technician adjusts during installation or maintenance
Compensation is latitude-specific
Aircraft operating far from original latitude may need recalibration
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Magnetic compass compensation), POH, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.38.6

syllabus_desc: With respect to magnetic dip: Define residual dip
concept: Residual dip (or residual error): despite compensation efforts, a small amount of dip error typically remains in a magnetic compass. This is the unwanted vertical component of the Earth's magnetic field that could not be fully cancelled by the compensating magnets. Residual dip causes: (1) Acceleration error (or start-up error): when the aircraft accelerates from a constant heading, the compass gives a false reading that gradually corrects as speed stabilises. During acceleration northbound (in the N-hemisphere), the compass shows false easterly and westerly oscillations before settling to the correct northbound heading. (2) Turning error: when the aircraft turns (especially toward cardinal headings north or south), the compass gives erratic or false readings. The compass may swing ahead of or lag behind the actual turn. (3) Bank errors: in a banked turn, the residual dip causes additional compass errors. These errors are less pronounced in shallow banks and more pronounced in steep banks. Management: pilots must be aware of these compass errors and apply corrections: (1) Avoid relying solely on the magnetic compass during turns; use the heading indicator (gyroscopic compass) instead, which is more stable. (2) After completing a turn, allow time for the magnetic compass to settle before reading the heading. (3) Cross-check magnetic compass against the heading indicator to detect large errors. (4) If residual dip errors are very large, the compass should be professionally swung and recompensated.
exam_tip: Residual dip: small uncorrected vertical magnetic component causes acceleration error (false heading during speed changes) and turning error (erratic during turns). Use heading indicator; allow compass to settle.
key_facts: Small vertical magnetic component remains after compensation
Acceleration error: false heading during speed changes
Example: accelerating north shows false easterly/westerly swings
Turning error: erratic readings during turns
Bank error: steeper banks cause larger errors
Management: use heading indicator during turns, allow compass to settle
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Magnetic compass errors), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.38.6

syllabus_desc: State the effects of: Acceleration error
concept: Acceleration error (or start-up error) is a false heading indication that occurs when the aircraft accelerates or decelerates. Cause: residual magnetic dip (the small uncorrected vertical component of the Earth's magnetic field) causes the magnetic compass needles to experience a vertical force during acceleration or deceleration. When the aircraft accelerates, inertial forces (pseudo-forces in the aircraft frame) interact with this residual dip, causing the compass card to tilt or oscillate, producing a momentary false heading. Symptoms in the northern hemisphere: during northbound acceleration, the compass initially shows false easterly and westerly swings before settling to the correct north heading. The opposite occurs during southbound acceleration. Example: intending to maintain a northbound heading (000°), the pilot begins to accelerate. Momentarily, the compass swings to show easterly headings (090°) or westerly headings (270°) before settling back to 000°. Management: (1) Be aware that the compass will show transient errors during speed changes. (2) Do not overcontrol based on compass readings during acceleration or deceleration; allow time for the compass to stabilise. (3) Use the heading indicator (gyroscopic compass) instead, which is free from acceleration errors. (4) Once at constant speed and steady heading, the magnetic compass will settle to the correct value. Acceleration error is typically small in light aircraft but becomes more pronounced in high-performance aircraft with rapid acceleration.
exam_tip: Acceleration error: false heading during speed changes (especially toward north/south in northern hemisphere). Do not overcontrol; use heading indicator instead. Compass settles once speed constant.
key_facts: Residual dip causes vertical force during acceleration
Compass card oscillates, giving false heading
North acceleration: shows false east/west swings
South acceleration: opposite error
Transient effect: disappears once speed is constant
Do not overcontrol; use heading indicator during speed changes
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Magnetic compass errors), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.38.8

syllabus_desc: State the effects of: Turning error
concept: Turning error is false heading indication that occurs during aircraft turns, especially toward north or south (in the northern hemisphere; south or north in the southern hemisphere). Cause: during a banked turn, the aircraft's vertical axis tilts. The residual magnetic dip (vertical component of Earth's magnetic field) then has a horizontal component in the aircraft's reference frame, which causes the compass card to respond incorrectly to the turn. Symptoms: (1) When turning toward north (northern hemisphere), the compass lags behind the actual turn and shows a heading more southerly than actual. (2) When turning toward south, the compass leads the turn and shows a heading more northerly than actual. (3) When turning toward east or west, the turning error is minimal. (4) The error is more pronounced in steeper banks and tighter turns. Example: maintaining a northbound heading (000°) and beginning a right turn toward east (090°), the compass initially lags and may show only 060° while the actual heading is 080°. As the turn continues toward east, the compass error diminishes. Management: (1) Avoid relying on the magnetic compass during turns, especially turns toward north or south. (2) Use the heading indicator (gyroscopic compass) to control the turn; the heading indicator is free from turning error. (3) After the turn is complete and the aircraft is in level flight at the new heading, allow the magnetic compass time to settle (typically 5-10 seconds) before reading the new heading. (4) Cross-check magnetic compass with heading indicator.
exam_tip: Turning error: compass lags during turn toward north, leads toward south (hemisphere-dependent). Compass more erratic in steep banks. Use heading indicator during turns; allow compass to settle after.
key_facts: Residual dip causes error during banked turns
Especially pronounced toward north/south headings
Toward north (N-hem): compass lags (shows too-south heading)
Toward south: compass leads (shows too-north heading)
Toward east/west: minimal error
Steeper banks cause larger errors
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Magnetic compass errors), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.38.8

syllabus_desc: State the compass pre-flight serviceability checks, and the precautions when carrying magnetic items in an aircraft.
concept: Pre-flight: check compass fluid is clear (no bubbles or discolouration), compass swings freely, lubber line is intact, lighting works (if fitted), and compass reads approximately correct heading when compared with a known reference. Precautions: keep magnetic items (mobile phones, tablets, tools, keys, headsets with magnets) away from the compass  -  they can cause deviation errors. The deviation card must be current (checked during compass swing). Secure all magnetic items in the cockpit during flight to prevent them moving closer to the compass.
exam_tip: Check fluid, freedom of movement, and approximate heading during preflight. Keep phones and magnetic items away from the compass  -  they cause deviation errors.
key_facts: Check fluid is clear with no bubbles
Verify compass swings freely
Confirm lubber line intact
Compare reading with known reference
Keep magnetic items away from compass
Mobile phones, tablets, tools can cause deviation
Ensure deviation card is current
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (compass checks), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.38.10

syllabus_desc: Outline the basic principle of operation of the vacuum system.
concept: The vacuum system provides suction to spin the gyroscopes in the attitude indicator, heading indicator, and (in some aircraft) the turn coordinator. An engine-driven vacuum pump creates suction (typically 3-4.5 inches of mercury) that draws filtered air through the instrument cases, spinning the gyro rotors at high speed. The suction gauge in the cockpit indicates vacuum system pressure  -  a reading below the green arc indicates pump failure or leak, and gyro instruments will become unreliable.
exam_tip: Vacuum pump spins the gyros. Normal suction: 3-4.5 hPa. Low suction = pump failure = gyros will slowly become unreliable.
key_facts: Engine-driven vacuum pump creates suction
Normal suction: 3-4.5 inches of mercury
Filtered air spins gyro rotors at high speed
Powers attitude indicator, heading indicator, and sometimes turn coordinator
Suction gauge monitors vacuum system health
Low suction = pump failure, gyros become unreliable
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (vacuum system), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.40.2

syllabus_desc: State the likely effects of reduced or nil suction in the vacuum system.
concept: If the vacuum pump fails or suction is lost, the gyroscope rotors gradually slow down and lose their reference. The attitude indicator (AI) will slowly tilt and give false pitch and bank indications. The heading indicator (DGI) will precess and drift from the correct heading. These instruments become unreliable within 5-10 minutes of vacuum loss but may initially appear to work normally  -  this makes vacuum failure insidious. If vacuum is lost in instrument meteorological conditions (IMC), declare an emergency and divert to VFR conditions immediately.
exam_tip: Vacuum failure is insidious  -  gyros seem to work for several minutes before becoming obviously wrong. In IMC, this is extremely dangerous. Always monitor the suction gauge.
key_facts: Gyro rotors gradually slow down after vacuum loss
AI gives false pitch and bank indications
DGI drifts from correct heading
Instruments may appear normal initially (insidious failure)
Unreliable within 5-10 minutes
In IMC: declare emergency, divert to VFR immediately
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (vacuum failure), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.40.4

syllabus_desc: Describe the gyroscopic properties of: Rigidity
concept: Rigidity (or gyroscopic inertia) is the property of a spinning gyroscope to maintain its plane of spin (axis of rotation) fixed in space unless acted upon by an external force. A spinning rotor, once set to spin around an axis, resists changes to that axis due to its rotational inertia. In aircraft navigation and flight control instruments, the gyroscope is spun at very high speed (typically 10,000-20,000 RPM). Once spinning, the rotor maintains its orientation in space relative to the inertial reference frame (Earth), not relative to the aircraft. This property is used in the heading indicator: the rotor spins in a vertical plane, maintaining its orientation in space. As the aircraft turns, the rotor's axis remains fixed in space (pointing toward the same direction in space), but the aircraft rotates around it. The difference between the rotor's fixed axis and the aircraft's rotating fuselage is measured and displayed as the aircraft's heading. Rigidity also applies to the attitude indicator (artificial horizon): a gyroscope spinning in a horizontal plane maintains its horizontal orientation, providing a stable reference for pitch and bank attitudes even during aircraft turns. Without rigidity, the gyroscope would tumble and be useless as a flight reference.
exam_tip: Rigidity: spinning gyro maintains axis of spin fixed in space. Does not rotate with aircraft. Provides stable reference for heading and attitude independent of aircraft movement.
key_facts: Spinning rotor resists changes to its axis (rotational inertia)
Rotor speed: typically 10,000-20,000 RPM
Axis remains fixed in inertial space, not aircraft space
Heading indicator: vertical-spin rotor maintains heading reference
Attitude indicator: horizontal-spin rotor maintains pitch/bank reference
Essential for stable flight instrument reference
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Gyroscopic principles), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.40.6

syllabus_desc: Describe the gyroscopic properties of: Precession
concept: Precession is the property of a spinning gyroscope to respond to an applied force in a direction perpendicular to both the force and the axis of spin. When a force is applied to a gyroscope, the rotor does not move in the direction of the applied force; instead, it moves 90° to that direction in the plane of rotation. In aircraft flight instruments, precession is used to measure and display angular motion: (1) Heading indicator (gyroscopic heading): when the aircraft turns, the magnetic compass signal or other heading reference applies a precession force to the gyro. This force causes the rotor axis to precess (gradually shift its orientation) until it realigns with the sensed heading direction. The amount of precession indicates the turn angle. (2) Turn coordinator/turn indicator: when the aircraft rolls (banks), a sensor detects the rolling motion and applies a force to a canted gyroscope rotor. Precession causes the rotor axis to precess, and a needle indicates the direction and rate of turn. Precession is not instantaneous; there is a built-in lag as the rotor slowly precesses in response to applied forces. This lag is acceptable in flight instruments because it provides damping (reduces oscillation). However, high-speed maneuvers or very rapid turns can exceed the precession rate, causing temporary lag errors in the instruments.
exam_tip: Precession: gyro responds to applied force perpendicular to the force. In heading indicator: magnetic compass force causes gyro to precess, indicating turn. Built-in lag provides damping.
key_facts: Applied force causes rotor to move 90° perpendicular
Not instantaneous; there is lag as rotor precesses
Heading indicator: magnetic compass force → precession → heading update
Turn indicator: turn force → precession → turn indication
Lag provides damping (reduces oscillations)
High-speed turns may exceed precession rate (lag error)
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Gyroscopic principles), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.40.6

syllabus_desc: With respect to the turn indicator/coordinator: Explain the basic principle of a rate gyroscope
concept: A rate gyroscope measures the aircraft's angular velocity (rate of turn) rather than maintaining a fixed orientation like a directional gyroscope. The rate gyroscope rotor spins in a horizontal plane. When the aircraft turns, forces are applied to the rotor's axis. Precession of the rotor causes a needle on a gauge to deflect in proportion to the aircraft's turn rate. The faster the aircraft turns, the more precession occurs, and the more the needle deflects. Rate gyroscopes are used in turn indicators and turn coordinators. The output (needle position) shows: (1) The direction of turn (left or right). (2) The rate of turn (how fast; the needle indicates rate in degrees per second, typically 0-3 degrees per second in light aircraft). The rate gyroscope measures instantaneous turn rate and does not accumulate errors like a heading-maintaining gyroscope (which can suffer from precession drift over time). For this reason, the rate gyroscope is useful for monitoring turn rate in IMC when the pilot cannot see the horizon. The rate gyroscope does not directly indicate heading; it only indicates whether and how fast the aircraft is turning.
exam_tip: Rate gyroscope measures turn rate (angular velocity). Needle deflects proportional to turn speed. Shows direction (left/right) and rate (degrees/second). Does not indicate heading.
key_facts: Measures angular velocity (rate of turn)
Horizontal-spin rotor with precession output
Needle deflects in proportion to turn rate
Shows turn direction (left or right)
Typical range: 0-3 degrees per second
Useful for IMC when horizon not visible
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Turn indicator design), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.40.8

syllabus_desc: With respect to the turn indicator/coordinator: Differentiate between the different indications of the turn indicator and turn coordinator
concept: Turn indicator (rate-of-turn indicator): measures angular velocity (rate of turn) and displays it on a needle gauge. The instrument shows only the turn rate (degrees per second), not the bank angle or coordination. A turn indicator needle shows the aircraft is turning at, for example, 2 degrees per second, but it does not show whether the aircraft is properly banked or if the turn is coordinated with the correct rate of roll. Turn coordinator: a more advanced instrument that combines turn rate indication with a bank-angle indicator (sometimes called a miniature airplane or stick-and-ball indicator). The turn coordinator needle shows turn rate, AND a small symbolic aircraft (bank indicator) shows the aircraft's roll attitude (bank angle). Additionally, many turn coordinators include a ball (coordination ball or slip-skid indicator) that moves left or right to indicate whether the turn is coordinated. A coordinated turn is one where the rate of roll matches the rate of turn (no slip or skid). Comparison: (1) Turn indicator: turn rate only. (2) Turn coordinator: turn rate + bank angle + slip/skid indication. Modern aircraft increasingly use more sophisticated attitude indicators (artificial horizon) that show pitch and bank angles directly. However, turn indicators and turn coordinators remain common in light aircraft as essential backup instruments for IMC flight.
exam_tip: Turn indicator: shows turn rate only (needle). Turn coordinator: shows turn rate + bank angle (symbol) + slip/skid ball. Coordinator more informative.
key_facts: Turn indicator: turn rate (degrees/second) only
Turn coordinator: turn rate + bank angle (miniature airplane symbol)
Turn coordinator: includes slip/skid ball (coordination check)
Slip/skid ball: left/right indicates uncoordinated turn
Coordinated turn: no slip/skid, rate of roll matches turn rate
Turn coordinator more informative; used in modern light aircraft
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Turn instruments), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.40.8

syllabus_desc: With respect to the turn indicator/coordinator: State the function, indication and correct use of the coordination (balance) ball
concept: The coordination ball (or slip-skid ball, balance ball) is a small weighted ball that moves left or right inside a curved glass tube mounted beneath the turn needle on a turn coordinator. The ball responds to the combined effects of gravity and centripetal acceleration during aircraft turns. Function: the ball indicates whether the aircraft is in a properly coordinated turn (no slip or skid). In a coordinated turn: (1) The rate of roll matches the rate of turn (the aircraft banks at the correct angle for the turn rate). (2) The centripetal acceleration (from the turn) and gravity combine at the proper ratio. (3) The balance ball remains centered in the tube (neutral position). In an uncoordinated turn (slip): (1) The aircraft is rolled too little for the turn rate (banked too shallowly). (2) The centripetal acceleration is insufficient. (3) Gravity dominates, and the ball rolls toward the inside of the turn (toward the lower side of the tube). In an uncoordinated turn (skid): (1) The aircraft is rolled too much for the turn rate (banked too steeply). (2) The centripetal acceleration is excessive. (3) The ball rolls toward the outside of the turn (toward the upper side of the tube). Pilot technique: during a turn in IMC, the pilot monitors the coordination ball to ensure the turn is coordinated. If the ball is off-centre, the pilot adjusts the bank angle: if the ball is toward the inside, increase bank angle (roll more); if toward the outside, decrease bank angle (roll less) until the ball is centred.
exam_tip: Balance ball: centered = coordinated turn. Ball rolls inside (low) = slip (not enough bank). Ball rolls outside (high) = skid (too much bank). Pilot corrects bank angle to centre ball.
key_facts: Small weighted ball in curved tube
Responds to gravity and centripetal acceleration
Centered: coordinated turn
Rolls toward inside (low): slip (insufficient bank)
Rolls toward outside (high): skid (excessive bank)
Pilot adjusts bank angle to centre ball
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Turn coordinator design), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.40.8

syllabus_desc: With respect to the attitude indicator (or artificial horizon) explain: The basic principle of operation (earth gyroscope)
concept: An attitude indicator (artificial horizon, attitude gyro) displays the aircraft's pitch attitude (nose up/down) and bank angle (left/right wing position) relative to the actual horizon. The instrument contains a rapidly spinning gyroscope rotor that is mounted in gimbals (rotating supports) that allow the rotor axis to rotate in two perpendicular planes (pitch and roll). The rotor maintains a horizontal orientation due to its rigidity property. Inside the gyroscope assembly, the horizon line is painted or displayed on a rotating drum. As the aircraft pitches (nose up or down), the gyro remains horizontal (due to rigidity), and the drum (representing the horizon) appears to move down or up relative to a fixed aircraft symbol (small airplane or needle indicator). This motion creates the illusion that the horizon is moving, when in reality the aircraft is rotating around the gyroscope. Similarly, when the aircraft banks (rolls left or right), the gyro axis remains horizontal, but the drum rotates to show the aircraft's bank angle. A bank angle indicator (a line on the drum) shows the roll attitude: if the line is vertical, the aircraft is upright; if tilted, the aircraft is banked. The attitude indicator provides stable pitch and bank references independent of aircraft motion, making it essential for flight in instrument conditions (IMC) when the natural horizon is not visible.
exam_tip: Attitude indicator (artificial horizon): spinning gyro maintains horizontal reference. Drum shows horizon line relative to aircraft symbol. Displays pitch (nose up/down) and bank angle. Essential for IMC flight.
key_facts: Spinning gyroscope in gimbals
Rotor maintains horizontal orientation (rigidity)
Horizon line painted on rotating drum
Pitch motion: horizon appears to move up/down relative to aircraft symbol
Roll motion: bank angle line shows aircraft tilt
Provides stable pitch and bank reference
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Attitude indicator design), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.40.10

syllabus_desc: With respect to the attitude indicator (or artificial horizon) explain: How pitch attitude and bank angle are displayed
concept: Pitch attitude: displayed by the vertical position of the horizon line (the thin line painted on the rotating drum) relative to a fixed aircraft symbol (typically a small airplane or needle) at the centre of the instrument face. If the horizon line is above the aircraft symbol, the aircraft is pitched nose-down; if below, the aircraft is pitched nose-up. The amount of displacement from the centre corresponds to the aircraft's pitch angle. Graduations or tick marks above and below the horizon line show pitch angles in 5° increments (typical). The pilot can read the pitch attitude: if the horizon line is 15° below the aircraft symbol, the aircraft is pitched 15° nose-up. Bank angle: displayed by the bank angle indicator (typically a thick line or pointer on the drum, or a separate index on the instrument face) that shows the aircraft's roll attitude. The bank angle line tilts as the aircraft banks. Graduations on the bank angle indicator show bank angles (typically at 10°, 20°, 30°, 60° intervals). The pilot can read the bank angle: if the bank line is tilted 20° to the right of vertical, the aircraft is banked 20° to the right. The combination of pitch attitude (from horizon position) and bank angle (from bank indicator) gives the pilot a complete picture of the aircraft's spatial orientation relative to the true horizon, which is essential for safe flight in IMC when the actual horizon is not visible.
exam_tip: Pitch: horizon line position relative to aircraft symbol (above = down, below = up). Bank: line tilt (vertical = upright, tilted = banked). Graduations show angles in degrees.
key_facts: Pitch attitude: horizon line position
Horizon above symbol: aircraft pitched down
Horizon below symbol: aircraft pitched up
Pitch graduations: 5° increments
Bank angle: line or pointer tilt from vertical
Bank graduations: 10°, 20°, 30°, 60° marks
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Attitude indicator display), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.40.10

syllabus_desc: With respect to the attitude indicator (or artificial horizon) explain: The pilot checks for serviceability
concept: Serviceability checks for the attitude indicator: (1) Preflight (before flight): (a) Observe the instrument display with the aircraft at rest on the ground. The horizon line should be approximately at the centre (representing level flight), and the bank angle indicator should show zero tilt (upright). If the horizon is significantly off-centre or tilted, the gyro may be misaligned or the instrument may be faulty. (b) If the aircraft has a power-on test or self-check function (common on modern electronic attitude indicators), activate it and verify the instrument performs the test correctly. (2) Early in flight (initial climb, low altitude): (a) Establish straight-and-level flight at low altitude where the natural horizon is visible. Cross-check the attitude indicator display against the natural horizon outside the windscreen. The horizon line on the instrument should align approximately with the real horizon. (b) Bank the aircraft left and right, and verify the bank angle indicator on the attitude indicator matches the actual bank angle. (c) Pitch the aircraft up and down, and verify the horizon line on the indicator moves in the correct direction. (3) In flight (periodic checks): (a) Periodically glance at the attitude indicator to ensure it is displaying reasonable values consistent with pitch and bank inputs. (b) If the instrument display becomes erratic, shows tumbling, or becomes unresponsive, the gyro may have failed or tumbled (especially in conventional attitude indicators, not vacuum-powered models are less prone to tumbling). (c) If the attitude indicator appears to be malfunctioning, rely on the heading indicator and turn coordinator for attitude reference, and prepare to declare an emergency and descend to VMC if needed. A failed attitude indicator in IMC is a serious situation.
exam_tip: Preflight: horizon at centre, zero bank. In flight: cross-check against natural horizon. Verify pitch/bank responses match. If erratic/tumbled: rely on other instruments, prepare to descend to VMC.
key_facts: Preflight: horizon centred, zero bank shown
Initial flight: cross-check against natural horizon
Pitch up/down: horizon moves correctly
Bank left/right: bank indicator shows correct angle
In flight: monitor for erratic display or tumbling
