Failed indicator in IMC: serious; declare emergency, prepare to descend to VMC
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (Preflight and in-flight checks), POH normal and emergency procedures, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.40.10

syllabus_desc: With respect to the heading indicator (or DGI), explain the: Advantages of a gyroscopic heading indicator (versus a compass)
concept: The heading indicator (gyroscopic compass, directional gyro, DG) displays the aircraft's magnetic heading (direction) using a spinning gyroscope combined with a magnetic compass reference. Advantages over the magnetic compass: (1) Stability: the heading indicator is much more stable during turns, acceleration, and deceleration. Unlike the magnetic compass, which lags, leads, and oscillates during turns and speed changes, the heading indicator maintains a smooth, steady display. (2) Precision: the heading indicator displays heading to the nearest 1-5°, whereas the magnetic compass may oscillate ±10° or more during maneuvers. (3) Turn indication: the heading indicator shows heading changes clearly and immediately as the aircraft turns, without lags or errors. (4) Declutter: the heading indicator face is cleaner and easier to read than a magnetic compass card with its 360° numbers. (5) Reliability in turns: the heading indicator does not suffer from turning errors or acceleration errors (unlike the magnetic compass). (6) Heading maintenance: in level flight, the heading indicator holds a steady heading reference, making it ideal for maintaining a desired heading in flight. Disadvantages: (1) Requires power (electrical or vacuum, depending on gyro type). (2) Requires periodic synchronization with the magnetic compass (drift over time). (3) Can tumble or become unreliable in unusual attitudes (especially in conventional mechanical heading indicators). For these reasons, the heading indicator is the primary heading instrument in most aircraft, with the magnetic compass serving as a backup for verification and synchronization.
exam_tip: HI advantages: stable (no lag/lead in turns), precise (1-5°), no turning errors, easy to read, excellent for level flight. Disadvantages: requires power, drifts, can tumble in unusual attitudes.
key_facts: Stable during turns: no acceleration/turning errors
Precise: reads to 1-5° vs compass ±10°
Immediate turn indication: no lags
Easy to read: clean display
Ideal for level flight and heading maintenance
Requires power; periodically synchronized with compass
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Heading indicator), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.40.12

syllabus_desc: With respect to the heading indicator (or DGI), explain the: Need for, and method of synchronising the HI with the compass
concept: The heading indicator drifts over time due to precession errors and mechanical friction. The drift rate is typically 2-5° per 15 minutes of flight, but can be higher in turbulent conditions or on extended flights. Synchronization is necessary to ensure the heading indicator reading remains accurate and aligned with the aircraft's true magnetic heading. Synchronization method: (1) In level flight on a stable heading, compare the heading indicator reading with the magnetic compass reading. (2) If they differ, note the difference. Example: heading indicator shows 350°, but the magnetic compass shows 355°. (3) Adjust the heading indicator heading adjustment knob (typically located at the bottom or side of the instrument) to match the magnetic compass. Turn the knob in small increments until the heading indicator reading aligns with the magnetic compass. (4) Allow the compass to settle for a few seconds before reading (to account for compass oscillations). (5) Once aligned, the heading indicator will again begin to drift slowly. Periodic re-checks (every 15-30 minutes of flight) ensure continued accuracy. Synchronization should only be performed in straight-and-level flight when the magnetic compass is reliable (on stable east or west headings, not north or south where compass errors are largest). Avoid synchronizing during or immediately after turns when the compass may be oscillating.
exam_tip: HI drifts 2-5° per 15 min. Synchronize in level flight: adjust knob to match stable magnetic compass reading. Re-check every 15-30 min. Do not synchronize on north/south headings or during turns.
key_facts: Drift rate: 2-5° per 15 minutes of flight
Synchronize in straight-and-level flight
Compare HI reading with magnetic compass
Adjust HI knob to match compass
Allow compass to settle before reading
Re-synchronize every 15-30 minutes
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Heading indicator operation), POH normal procedures, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.40.12

syllabus_desc: With respect to the heading indicator (or DGI), explain the: Pilot checks for serviceability
concept: Serviceability checks for the heading indicator: (1) Preflight (on the ground): (a) Ensure the heading indicator display is illuminated (if equipped with backlighting). (b) Verify the heading shown on the instrument matches the aircraft's magnetic heading on the ground (approximately; use the magnetic compass as reference). If not, perform synchronization. (c) Rotate the heading adjustment knob and confirm the heading display changes smoothly and responds to the knob movement. (d) For vacuum-operated heading indicators, verify the vacuum supply gauge (if installed) reads in the green (normal range, typically 4.5-5.5 in. Hg). (2) In flight (initial ascent, low altitude): (a) Establish straight-and-level flight at low altitude with natural horizon visible. (b) Cross-check the heading indicator reading against the known magnetic heading (can be estimated from the natural horizon relative to the sun, or cross-check against the magnetic compass). (c) Execute a shallow turn left and right (20-30° bank), and verify the heading indicator responds smoothly and shows the correct heading change. (d) Verify the heading indicator does not tumble or become erratic during the turns. (3) In flight (periodic checks): (a) Every 15-30 minutes, compare the heading indicator with the magnetic compass and re-synchronize if drift is more than 5-10°. (b) Monitor the heading indicator for smooth operation and responsive changes. (c) If the heading indicator becomes erratic, shows excessive drift (>15° in a few minutes), or begins to tumble, the gyro may be failing. Reduce power, declare an emergency if necessary, and prepare to use the magnetic compass for heading reference. A failed heading indicator significantly impacts the pilot's ability to navigate in IMC, making it a critical instrument for IFR flight.
exam_tip: Preflight: display illuminated, synch with compass, knob responsive, vacuum green. In flight: cross-check against compass, verify turn response, re-synch every 15-30 min. Excessive drift/tumbling: emergency.
key_facts: Preflight: backlight on, matches compass, knob responsive
Vacuum indicator (if equipped) in green
In flight: cross-check against compass/horizon
Verify turns: smooth response, correct heading change
Monitor for tumble: if excessive, gyro failing
Re-synchronize every 15-30 minutes
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4 (In-flight instrument checks), POH normal and emergency procedures, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.40.12

syllabus_desc: Briefly explain the errors likely to occur if: The gyro rotor rpm is low
concept: An electrically-driven gyroscope (such as a vacuum-powered gyro in some aircraft or an electric motor-driven gyro in others) requires the rotor to spin at a minimum speed to maintain adequate rigidity and precession properties. Low rotor RPM causes: (1) Reduced rigidity: a slowly spinning rotor does not have sufficient rotational inertia to maintain its axis fixed in space. The axis becomes prone to drift and misalignment. In a heading indicator, a slow rotor drifts rapidly (more than the normal 2-5° per 15 minutes). In an attitude indicator, a slow rotor may not maintain horizontal orientation, causing the displayed pitch and bank to become inaccurate. (2) Reduced precession: a slowly spinning rotor precesses slowly. In a heading indicator, the heading may not update quickly in response to turns; the needle lags the actual heading. (3) Poor responsiveness: the instruments become sluggish and unresponsive to aircraft motion. (4) Instrument failure: if RPM drops below the minimum operating threshold, the gyroscope ceases to function as a reliable instrument. Causes of low rotor RPM: (1) Vacuum or electrical power insufficient (vacuum system problem, electrical supply problem). (2) Gyroscope bearing wear or friction (aging instrument). (3) Bearing lubrication failure (no oil circulation). Prevention and management: (1) Verify gyro vacuum or electrical power supply is adequate during preflight. (2) During flight, if gyro instruments show signs of slow response, sluggish movement, or excessive drift, suspect low rotor RPM. (3) Check vacuum gauge (if vacuum-operated) or electrical power supply indicators. (4) If low RPM is confirmed, the affected gyro instruments may become unreliable. Declare an emergency if needed and prepare to rely on alternative instruments or descent to VMC.
exam_tip: Low gyro RPM: reduced rigidity (rapid drift), poor precession (sluggish response), instruments become unreliable. Causes: low vacuum/power, bearing wear. Check vacuum gauge; if low, gyro failing.
key_facts: Low RPM reduces rigidity: axis drifts, not fixed in space
Heading indicator: drifts >5° per 15 min
Attitude indicator: cannot maintain horizontal reference
Low precession: sluggish response to turns
Causes: low vacuum/power, bearing wear, lubrication failure
Management: check vacuum/power, prepare for emergency
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Gyroscope operation), POH, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.40.14

syllabus_desc: Briefly explain the errors likely to occur if: There is an indication of a power failure on an electrically-driven gyro
concept: An electrically-driven gyroscope requires continuous electrical power to maintain rotor spin speed. Power failure causes the rotor to slow down and eventually stop spinning, rendering the gyro instrument inoperable. Indications of power failure on an electrically-driven gyro: (1) Instrument stops responding: the heading indicator or attitude indicator (whichever uses the electric gyro) becomes unresponsive to aircraft motion. The needle or display freezes at whatever value it showed when power was lost. (2) Drift indicator: some gyro instruments have a flag or indicator that appears when the rotor RPM drops below the minimum operating speed. This flag may say "OFF" or show a red warning symbol. (3) Gradual decay: if power is lost suddenly (e.g., circuit breaker pops), the rotor decays quickly. If there is partial power loss or intermittent power, the rotor may gradually slow, causing sluggish or erratic instrument behavior before complete failure. (4) Loss of instrument reference: once the gyro rotor has stopped, the instrument no longer provides pitch, bank, or heading reference (depending on the instrument type). The pilot loses the primary reference instrument for IMC flight. Distinction from vacuum-powered gyros: vacuum-driven gyroscopes typically fail more gradually (vacuum pump deteriorates or vacuum line blockage) and may show signs (vacuum gauge) before complete failure. Electrically-driven gyros may fail more abruptly if electrical power is suddenly lost (circuit breaker trip, loose connector). Management: (1) Check electrical system: ammeter, voltmeter, and specific circuit breaker for the gyro. (2) If circuit breaker has popped, check for electrical overload (short circuit). (3) If electrical power is lost and cannot be restored, the gyro is inoperable. Rely on alternative instruments or declare an emergency and prepare to descend to VMC.
exam_tip: Electric gyro power failure: rotor stops spinning, instrument freezes, flag may appear (OFF/red warning). Instrument becomes reference for heading/attitude. Declare emergency, prepare to descend to VMC.
key_facts: Power loss stops rotor; instrument freezes
No heading or attitude reference available
Flag or warning indicator may appear
Distinct from vacuum gyros: electric failure often abrupt
Check electrical system: ammeter, voltmeter, circuit breaker
If power lost and unreplaceable: prepare to descend to VMC
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.5 (Gyroscope electrical failure), POH emergency procedures, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.40.14

syllabus_desc: Describe the indications of toppling.
concept: Gyro toppling occurs when a gyroscope is forced beyond its operating limits (typically by extreme manoeuvres, aerobatics, or operating the instrument before the gyro is fully spun up). When toppled, the gyroscope loses its reference and gives completely erroneous indications  -  the AI may show random pitch and bank, and the DGI may spin wildly. Recovery requires re-erecting the gyro, which can take several minutes. The AI has a built-in erection mechanism, but it operates slowly. Until the gyro re-erects, the instrument cannot be trusted.
exam_tip: Toppled gyro = completely wrong indications (random pitch, bank, heading). Can happen from extreme manoeuvres or using instruments before spin-up. Takes several minutes to re-erect.
key_facts: Toppling: gyro forced beyond operating limits
Causes: extreme manoeuvres, aerobatics, premature use before spin-up
Indications: random pitch/bank on AI, spinning DGI
Built-in erection mechanism re-erects slowly (several minutes)
Cannot trust instrument until re-erected
Avoid extreme manoeuvres when relying on gyro instruments
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.7 (gyro toppling), NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.40.16

syllabus_desc: Describe the basic principles and operation of a GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System).
concept: GNSS uses signals from orbiting satellites to determine precise position, velocity and time. At least 24 satellites are distributed in six orbital planes at 20,200 km altitude. The receiver calculates position by measuring signal travel time from multiple satellites (trilateration). GPS, GLONASS, Galileo and BeiDou are GNSS systems. Accuracy typically 5-10m in civilian mode.
exam_tip: GNSS needs minimum 4 satellites for 3D position fix (3 for 2D).
key_facts: Constellation of 24+ satellites
Trilateration method
Atomic clocks on satellites
Ground augmentation systems
Accuracy 5-10m civilian
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.42.2

syllabus_desc: Describe the limitations and failure indications of a GNSS system.
concept: GNSS limitations include: signal blockage in tunnels/under trees, multipath errors from signal reflection, atmospheric ionospheric delays, poor satellite geometry (high dilution of precision/DOP), and intentional signal degradation. Failure indications: loss of signal, jumping position fixes, high DOP values, no GPS fix when previously locked, and integrity warnings. Cold start initialization takes 30+ seconds; warm start 15 seconds.
exam_tip: High DOP indicates poor satellite geometry; avoid flying under poor DOP conditions.
key_facts: Signal blockage/multipath errors
Ionospheric delays
Dilution of Precision (DOP)
Cold/warm/hot start times
Loss of signal indication
Receiver integrity warning
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.42.4

syllabus_desc: Briefly describe the basic function and operation of TCAS.
concept: Traffic Collision Avoidance System uses aircraft transponders to detect nearby aircraft and predict collision. TCAS I provides Traffic Advisories (TA) to increase crew awareness. TCAS II adds Resolution Advisories (RA) commanding vertical maneuvers to avoid collision. Range up to 40 nautical miles. System uses distance, altitude and closure rate to determine threat level. Operates independently of ground control.
exam_tip: TCAS II provides climb/descend commands (RA); TCAS I only warns.
key_facts: Two TCAS levels (I and II)
TA and RA advisories
40nm detection range
Transponder required
Closure rate calculation
Vertical avoidance only
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.44.2

syllabus_desc: Briefly describe the basic function and operation of TAWS (Terrain Awareness and Warning System).
concept: TAWS prevents controlled flight into terrain (CFIT) using terrain database, GPS altitude, barometric altitude, aircraft attitude and radio altimeter. System displays terrain on cockpit display and provides aural warnings when aircraft approaches high terrain or descends below safe altitude for location. Modes include enveloping, display and combined modes. Forward-looking capability predicts terrain hazard.
exam_tip: TAWS warns of terrain ahead; don't ignore aural warnings.
key_facts: Terrain database stored on-board
GPS and barometric altitude used
Forward-looking prediction
Aural warnings and alerts
Display in cockpit
Can be terrain-referenced approach
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.46.2

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and operation of a typical EFIS cockpit display system.
concept: Electronic Flight Instrument System replaces mechanical instruments with integrated digital displays. Primary Flight Display (PFD) shows attitude, airspeed, altitude, heading and vertical speed. Navigation Display (ND) shows flight plan, position, navigation sources. EFIS integrates data from multiple sensors and provides redundancy. Displays use LCD or CRT technology. System can be configured for single or dual pilot operation.
exam_tip: EFIS shows PFD and ND; cross-check with standby instruments.
key_facts: Primary Flight Display (PFD)
Navigation Display (ND)
Integrated data sources
Sensor fusion
Built-in redundancy
Configuration flexibility
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.48.2

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of the: Air data computer
concept: The Air Data Computer (ADC) receives pitot and static pressure inputs from the pitot-static probe and processes these to calculate and output airspeed (IAS/CAS/TAS), altitude (QFE/QNH), vertical speed (rate of climb/descent), outside air temperature (OAT), and Mach number. The ADC is the primary source of air data information for the EFIS display system.
exam_tip: ADC converts static and pitot pressure inputs to airspeed, altitude, and vertical speed. Understand the difference between IAS, CAS, and TAS.
key_facts: Receives pitot and static pressure inputs
Calculates airspeed (IAS, CAS, TAS)
Calculates altitude and vertical speed
Outputs outside air temperature (OAT)
Calculates Mach number
Central source of air data for EFIS
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.48.4(a)

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of the: Signal generator
concept: The Signal Generator (SG) receives magnetic heading input from the aircraft's compass system and converts this analog compass heading signal into a digital output signal for display on the EFIS flight display. The SG acts as an interface between the compass and the digital EFIS system.
exam_tip: Signal Generator converts compass heading to EFIS display format. Don't confuse with other heading sources (inertial, GPS).
key_facts: Receives analog magnetic heading input
Converts compass signal to digital format
Outputs heading to EFIS display
Interface between compass and display system
Accuracy depends on compass accuracy
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.48.4(b)

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of the: Input data sources to a typical basic EFIS flight display system
concept: A typical basic EFIS flight display system integrates multiple data sources: pitot-static probe (airspeed and altitude via ADC), magnetic compass (heading via Signal Generator), inertial reference system (attitude and rate information), GPS receiver (position and ground track), radio altimeter (height above ground), engine sensors (power and performance parameters), and sometimes angle-of-attack sensor. This multi-source integration provides redundancy and cross-checking of critical flight data.
exam_tip: Know the main input sources: pitot-static, compass, inertial, GPS, radio altimeter, and engine sensors. Understand redundancy.
key_facts: Pitot-static probe for airspeed and altitude
Magnetic compass for heading input
Inertial reference for attitude and rates
GPS receiver for position and track
Radio altimeter for height above ground
Engine sensors for power parameters
Angle-of-attack sensor (some systems)
Data integration and cross-checking
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.48.4(c)

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and operation of a typical basic AHRS (Attitude Heading Reference System).
concept: AHRS is an inertial reference system that continuously calculates aircraft pitch, roll and heading using accelerometers and gyroscopes. Uses triple-redundant solid-state gyros and accelerometers. Integrates with air data to provide attitude without reliance on magnetic compass. Provides pitch/roll accurate to ±2-5 degrees. Self-aligns on ground during initialization (typically 2-5 minutes). Outputs attitude data to flight displays and autopilot.
exam_tip: AHRS provides independent attitude; compares with magnetic compass for heading.
key_facts: Solid-state gyroscopes and accelerometers
Pitch and roll calculation
Heading reference integration
Ground alignment requirement
Continuous operation
±2-5° accuracy typical
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.48.6

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and operation of an aircraft ELT.
concept: Emergency Locator Transmitter (ELT) transmits distress signals on 121.5 MHz and 406 MHz in event of aircraft accident or manual activation. Automatic ELTs (ELT-AF) activate on impact via G-switch detecting deceleration >5g. Manual ELTs (ELT-M) require crew activation. 406 MHz signals contain aircraft registration and location data enabling satellite detection (COSPAS-SARSAT system). Battery provides minimum 48 hours transmission. Antenna must be protected from damage to function properly.
exam_tip: ELT transmits on 406 MHz for satellite detection; 121.5 MHz for direction finding.
key_facts: Dual frequency (121.5 & 406 MHz)
Automatic G-switch activation
Manual activation capability
48-hour battery life
Registration data in 406 signal
Satellite detection system
source: Stoneybrook AU Module 1.4, NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25 §12.50.2

syllabus_desc: Describe the main means for air cooling an engine, including: (a) cooling fans; (b) baffles; (c) fins.
concept: Your helicopter engine generates serious heat, so you need to manage it. The cooling system basically forces air over hot cylinder fins — think of it like a computer radiator, but for aviation. Baffles direct air flow where it needs to go, while the cooling fan creates the airflow. The fins on the cylinders give you maximum surface area to shed that heat into the air.
exam_tip: Know the three cooling methods and why each matters. Examiners often ask what happens if baffles crack or the cooling fan fails — power loss and overheating, obviously.
key_facts: Cooling fins on cylinders increase surface area for heat transfer
Baffles direct ram air and cooling airflow over cylinders efficiently
Cooling fan assists airflow at low speeds (hover, taxiing); less critical at cruise
Inadequate cooling causes cylinder head temperatures to exceed limits, reducing available power
Most light helicopter engines use air-cooled cylinders (no liquid cooling)
source: Wagtendonk Ch 1, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 4

syllabus_desc: State the importance of having an engine rundown period after flight.
concept: After you land, don't just shut down and walk away. Your engine is hot, and the oil still needs to circulate to cool things down gradually. Give it a good few minutes at idle power — some instructors say 5 minutes is the go, others say 3 at minimum. This cools the cylinders evenly and prevents thermal shock that could crack components.
exam_tip: Memorise: rundown period prevents thermal shock and allows even cooling. Examiners like to ask what happens if you don't do it — warping, cracking, premature wear.
key_facts: Engine rundown period allows gradual cooling after flight operations
Idle operation permits continued oil circulation without thermal stress
Prevents thermal shock from rapid cooling of hot cylinders
Typical rundown is 3–5 minutes at idle after landing
Neglecting rundown period accelerates component wear and can cause internal damage
source: Wagtendonk Ch 1, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 4

syllabus_desc: Describe the construction of typical fixed landing skids on a light helicopter.
concept: Most light helicopters like the Robinson R22 and R44 sit on fixed skids rather than wheels — basically two tubular steel tubes that run forward and aft. They're simple, lightweight, and you can land on just about anything flat. The skids are bolted to the airframe with some cross-bracing for rigidity. Rubber or felt pads on the bottom absorb landing shock and reduce noise.
exam_tip: Remember: skids are tubular construction, fixed (not retrractable), and use shock-absorbing pads. Examiners might ask about load distribution and why skids work better than wheels in rough terrain.
key_facts: Light helicopter skids are typically tubular steel construction, welded or riveted
Two main longitudinal skids run full length of aircraft for lateral stability
Cross-bracing members provide torsional rigidity and load distribution
Shock-absorbing pads (rubber or felt) reduce landing impact and noise
Skids are fixed, non-retractable, allowing unrestricted ground clearance
source: Wagtendonk Ch 20, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 4

syllabus_desc: Explain how induced drag varies depending on: (a) angle of attack of the rotor blade; (b) aspect ratio.
concept: Induced drag is the price you pay for lift — the rotor blade's tip vortices create downwash that opposes your flight. When you increase angle of attack (pitching the blade more), you demand more lift, which means stronger tip vortices and more induced drag. Aspect ratio is the blade's length-to-width ratio: long, thin blades have higher aspect ratio and generate lift more efficiently, so they produce less induced drag at the same lift. Think of it this way: a short, stubby blade has to work harder to stay aloft than a long, elegant one.
exam_tip: High angle of attack = high induced drag. High aspect ratio = low induced drag. These relationships come up in performance questions — know them cold.
key_facts: Induced drag is proportional to the square of lift coefficient (or angle of attack)
Higher blade angle of attack produces stronger tip vortices, increasing induced drag
Aspect ratio = rotor blade length ÷ average chord (width)
High aspect ratio (long, thin blades) produces less induced drag for same lift
Low aspect ratio (short, stubby blades) requires higher angles of attack and higher induced drag
Total drag = profile drag + induced drag; induced drag dominates in hover
source: Wagtendonk Ch 3, Ch 5, Ch 6, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 2

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of the following factors on hovering flight: (a) density altitude; (b) helicopter gross weight; (c) ground effect.
concept: Hovering is the helicopter's party trick, but it's surprisingly demanding. At high density altitude — say, hot day at Queenstown in January — the air is thinner, so your rotor has to work harder to generate the same lift. Load up extra fuel and a door-mounted observer, and you've added weight, which means more blade pitch, more power demand. Here's the clever bit: ground effect. When you're within about one rotor diameter of the ground, the downwash bounces back up and interferes with the incoming flow, reducing the induced power required by roughly 10–15%. Hover "in ground effect" (IGE) at Ardmore, but hover "out of ground effect" (OGE) at 500 feet, and you'll feel the difference on the collective.
exam_tip: Ground effect saves you power near the ground — remember it's within one rotor diameter. High density altitude and heavy weight both demand more power. If you can't hover OGE, you're pushing the limit.
key_facts: Density altitude reduces air density; thin air requires more power to hover
Increased gross weight requires higher collective pitch and more power
Ground effect exists within approximately one rotor diameter of the surface
Ground effect reduces induced flow velocity, decreasing power required by 10–15%
Hover in ground effect (IGE) is easier than out of ground effect (OGE)
Combined high DA + high weight + out-of-ground effect = maximum power demand
source: Wagtendonk Ch 11, Ch 2, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 7, Ch 9

syllabus_desc: Describe: (a) over pitching, and the recovery from it; (b) recirculation.
concept: Over-pitching is what happens when you demand more power from the rotor than the engine can deliver. You keep pulling collective, but instead of climbing, your rotor RPM starts to decay — you'll see it drop on the tach. If it goes below the green arc, you're in trouble because the rotor loses aerodynamic efficiency and authority. Recovery is straightforward: lower the collective immediately to reduce power demand and let RPM recover. Recirculation is trickier — it happens in the hover when downwash hits the ground and bounces back up into the rotor system, creating turbulent air. This reduces lift and feels like the helicopter sinks slightly. You'll often feel it as a soft settling when you hover in ground effect.
exam_tip: Over-pitching = RRPM decay. Fix it: lower collective fast. Recirculation feels like the aircraft wants to settle — it's normal in the hover but you need to recognise it. Both come up in hover emergencies.
key_facts: Over-pitching occurs when collective demand exceeds available engine power
Rotor RPM decay is the primary indication of over-pitching
Recovery from over-pitching: immediately lower collective to reduce pitch demand
Recirculation is bouncing of ground vortex air back into the rotor disc
Recirculation reduces effective rotor disc angle of attack and available lift
Recirculation is pronounced in light rotor-loading hover and causes transient sinking
source: Wagtendonk Ch 11, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 9

syllabus_desc: Identify the forces acting on the helicopter in steady forward flight.
concept: In steady forward flight, you've got four forces to balance, just like a fixed-wing aircraft. Thrust from your rotor points forward (and slightly up because the rotor disc is tilted), lift acts perpendicular to the rotor disc, drag opposes your motion, and weight acts straight down. In steady flight, thrust equals drag, and total rotor lift equals weight — everything's in equilibrium. The rotor disc tilts forward because you've pushed the cyclic, which changes the pitch of individual blades as they rotate.
exam_tip: Four forces: thrust, lift (from rotor), drag, weight. In steady flight, thrust = drag and lift = weight. The rotor disc must be tilted forward for forward motion.
key_facts: Thrust vector generated by rotor tilting forward (cyclic input)
Rotor lift acts perpendicular to rotor disc plane in forward flight
Drag acts opposite to direction of motion (profile and parasitic drag)
Weight acts vertically downward
In steady forward flight, thrust balances drag and rotor lift balances weight
Rotor disc tilt angle increases with forward speed up to best range speed
source: Wagtendonk Ch 12, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 2, Ch 9

syllabus_desc: Explain the following: (a) dissymmetry of lift; (b) flap-back (or blow-back); (c) translational lift.
concept: Dissymmetry of lift is unavoidable when the helicopter moves forward: the advancing blade (moving through the air in the same direction as the fuselage) sees higher relative airspeed, so it generates more lift. The retreating blade (moving backward relative to the fuselage) sees lower relative airspeed and generates less lift. This'd make the helicopter roll like crazy, but articulated and semi-rigid rotors use blade flapping — the advancing blade flaps up, retreating blade flaps down, automatically re-balancing things. Flap-back (or blow-back) is related: as you accelerate, the advancing blade flaps up more, tilting the rotor disc aft. You need forward cyclic to compensate. Finally, translational lift: once you hit roughly 15–24 knots, fresh undisturbed air enters the rotor system instead of recirculated downwash. This suddenly improves rotor efficiency, and you'll feel the helicopter want to climb — it's quite a kick at first.
exam_tip: Dissymmetry = advancing blade sees more airspeed. Flap-back = disc tilts aft as you accelerate. Translational lift = big efficiency gain around 15–24 kt — that's why initial climb is easier once you're moving. These three are absolutely fundamental.
key_facts: Advancing blade sees higher relative airspeed; retreating blade sees lower relative airspeed
This differential airspeed creates dissymmetry of lift (unequal lift across the rotor disc)
Blade flapping (articulated/semi-rigid) automatically compensates for dissymmetry
Flap-back: advancing blade flaps up more as forward speed increases, tilting disc aft
Forward cyclic required to counteract flap-back and maintain level flight
Translational lift occurs around 15–24 kt when rotor enters undisturbed air, reducing induced drag significantly
source: Wagtendonk Ch 12, Ch 3, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 2, Ch 9

syllabus_desc: State the meaning of best: (a) range speed; (b) endurance speed.
concept: Best range speed is the airspeed at which you burn the least fuel per nautical mile flown — it's purely about fuel efficiency over distance. You'll find this on your performance charts, and it's typically something like 60–80 knots depending on your helicopter type and weight. Best endurance speed, on the other hand, is the airspeed that lets you stay in the air longest on a given fuel load — burn the least fuel per hour. That's usually a slower speed, around 30–50 knots, because you're minimising total power required rather than optimising distance per fuel unit.
exam_tip: Best range = furthest distance on fuel (nm/gallon). Best endurance = longest time in air (hours per fuel). Range speed is faster. If you're trying to get somewhere, use range speed; if you're loitering or searching, use endurance speed.
key_facts: Best range speed = airspeed for minimum fuel consumption per nautical mile
Best endurance speed = airspeed for minimum fuel consumption per hour
Best range speed is faster than best endurance speed
Best range typically occurs near best lift-to-drag ratio (L/D) of the helicopter
Best endurance occurs at lower speed where total power required is minimised
Both speeds vary with helicopter weight and altitude (density altitude)
source: Wagtendonk Ch 13, Ch 5, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 7

syllabus_desc: Explain how the flare results in changes to the following: (a) airspeed and groundspeed; (b) rotor rpm.
concept: The flare is your braking manoeuvre before landing — you pitch the nose up (aft cyclic), which tilts the rotor disc aft and creates a rearward thrust component that kills your forward airspeed. Your groundspeed decreases too, especially if you've got a headwind helping you slow down. But here's what catches students: rotor RPM tends to rise slightly during a flare because you're suddenly demanding less forward thrust, so more of the engine's power goes into lifting. You might see the rotor tachometer creep up toward the top of the green arc. If RPM starts creeping too high, you'll ease the collective down slightly to maintain it in the green.
exam_tip: Flare: nose up, rotor disc tilts aft, creates braking thrust. Airspeed and groundspeed both drop. Watch for rotor RPM rise during flare — that's normal, but don't let it go over-speed. Manage it with collective.
key_facts: Flare pitches nose up (aft cyclic), creating rearward rotor thrust component
Rearward thrust opposes forward motion, reducing airspeed
Groundspeed reduces more if flaring into headwind
Rotor RPM may increase during flare if collective remains constant
RPM increase occurs because less engine power is needed for forward thrust
Pilot manages RPM during flare with collective to keep within green arc
source: Wagtendonk Ch 16, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 9

syllabus_desc: Define: (a) rate of climb; (b) angle of climb.
concept: Rate of climb is simply how fast you're gaining altitude — it's measured in feet per minute (fpm). You'll see it on your vertical speed indicator. Angle of climb is different: it's the angle between your flight path and the horizon, measured in degrees. You can climb steeply (high angle) but slowly (low rate of climb), or shallowly (low angle) but quickly (high rate of climb). A helicopter climbing at best rate might be doing 500 fpm at a shallow angle, while a helicopter climbing at best angle might be doing 300 fpm at a much steeper pitch.
exam_tip: Rate of climb = vertical speed (fpm). Angle of climb = degrees above horizon. They're not the same thing — know the difference and you'll ace any question about climb performance.
key_facts: Rate of climb = vertical distance gained per unit time (feet per minute)
Angle of climb = angle between flight path and horizontal datum (degrees)
Rate of climb is shown on vertical speed indicator (VSI/variometer)
Best rate of climb speed (Vy) maximises vertical speed in fpm
Best angle of climb speed (Vx) maximises altitude gain per distance travelled
Vy is faster and shallower than Vx; Vx is slower and steeper
source: Wagtendonk Ch 14, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 7, Ch 9

syllabus_desc: State the effect of wind on angle of climb.
concept: Wind doesn't directly change your angle of climb relative to the air — that's determined by your airspeed, pitch attitude, and available power. But it absolutely changes your angle of climb relative to the ground. A headwind reduces your groundspeed, so even though your airspeed climbs at the same angle, your actual path over the ground is steeper. Climbing into a headwind, you gain altitude faster relative to the landscape below. Conversely, a tailwind reduces your climb angle over the ground. From a practical standpoint: if you need to clear terrain on departure, you'll climb into wind if possible.
exam_tip: Wind changes angle of climb relative to ground, not relative to air. Headwind = steeper climb angle over ground. Tailwind = shallower climb angle over ground. This matters for terrain clearance.
key_facts: Angle of climb relative to the air depends on airspeed and pitch attitude
Headwind increases climb angle relative to the ground (steeper path)
Tailwind decreases climb angle relative to the ground (shallower path)
Headwind component aids terrain clearance on departure climbs
Wind effect on climb angle is purely a ground-reference phenomenon
Airspeed (relative wind) is the determinant; groundspeed changes the ground path
source: Wagtendonk Ch 14, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 9

syllabus_desc: State how changes in airspeed influence angle of descent (constant power/zero wind).
concept: Here's where helicopter descent behaviour gets interesting — it's not like a fixed-wing aircraft. In a helicopter, if you hold power constant and increase forward airspeed, your angle of descent actually decreases (you descend less steeply). That's because you're trading height for speed; the rotor's producing more lift relative to weight as you accelerate through the translational lift range, so you descend more gently. Conversely, slow down while holding power constant, and your descent angle increases (steeper descent). This is counterintuitive compared to fixed-wing thinking, so pay attention during the lesson.
exam_tip: Constant power: more airspeed = shallower descent. Less airspeed = steeper descent. This is opposite to fixed-wing intuition — it catches everyone at first. Remember the physics: translational lift helps as you speed up.
key_facts: In constant-power descent, increased airspeed reduces descent angle (shallower glide)
Reduced airspeed in constant-power descent increases descent angle (steeper glide)
Translational lift improves rotor efficiency as airspeed increases
This behaviour is opposite to fixed-wing aircraft (which descend steeper with more speed)
Descent angle = arctan(rate of descent / forward airspeed)
Effect is most pronounced between hover and translational lift speeds
source: Wagtendonk Ch 14, Ch 12, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 9

syllabus_desc: State the effect of wind on angle of descent.
concept: Wind affects your descent angle relative to the ground the same way it affects climb angle. A headwind steepens your descent path over the ground because you're losing groundspeed while descending, so you come down at a steeper angle relative to what you see below. A tailwind shallows out your descent path. Think about it practically: descending toward an airfield in a headwind, you come down more steeply and have less distance to glide. In a tailwind, you drift further downwind while descending, so your ground track is shallower. Always plan descents into wind if you're working with terrain or trying to land at a particular spot.
exam_tip: Headwind = steeper descent over ground. Tailwind = shallower descent over ground. Same logic as climb angles. This matters for approach planning and obstacle clearance.
key_facts: Headwind component increases descent angle relative to the ground
Tailwind component decreases descent angle relative to the ground
Wind effect is relative to ground track, not airspeed-referenced descent
Headwind aids descent performance on approach (steeper glide path)
Tailwind headwind can extend ground distance during constant-airspeed descent
Descent angle relative to air mass remains unchanged by wind
source: Wagtendonk Ch 14, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 9

syllabus_desc: Identify the forces acting on a helicopter in a level turn.
concept: In a level turn, you've got the same four forces as in straight flight — weight, lift (rotor force), thrust, and drag — but now the rotor lift has two jobs: it needs to support your weight vertically, and it needs to provide the centripetal force that turns the aircraft. So you bank the aircraft, which tilts the rotor disc. The rotor lift now points inward and upward. The vertical component still equals weight, but the horizontal (inward) component provides the turning force. You'll need more power because you're demanding more total lift to maintain altitude while turning.
exam_tip: Level turn: rotor lift must support weight AND provide centripetal force. That's why you need more power. Know that the inward component of lift creates the turn.
key_facts: Rotor lift in turn has vertical component (balances weight) and horizontal component (provides centripetal force)
Horizontal lift component points toward centre of turn, creating centripetal acceleration
Total rotor lift must increase in a turn (at same airspeed and altitude)
Increased lift demand requires increased collective pitch
Power required increases in a turn due to higher total lift demand
Load factor = total lift required / weight (always greater than 1 in a turn)
source: Wagtendonk Ch 15, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 9

syllabus_desc: State the effect of angle of bank on rate and radius of turn, load factor and power required.
concept: Bank angle is your control variable, and it affects everything. Increase bank angle, and your rate of turn increases — you'll change heading faster. But radius of turn decreases because you're turning tighter. Load factor (the G-force you feel) goes up dramatically with bank angle — a 30-degree bank gives you a load factor of about 1.15 G, but a 60-degree bank pushes it toward 2 G. Power required also climbs steeply because you need more total lift to sustain the turn at higher bank angles. Shallow banks are efficient turns; steep banks are tight but power-hungry.
exam_tip: Bank angle up = rate of turn up, radius down, load factor up, power up. That's the memory rule. Know that load factor increases with the cube of bank angle; steep banks get expensive fast.
key_facts: Increased bank angle increases rate of turn (degrees per second)
Increased bank angle decreases radius of turn (tighter turn)
Load factor increases with bank angle (load factor = 1/cos(bank angle))
At 30° bank: load factor ≈ 1.15 G; at 60° bank: load factor ≈ 2.0 G
Power required increases substantially with bank angle due to higher lift demand
Steeper banks reduce range and endurance due to increased power requirement
source: Wagtendonk Ch 15, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 9

syllabus_desc: Describe a rate 1 turn, and a rule-of-thumb method of calculating the bank angle required.
concept: A rate 1 turn is a standard-rate turn: you change heading at 3 degrees per second, so you'd make a complete 360-degree turn in 2 minutes. Instrument pilots fly rate 1 turns all the time. Here's a handy rule of thumb: to calculate the bank angle needed for a rate 1 turn at a given airspeed, take your true airspeed, divide by 10, and add 7. So if you're doing 60 knots, that's 6 plus 7 = 13 degrees of bank. At 90 knots, 9 plus 7 = 16 degrees. It's not dead accurate, but it gets you in the ballpark and is much easier than pulling out tables.
exam_tip: Rate 1 turn = 3°/sec = 360° in 2 minutes. Formula: bank angle ≈ (TAS ÷ 10) + 7 degrees. The examiners love this rule of thumb — it shows you understand the relationship.
key_facts: Rate 1 turn = 3 degrees per second rate of change of heading
Rate 1 turn completes 360 degrees in exactly 2 minutes
Rule of thumb: bank angle (degrees) ≈ (TAS in knots ÷ 10) + 7
Formula works reasonably well for typical helicopter speeds (30–80 knots)
Rate 1 turn approximation becomes less accurate at very high or very low speeds
Rate 1 turns are standard in instrument procedures and crosschecks
source: Wagtendonk Ch 15, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 9

syllabus_desc: For climbing and descending turns, describe the: (a) effect of bank on the rate of climb/descent; (b) requirement for increased power.
concept: When you add a turn to a climb or descent, you're compounding the demands on your helicopter. A climbing turn requires bank, which increases load factor and demands more lift. That extra lift demand reduces the vertical lift component available for climbing, so your rate of climb decreases compared to a wings-level climb at the same airspeed. It's a trade-off: the steeper you bank, the slower you climb. Descending turns behave similarly — bank increases load factor, but now you're fighting gravity, so the effect is a bit different. Power requirement goes up significantly in both cases because you're sustaining both the descent/climb and the turn load simultaneously. Practice these manoeuvres and you'll develop an intuitive feel for the power settings required.
exam_tip: Climbing turn: bank reduces rate of climb. Descending turn: bank increases power required. Remember, you're fighting load factor in both cases. Examiners often ask about power management during turning climbs.
key_facts: Turning climb reduces rate of climb compared to wings-level climb (same speed)
Bank angle increases load factor, reducing vertical lift component
Steeper banks in climbing turns result in shallower climb angles
Power requirement increases in turning climb to sustain both climb and turn loads
Descending turns also require more power than wings-level descent
Coordination (coordinated aileron and pedal inputs) is essential to maintain control
source: Wagtendonk Ch 14, Ch 15, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 9

syllabus_desc: Define autorotation.
concept: Autorotation is when the rotor keeps spinning after engine power is lost — the helicopter essentially glides downward, and the rotor disc rotates from the airflow coming up through it. It's your emergency procedure for engine failure. The instant you lose engine power, you lower the collective all the way down to zero pitch. This unloads the rotor and lets it speed up. The helicopter descends at a controlled rate, and the rotor keeps turning because of the upward airflow. Near the ground, you pull the collective back up to convert rotor kinetic energy into lift for the landing flare. It's a remarkable bit of physics, and it's why helicopters are so safe even with one engine.
exam_tip: Autorotation: engine failure = collective down immediately, rotor keeps turning from updraft. The key is understanding that you're converting gravitational potential energy into rotor kinetic energy. Know the steps cold.
key_facts: Autorotation occurs when rotor is driven by upward airflow after engine power loss
Collective pitch lowered to zero immediately on engine failure to unload rotor
Helicopter descends in controlled manner while rotor maintains RPM
Rotor kinetic energy is converted to lift in final flare before landing
Autorotation is the primary emergency procedure for engine failure
Successful autorotation depends on prompt collective action and proper cyclic control
source: Wagtendonk Ch 18, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 11

syllabus_desc: Identify the stalled, driving and driven regions (sections) of the rotor disc in autorotation.
concept: During autorotation, the rotor disc is divided into three distinct regions based on blade airflow characteristics. The inner section (roughly inner third) is stalled — the blade angle of attack is too high for the prevailing airflow, so it's not generating lift efficiently. It's actually consuming power. The middle section is the driving region — here, the blade has a good angle of attack and generates useful lift, creating a net power surplus. The outer section is driven — the blade angle here is shallow, not generating much lift or drag, but it's being dragged along by the rotor's rotational motion. Think of it this way: the middle section pulls the inner and outer sections along. The balance between these three regions determines whether your rotor RPM stays healthy during descent.
exam_tip: Inner = stalled (power sink). Middle = driving (power source). Outer = driven (along for the ride). This three-region split is fundamental to understanding autorotation stability.
key_facts: Stalled region: inner blade section with high angle of attack; produces little or no lift
Driving region: middle blade section with optimal angle of attack; produces excess lift (power)
Driven region: outer blade section with shallow angle; being dragged by rotor inertia
Power balance: driving region must produce enough lift to support stalled and driven regions
Rotor RPM stability depends on maintaining power balance between regions
Any disturbance (speed change, altitude) can shift region boundaries and affect RPM
source: Wagtendonk Ch 18, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 11

syllabus_desc: Explain the need to lower the collective at the start of an autorotation.
concept: This is absolutely critical: the moment you lose engine power, you must lower the collective to zero pitch immediately. Why? Because you're about to lose the engine's torque reaction holding the rotor down. As the engine quits, the rotor would normally decelerate rapidly. By lowering collective pitch, you reduce the aerodynamic load on the rotor, allowing it to speed up and maintain RPM. The zero-pitch condition also minimises vibration and aerodynamic shock. If you don't lower collective quickly enough, rotor RPM decays, you lose aerodynamic authority, and the landing becomes much harder to control. In the cockpit, your first instinct on engine loss must be feet-and-hand-together — left pedal (anti-torque for the lost engine torque) and collective down.
exam_tip: Collective down on engine failure = unload rotor = maintain RPM. This is not optional. Delay and you'll lose RPM. The sequence is: engine failure → collective down → cyclic for attitude → manage descent → flare at the bottom.
key_facts: Zero collective pitch unloads rotor aerodynamically, allowing RPM to increase
High collective pitch would create excessive drag and decelerate the rotor
Rotor RPM must be maintained above minimum (typically 80% nominal) for flare control
Collective lowering reduces blade pitch angle, reducing angle of attack and load
Prompt collective action is essential; delay results in RPM decay and loss of control
RPM recovery after collective dump takes several seconds; monitor tachometer carefully
source: Wagtendonk Ch 18, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 11

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of the following factors on the range and endurance in autorotation: (a) airspeed; (b) altitude; (c) helicopter gross weight; (d) wind velocity.
concept: Autorotation range and endurance depend on several factors. Airspeed matters tremendously — there's an ideal speed (typically 50–70 knots depending on the helicopter type) that maximises glide distance and time available. Too slow, and you're inefficient; too fast, and you're descending steeply. Altitude is your energy reservoir — more altitude gives you more time and distance. Gross weight affects both range and endurance in a counterintuitive way: a heavier helicopter actually descends faster, but sometimes glides further because of better penetration. Wind is straightforward: a headwind shortens your range but increases endurance time (you stay airborne longer); a tailwind extends range but reduces time aloft. Practice autorotations at different weights and speeds, and you'll develop the instinct for managing them.
exam_tip: Best auto speed ≈ 50–70 kt (type-dependent). Headwind = short range, long time. Tailwind = long range, short time. Altitude is your friend — use it wisely.
key_facts: Best autorotation speed typically ranges from 50–70 knots (type-dependent)
Optimal speed balances descent rate and forward penetration for maximum range
Higher altitude provides more time and distance in autorotation
Gross weight affects descent rate; heavier aircraft descend faster but penetrate better
Headwind increases glide endurance time but decreases ground range
Tailwind decreases glide endurance time but increases ground range
source: Wagtendonk Ch 18, Ch 13, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 11, Ch 7

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle features of a Height-Velocity diagram (avoid curve).
concept: The height-velocity diagram is your autorotation safety chart — it shows which combinations of altitude and airspeed allow a safe autorotative landing. It's often called the avoid curve or dead man's curve because the shaded area is where you should never be: combinations of low altitude and low airspeed where a successful autorotation isn't possible. The boundaries of this curve are determined by the helicopter type, weight, and conditions. Generally, if you're very low and very slow (hover height and hover speed), you've got no rotor RPM margin and insufficient time to develop a flare. If you're at very low altitude and high speed, you don't have altitude to bleed off that speed safely. The safe area is where you have enough altitude and the right airspeed to execute a controlled descent and flare. Every helicopter type has its own H-V diagram — know yours cold because it could save your life.
exam_tip: Height-velocity diagram shows avoid curve (shaded area). Outside the curve = safe autorotation possible. Inside the curve = engine failure here could be fatal. You must know your aircraft's diagram and respect it.
key_facts: Height-velocity diagram shows combinations of altitude and airspeed for safe autorotation
Shaded avoid curve represents conditions where safe autorotation may not be possible
Low altitude + low airspeed = no margin for RPM build and flare
Low altitude + high airspeed = insufficient altitude to slow down safely
Safe region is where adequate altitude and appropriate airspeed allow controlled landing
Diagram varies by helicopter type, gross weight, and density altitude
source: Wagtendonk Ch 18, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 11

syllabus_desc: State the purpose of a helicopter transmission system.
concept: The transmission system connects the engine to the rotor so it can do useful work. Engines turn at high RPM — say, 2,700 RPM for a Lycoming — but the rotor needs to turn much slower, typically 300–600 RPM depending on the aircraft. The transmission gearbox steps down that engine RPM to rotor RPM through a series of gear reductions. It also splits power: some goes to the main rotor, some to the tail rotor. Plus, it includes safety devices like a freewheeling unit (lets the rotor keep turning if the engine fails) and a clutch (connects/disconnects the engine from the gearbox). Without a transmission, you'd either have an impossibly slow engine or an impossibly fast rotor, and you'd have no way to handle engine failure. It's one of the most critical systems on the aircraft.
exam_tip: Transmission = gearbox that reduces engine RPM to rotor RPM, splits power between main and tail rotors, includes clutch and freewheeling unit. Know the basic layout and purpose of each component.
key_facts: Transmission reduces engine RPM to appropriate rotor RPM (typically 4–10:1 reduction)
Transmission distributes engine power to main rotor and tail rotor
Main gearbox is the central component, usually single-stage or multi-stage gear reduction
Transmission includes bearings, seals, and lubrication system for cooling and wear prevention
Freewheeling unit and rotor brake protect against over-speed if engine quits
Transmission health is monitored by chip detectors (metal particle indication)
source: Wagtendonk Ch 20, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 4

syllabus_desc: Describe the function(s) and operation of the following transmission system components: (a) main rotor gearbox; (b) clutch (belt drive and centrifugal); (c) freewheeling unit; (d) rotor brake; (e) tail rotor drive and gearbox; (f) chip detectors.
concept: Let's walk through each one. The main rotor gearbox is the heart — it steps down engine RPM and delivers power to the rotor swash plate. The clutch connects and disconnects the engine from the gearbox; belt-drive clutches use friction between belt and pulleys (like a car's serpentine belt), while centrifugal clutches engage automatically once engine RPM reaches a threshold. The freewheeling unit is a one-way bearing that locks the engine to the rotor during normal operation but instantly disengages if the engine slows below rotor speed (like on engine failure), allowing the rotor to keep spinning. The rotor brake slows the rotor disc on the ground after shutdown so it stops faster — critical for safety. The tail rotor drive is a shaft (sometimes called a drive shaft or Jesus bolt) that transmits power from the main gearbox to the tail rotor gearbox, which steps it down to the tail rotor speed. Finally, chip detectors are tiny magnets in the gearbox housing that catch metal particles; if they collect ferrous material, it signals internal wear or damage. You check them during preflight.
exam_tip: Gearbox = main reduction. Clutch = on/off, two types. Freewheeling = allows rotor to spin freely if engine quits. Rotor brake = stop the disc. Tail rotor drive = shaft + second gearbox. Chip detectors = wear warning. Know the functional purpose of each.
key_facts: Main rotor gearbox reduces engine RPM by 4–10:1 and delivers power to swash plate
Belt-drive clutch: friction couple between belt and pulleys; centrifugal engages at RPM threshold
Freewheeling unit (one-way bearing) locks during normal operation, disengages on engine loss
Rotor brake: wet multi-plate brake on gearbox output, slows rotor after shutdown
Tail rotor drive shaft transmits power from main gearbox to tail rotor gearbox (further reduction)
Chip detectors: magnets in gearbox and transmission housing detect metallic particles indicating wear
source: Wagtendonk Ch 20, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 4

syllabus_desc: Describe the purpose and basic principle of operation of the: (a) swash plate; (b) pitch link advance angle.
concept: The swash plate is the clever mechanism that lets you change rotor blade pitch while the rotor is spinning. It's a tilting disc that sits below the rotor, and it rotates with the rotor but also tilts. As the blade comes around on the rotating swash plate, the height of the disc under that blade determines the pitch angle: push the cyclic forward, tilt the swash plate forward, and blades get more pitch on the back of the disc, less on the front, creating forward tilt. The pitch links connect the swash plate to the blade pitch horns, translating swash plate motion into blade pitch changes. The pitch link advance angle is a small geometric offset — the pitch link is positioned slightly ahead (in the direction of rotation) of where the swash plate tilt is maximum. This advance compensates for the lag in blade response; because the blade lags slightly behind the swash plate motion due to inertia, the advance angle positions the pitch link to apply force at the right moment. Without it, your cyclic inputs would be sluggish and un-responsive.
exam_tip: Swash plate = tilting disc under rotor that changes blade pitch via pitch links. Advance angle = offset to compensate for blade lag, making cyclic inputs responsive. These are mechanical masterstrokes.
key_facts: Swash plate: tilting, rotating disc that converts cyclic/collective inputs to blade pitch changes
Swash plate tilts to change pitch angle of each blade as it rotates around the disc
Pitch links mechanically connect swash plate to blade pitch horns
Pitch link advance angle: geometric offset of pitch link ahead of swash plate tilt peak
Advance angle compensates for blade response lag due to inertia and aerodynamic forces
Correct advance angle is critical for responsive, stable cyclic control
source: Wagtendonk Ch 20, Ch 6, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 3, Ch 4

syllabus_desc: Describe the construction of current rotor blades.
concept: Modern helicopter rotor blades are engineering marvels. They're typically built with a spar (main structural beam, usually of aluminium or composite material) running spanwise for strength, and a more compliant shell around it (made of fibreglass, carbon fibre, or Kevlar laminates) that forms the aerodynamic shape. The blade cross-section is an aerofoil — shaped to generate lift. Inside, you'll find acoustic material and sometimes fluid-filled chambers for vibration damping. The root end (where the blade connects to the hub) is heavy and reinforced to handle the enormous loads and vibration. The tip is tapered for aerodynamic efficiency. Most modern blades are all-composite (fibre-reinforced plastics), which offers great strength-to-weight ratio and fatigue resistance compared to older metal designs. Some older aircraft still have metal blades, but composites are the standard now.
exam_tip: Modern rotor blades: composite construction (fibreglass, carbon fibre, Kevlar), spar for strength, aerofoil cross-section, tapered tip, reinforced root. Know the materials and structural principles.
key_facts: Rotor blade spar (main beam): aluminium or composite, carries primary bending loads
Blade shell: fibreglass, carbon fibre, or Kevlar laminates forming aerodynamic shape
Blade aerofoil cross-section generates lift; section may vary spanwise for efficiency
Composite blades offer superior fatigue resistance and strength-to-weight ratio vs. metal
Root end is reinforced to withstand centrifugal and vibration loads
Tip taper reduces weight and aerodynamic loading in the outer blade region
source: Wagtendonk Ch 7, Ch 20, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 4

syllabus_desc: Outline the basic features of the following systems: (a) rigid rotor; (b) semi-rigid rotor; (c) fully articulated rotor.
concept: There are three main rotor configurations. A rigid rotor (uncommon in single-engine helicopters) has no hinges or flex points at the root — the blade structure is fixed to the hub and flexes only elastically. It's very efficient but requires complex blade tracking. A semi-rigid rotor (used on Robinson R22 and R44) has a teetering hub with two blades on opposite sides; the hub rocks (teeter) about a transverse axis, allowing each blade to flap independently. It's simple, robust, and fairly cheap to manufacture. A fully articulated rotor (used on larger helicopters like the Bell 407) has individual hinges for each blade allowing flap, lead-lag, and feather motions — much more complex but offers independent blade motion and better handling characteristics. Each system has trade-offs between complexity, cost, efficiency, and handling.
exam_tip: Rigid = fixed, no hinges, very efficient. Semi-rigid = teetering, two blades, simple. Fully articulated = flap/lead-lag/feather hinges, complex, independent motion. Know which type is on your helicopter.
key_facts: Rigid rotor: no hinges at blade root; blades flex elastically, hub is rigid
Rigid rotor: most efficient; requires precise blade tracking and ground resonance management
Semi-rigid rotor: teetering hub; two blades rock together about transverse hinge axis
Semi-rigid rotor: simple, robust, lower cost; used on light helicopters (R22, R44)
Fully articulated rotor: individual blade hinges allowing flap, lead-lag, and feather motions
Fully articulated rotor: complex, independent blade motion; used on larger/military helicopters
source: Wagtendonk Ch 20, Ch 7, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 4

syllabus_desc: Describe the basic features of the tail rotor.
concept: The tail rotor is a small rotor mounted vertically at the helicopter's tail boom. It does one essential job: generates thrust perpendicular to the tail boom axis to counteract the main rotor's torque effect. As the main rotor turns, it tries to spin the fuselage in the opposite direction (Newton's third law). The tail rotor, spinning in a different plane, produces a sideways thrust (usually to the right in most designs) that exactly cancels this torque. The tail rotor is tiny compared to the main rotor — only 10–15% the diameter — but it's critical for directional control. The pedals control tail rotor pitch: push right pedal, increase tail rotor pitch, get more anti-torque thrust, helicopter turns right. The tail rotor also helps in forward flight because at cruise, a vertical fin on the tail boom provides some anti-torque force, reducing pedal workload.
exam_tip: Tail rotor = anti-torque rotor. Pedals control its pitch. More pedal = more pitch = more anti-torque thrust = helicopter turns. In a hover, significant pedal input is needed; in cruise, less pedal because the vertical fin helps.
key_facts: Tail rotor generates thrust perpendicular to tail boom, counteracting main rotor torque
Tail rotor diameter typically 10–15% of main rotor diameter
Tail rotor pitch controlled by pedal inputs; increases/decreases anti-torque thrust
Right pedal forward typically increases tail rotor pitch (more anti-torque, turn right)
Vertical fin on tail boom provides passive anti-torque in forward flight (reduces pedal workload)
Tail rotor failure (loss of anti-torque) is an emergency requiring immediate control input
source: Wagtendonk Ch 9, Ch 20, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 3, Ch 4

syllabus_desc: With respect to helicopter controls, explain the purpose and the basic principle of operation of the following: (a) collective control; (b) cyclic control; (c) twist grip throttle including its effect on manifold pressure and rpm; (d) tail rotor pedals; (e) rotor brake.
concept: Your helicopter controls are quite different from a fixed-wing aircraft. The collective (left hand) raises or lowers the pitch of all rotor blades equally, increasing or decreasing total lift — pull up for more power, push down for less. The cyclic (right hand) tilts the rotor disc by varying blade pitch around the rotation cycle, steering the helicopter fore-aft and side-to-side. The twist grip throttle (right hand, twist inward/outward on the cyclic grip) works like an airplane throttle: it controls engine power and manifold pressure, which in turn affects rotor RPM. The pedals control tail rotor pitch — left pedal forward for anti-torque, right pedal forward reduces it, turning the aircraft. The rotor brake is usually a pedal or switch that applies friction to the rotor hub after shutdown, slowing the disc. All five controls work in concert, and coordinating them smoothly is what good helicopter flying is all about.
exam_tip: Collective = pitch of all blades (vertical control). Cyclic = tilt rotor disc (roll/pitch). Twist grip = throttle/power. Pedals = tail rotor pitch (yaw). Rotor brake = stop the disc. Know all five controls and their functions.
key_facts: Collective pitch control: increases/decreases all blade pitches equally for vertical thrust
Cyclic pitch control: varies blade pitch cyclically, tilting rotor disc for lateral/longitudinal control
Twist grip throttle: controls engine power, manifold pressure, and rotor RPM
Tail rotor pedals: control tail rotor pitch, generating anti-torque thrust for yaw control
Rotor brake: friction brake applied post-shutdown to stop rotor disc (safety feature)
Coordinated control of all five inputs is essential for smooth, efficient helicopter operation
source: Wagtendonk Ch 10, Ch 20, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 3, Ch 9

syllabus_desc: Explain the typical markings and correct interpretation of a dual needle tachometer.
concept: Most helicopters have a dual-needle tachometer — it's basically two needles on one dial. One needle shows engine RPM, the other shows rotor RPM. In normal flight, these two needles should be "married" (sitting right on top of each other in the green arc). If they split apart, something's wrong: the engine might be over-speeding relative to the rotor, or the rotor might be slowing down relative to the engine. A wide split indicates clutch slip — the belt-drive or centrifugal clutch isn't transferring power properly. The green arc (typically 300–400 rotor RPM for light helicopters) is where you want to be. The yellow arc is caution range — you can operate there briefly but not for sustained flight. The red radial line is never-exceed RPM. You'll monitor this dual tach throughout the flight, and any unexpected needle split gets your attention immediately.
exam_tip: Dual tach: two needles, should be married (together) in green arc. Split = clutch slip or other issue. Green = normal, yellow = caution, red = never exceed. Learn to read it at a glance.
key_facts: Dual needle tachometer displays engine RPM and rotor RPM on same dial
Needles should be married (overlapping) in normal flight within green arc
Green arc: normal operating RPM range (typically 300–400 rotor RPM for light helicopters)
Yellow arc: caution range; brief operation only, indicates high or low RPM stress
Red radial line: never-exceed RPM; operation beyond this causes over-stress and damage
Needle split indicates engine-to-rotor speed mismatch; likely clutch slip or transmission issue
source: Wagtendonk Ch 10, Ch 20, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 8, Ch 9

syllabus_desc: Explain the need for reporting any damage to rotor components that has not been marked as being previously assessed.
concept: Rotor components are highly stressed, and even small damage can propagate into a serious structural failure in flight. If you spot a nick, crack, or dent on a rotor blade during preflight or post-flight inspection, you must report it to maintenance immediately. Maintenance will assess the damage, determine if it's within limits, and either approve the aircraft for flight or ground it for repair. If damage has already been marked (usually with a white or red paint mark around it), that indicates maintenance has already evaluated it and deemed it acceptable. But if you spot unmarked damage, you can't assume it's safe — you report it and let the experts decide. Flying with unassessed rotor damage is a serious safety risk because that damage might grow with every cycle, eventually leading to blade failure. CAANZ and NZ CARs require this reporting, and your aviation medical certificate depends on your judgment and safety culture.
exam_tip: Any rotor damage not previously marked = report immediately. Don't assume it's OK just because it looks small. Maintenance assesses it; you report it. This is your responsibility as pilot-in-command.
key_facts: Rotor blades are highly stressed; even small damage can propagate in flight
Unmarked damage must be reported to maintenance for assessment and clearance
Previously marked damage indicates maintenance has already evaluated and approved it
Failure to report unassessed damage violates NZ CARs and safety protocols
Rotor damage can grow with each flight cycle, potentially causing catastrophic failure
Pilot-in-command responsibility includes pre- and post-flight inspection and damage reporting
source: AC 61-3 Rev 27, FAA Helicopter Flying Handbook Ch 4, Ch 9
